console.log(missing);  // RHS, never declared → ReferenceError

Strict mode closes the footgun — there a failed LHS throws ReferenceError, just like a failed RHS:

"use strict";
function f() {
  leak = 10;        // ReferenceError: leak is not defined
}
f();

ES modules and class bodies are strict by default, so modern code gets this protection for free.

One nuance: a plain RHS read of an undeclared name throws, except typeof undeclaredVar, which returns "undefined" instead of throwing — the safe feature-detect. The exception does not apply to let/const still in their TDZ — there even typeof throws (covered in the Hoisting & TDZ subtopic).

cards.question: What is hoisting, mechanically?
cards.answer:
Hoisting is not the engine moving lines to the top. It is the visible consequence of the compile pass: every declaration is registered in its scope before any code runs, so the names already exist when execution starts.

For var, the variable is created and initialized to undefined at the start of its scope. The assignment stays where you wrote it and runs only when execution reaches that line. So declarations hoist, assignments do not — reading a var before its assignment gives undefined, not an error:

console.log(score); // undefined — declared, not yet assigned
var score = 42;
console.log(score); // 42

Function declarations hoist complete — the name and the full function body are registered together, so the function is callable before its line:

greet(); // "hi" — the whole function is ready before this line

function greet() {
  console.log("hi");
}

The asymmetry makes sense once you see the two passes: a declaration is static information the parser can register up front, while an assignment is runtime work that has to wait for the execute pass. "Hoisting" is just the name for how that split looks from the outside.

cards.question: What is the temporal dead zone?
cards.answer:
The temporal dead zone (TDZ) is the stretch from the start of a scope to the let/const declaration line, during which the variable exists but is uninitialized — any access throws ReferenceError.

let and const are also registered at compile time — hoisted, in that sense — just like var. The difference is what happens next: var is initialized to undefined immediately, while let/const are left in a special "uninitialized" state. The engine knows the variable, but refuses to touch it until the declaration line runs:

console.log(user); // ReferenceError: Cannot access 'user' before initialization
let user = "Ann";

Note the error message: not "user is not defined" but "cannot access before initialization". The variable is there — it is in its dead zone.

Because the variable is registered for its whole scope, the TDZ also blocks reaching an outer variable of the same name:

let x = 1;

function func() {
  console.log(x); // ReferenceError — the inner x exists here, but is in its TDZ
  let x = 2;
}

func();

The zone is temporal — about execution time, not position in the text. Code written above the declaration may mention the variable, as long as it runs after the declaration has executed:

function read() { return token; } // mentions token before its line — fine
let token = "abc";
console.log(read()); // "abc" — by call time the declaration has run

Even typeof throws in the TDZ — unlike typeof of a truly undeclared name, which returns "undefined":

console.log(typeof neverDeclared); // "undefined" — safe
console.log(typeof token);         // ReferenceError — token is in its TDZ
let token = "abc";

Why the language added it: with var, use-before-declaration silently produced undefined and the bug surfaced somewhere far away. The TDZ turns it into a loud error at the exact line. It also makes const coherent — a const read before its declaration line can never have a value: initializing it to undefined and assigning the real value later would mean a constant with two observable values.

Telling the TDZ ReferenceError apart from undefined and undeclared names is covered in the Types topic; this card owns the mechanics.

cards.question: What hoists to where?
cards.answer:
Each declaration form hoists to a different scope and arrives in a different state: var to the nearest function, let/const to their block, function declarations whole, function expressions not at all — only their variable hoists.

DeclarationHoists toState at scope startEarly access gives
var xnearest function (or global) scope — blocks ignoredundefinedundefined
let x / const xits blockuninitialized (TDZ)ReferenceError
function f() {}its scope, wholethe function itselfworks — callable
var f = function () {}only the variable fundefinedundefined — calling it: TypeError

var ignores blocks entirely — an if or for body does not contain it; the nearest function does. let/const stay inside the block, in their TDZ until the declaration line:

function run() {
  if (true) {
    var v = 1;
    let l = 2;
  }
  console.log(v); // 1 — var ignored the block, lives in the function
  console.log(l); // ReferenceError: l is not defined
}
run();

For a function expression, only the variable hoists — the function value is created when the assignment runs. So calling it early is a TypeError: the name exists and holds undefined, and undefined is not callable.

sayHi(); // TypeError: sayHi is not a function — sayHi is hoisted as undefined
var sayHi = function () {
  console.log("hi");
};

Compare with a name that was never declared at all — that lookup fails entirely, so it is a ReferenceError:

nowhere(); // ReferenceError: nowhere is not defined — the name does not exist at all

The error type tells you which case you are in: TypeError means the name hoisted but holds no function yet; a ReferenceError saying "is not defined" means the name does not exist at all. Declarations vs expressions in depth: see the Function Expressions & Scope topic. The rest of var's quirks: see the Global Scope & var topic.

cards.question: What is shadowing?
cards.answer:
When an inner scope declares a variable with the same name as an outer one, the inner declaration shadows the outer: inside that scope, the name refers to the inner variable only.

It falls straight out of how lookup works: the search walks outward and stops at the first match. The outer variable still exists and keeps its value — it just can't be reached from inside the shadow, because lookup never walks past the inner match.

let answer = "outer";

function show() {
  let answer = "inner";   // shadows the outer `answer`
  console.log(answer);    // "inner" — lookup stops at the first match
}

show();                   // "inner"
console.log(answer);      // "outer" — still exists, untouched

Parameters are declarations too, so they shadow the same way:

let name = "global";

function greet(name) {    // the parameter shadows the outer `name`
  console.log(name);
}

greet("param");           // "param"

Shadowing is legal across any scope boundary — even let shadowing an outer var. The boundary is what makes it legal: redeclaring with let in the same scope is a SyntaxError, but a different scope means a different variable.

var count = 1;

{
  let count = 2;          // legal: different scope
  console.log(count);     // 2
}

console.log(count);       // 1

cards.question: Can you ever reach a shadowed variable?
cards.answer:
From inside the shadow — normally no. There is no syntax for "the x one scope up"; lookup always stops at the nearest match.

One escape hatch exists, in browsers only: a shadowed global var is still reachable as window.x. A var in a browser script also becomes a property of window, and property access doesn't use the scope chain — so the shadow doesn't apply to it.

// browser
var x = "global";          // also becomes window.x

function f() {
  let x = "local";         // shadows the global x
  console.log(x);          // "local"
  console.log(window.x);   // "global" — the property route, not a scope lookup
}
f();

let/const globals never become window properties, so once shadowed they are truly unreachable. Why var globals land on window at all is covered in the Global Scope & var topic.

In practice, reaching for this trick is a sign to rename: shadowing makes the same name mean different things a few lines apart. It's enough of a readability hazard that many teams ban it with the no-shadow ESLint rule.

cards.question: What is an execution context?
cards.answer:
An execution context is the environment a piece of code runs in. The engine creates one before executing the code, and it holds everything that code needs to run — its variables and the value of this.

There are three kinds: one global context for the top-level code, a new context for every function call, and a context for eval. Per call, not per function — calling the same function three times creates three separate contexts, each with its own fresh local variables:

function count() {
  var n = 0;        // a fresh `n` in this call's context
  n++;
  return n;
}
count(); // 1
count(); // 1 — new call, new context, new `n`

Each context is built in two phases:

PhaseWhat happens
CreationThe environment record is set up: every declaration in the scope is registered, and this is determined.
ExecutionThe code runs line by line; assignments happen here.

The creation phase is the per-call counterpart of the compile pass from "Compile, then execute": names exist before the first line runs, which is why hoisting works.

console.log(x);   // undefined — `x` was registered in the creation phase
var x = 5;        // the assignment waits for the execution phase

Contexts stack. The call stack is the stack of execution contexts: the global context sits at the bottom, every call pushes a new context on top, every return pops one off. The context on top is the one currently running.

cards.question: How do execution contexts relate to scope?
cards.answer:
Each execution context holds an environment record — the internal object where that scope's variables actually live. The scope chain is a chain of these environment records.

The chain's shape is fixed lexically. Every function carries a hidden internal reference ([[Environment]]) to the environment of the place where it was written, set once when the function is created. So no matter which context calls the function, its lookup chain is the one from the source text: own record → outer → … → global, following that reference — never the call stack.

function foo() {
  console.log(a);
}

function bar() {
  var a = 2;
  foo();            // bar's context is on the stack — but not in foo's chain
}

var a = 1;
bar();              // 1

While foo runs, bar's context sits right below it on the stack. But foo was written at the top level, so its chain goes from its own record straight to the global record — bar's record is on the stack, not in the chain.

The two have different lifetimes: contexts and their records are created and destroyed with calls; the lexical structure they plug into is fixed when the code is parsed and never changes.

this also lives in the context, but it is not part of the scope chain and is resolved by call-site rules — see the this & Binding topic. What happens when a function's record must outlive the call that created it is covered in the Closure topic.

cards.question: What is the difference between a function declaration and a function expression?
cards.answer:
A function declaration is a standalone statement that starts with the function keyword and must have a name. A function expression is a function created inside an expression — assigned to a variable, passed as an argument, returned from another function — and its name is optional.

// Function Declaration — a statement of its own
function sum(a, b) {
  return a + b;
}

// Function Expression — the function is a value, assigned to a variable
let mul = function(a, b) {
  return a * b;
};

However it's created, a function is a value. The name without parentheses is a reference to the function — only parentheses call it. That means a function can be copied into another variable, and printing one shows its source code:

function sayHi() {
  console.log("Hello");
}

let func = sayHi;       // no parentheses: copies the function itself
func();                 // Hello — same function, second name

let result = sayHi();   // parentheses: runs sayHi, stores its result — undefined here

console.log(String(sayHi)); // "function sayHi() {...}" — the source code

The parser decides by position. If function appears at the start of a statement, it's a declaration. Anywhere a value is expected — after =, inside parentheses, in an argument list — it's an expression. That's why an IIFE needs wrapping parentheses: (function() { ... })() puts the function in value position, so it can be anonymous and called immediately.

The optional name on an expression is still useful. A named function expression gives the function an inner name visible only inside its own body — a reliable way to call itself:

let sayHi = function func(who) {
  if (!who) return func("Guest");   // self-call survives reassignment of sayHi
  console.log(`Hello, ${who}`);
};

console.log(typeof func);   // "undefined" — the inner name is invisible outside

Even a fully anonymous expression gets a name property inferred from its assignment: let greet = function() {} gives greet.name === "greet".

cards.question: How do they behave differently?
cards.answer:
The key difference is creation time: a declaration is created during the engine's compile pass, so it's usable anywhere in its scope — even above its line in the source. An expression is created only when execution reaches it.

sayHi("John");   // "Hello, John" — already created

function sayHi(name) {
  console.log(`Hello, ${name}`);
}

Call an expression before its line and the function doesn't exist yet — the variable does, but it holds undefined:

sayBye("John");   // TypeError: sayBye is not a function

var sayBye = function(name) {
  console.log(`Bye, ${name}`);
};

With let/const the early call throws ReferenceError instead — the variable isn't initialized yet. The mechanics behind both errors are covered in the Scope topic (Hoisting & TDZ).

Second difference: block visibility. In strict mode, a declaration inside a code block is visible only inside that block:

"use strict";

if (true) {
  function hi() { console.log("hi"); }
  hi();   // works — visible inside the block
}

hi();   // ReferenceError: hi is not defined

Outside strict mode the declaration leaks into the enclosing function scope — a legacy quirk, don't rely on it. To use a function outside the block it was created in, assign an expression to a variable declared outside the block.

Third difference: the semicolon. An expression assignment is an ordinary statement, like let x = 5;, so it ends with ;. A declaration is not an assignment — no semicolon needed. The semicolon terminates the assignment, not the function syntax.

cards.question: Which should you use when?
cards.answer:
Prefer function declarations as the default. Reach for an expression when the function is genuinely a value: created conditionally, passed inline, or stored in a data structure.

Declarations win on readability — function calcRent(...) is easier to spot when scanning code than let calcRent = function(...) — and they're callable from anywhere in their scope, so you're free to put the main logic at the top of a file and the helper definitions below.

Expressions earn their place when creation depends on a condition:

let age = 16;

let welcome = (age < 18)
  ? function() { console.log("Hello!"); }
  : function() { console.log("Greetings!"); };

welcome();   // "Hello!"

A declaration can't do this — in strict mode it would stay trapped inside the branch's block.

The other natural homes for expressions: a callback passed straight into another function, and a function stored as an object property or array element. For short inline callbacks, an arrow is the usual form — [1, 2, 3].map(n => n * 2). Arrow specifics are covered in the Arrow functions subtopic.

cards.question: What is a callback function?
cards.answer:
A callback is a function passed as an argument to another function, with the expectation that it will be "called back" later, when the right moment comes. This works because a function is a value representing an action: handing one over hands over the action, and the receiver decides if and when to run it.

function ask(question, answer, yes, no) {
  console.log(question);
  if (answer) yes();
  else no();
}

function showOk() { console.log("You agreed."); }
function showCancel() { console.log("You canceled."); }

// showOk and showCancel are callbacks — ask calls one of them back
ask("Do you agree?", true, showOk, showCancel);
// Do you agree?
// You agreed.

ask doesn't know what agreeing or canceling should do. The caller supplies the actions as values, and ask only picks which one to invoke — in a browser, answer would come from confirm(question) asking a real user.

The handlers don't need names. A function expression can be written right inside the call — created on the spot, unreachable outside it:

ask(
  "Do you agree?", false,
  function() { console.log("You agreed."); },
  function() { console.log("You canceled."); }
);
// Do you agree?
// You canceled.

This anonymous inline form is the everyday shape of callbacks: array methods like [1, 2, 3].map(n => n * 2), timers, and event handlers all take them. How callbacks evolved into promises and async/await is covered in the async topics.

cards.question: What is a named function expression?
cards.answer:
A named function expression (NFE) is a function expression with its own internal name: let sayHi = function func(who) {...}. The name func is visible only inside the function body, and there it always refers to the current function.

That gives the function a reliable way to call itself. The outer variable can be reassigned or nulled — the internal name keeps working:

let sayHi = function func(who) {
  if (who) {
    console.log(`Hello, ${who}`);
  } else {
    func("Guest"); // always the current function
  }
};

let welcome = sayHi;
sayHi = null;

welcome(); // Hello, Guest

Had the body called sayHi("Guest") instead, that line would fail here: by the time it runs, the outer sayHi is null — TypeError: sayHi is not a function. The internal name is immune to this because it is bound to the function itself, not to a variable in the outer scope.

Outside the body the name does not exist — func() there is a ReferenceError. This internal-only name is a feature of function expressions; for a function declaration, the name is just a regular variable in the enclosing scope.

cards.question: What is the function name property?
cards.answer:
Every function has a name property: function sayHi() {} reports "sayHi". Even anonymous expressions get one — the engine takes it from the context of the assignment.

let f = function() {};
console.log(f.name); // "f" — taken from the variable

let user = {
  sayBye: function() {}
};
console.log(user.sayBye.name); // "sayBye" — from the property

let greet = function inner() {};
console.log(greet.name); // "inner" — the NFE name wins

This "contextual name" shows up in stack traces and debuggers, which makes the function easy to identify when debugging. When there is no context to take a name from — say, a function created directly inside an array literal — name is an empty string.

A companion property, length, reports the declared parameter count; rest parameters are not counted:

function f1(a) {}
function many(a, b, ...more) {}

console.log(f1.length); // 1
console.log(many.length); // 2

cards.question: What are arrow functions?
cards.answer:
An arrow function is a shorter syntax for a function expression: (args) => expression creates a function that evaluates the expression and implicitly returns its result.

The syntax adapts to the parameter count and the body:

let sum = (a, b) => a + b;   // expression body: returns a + b implicitly
let double = n => n * 2;     // one parameter: parens optional
let sayHi = () => "Hi";      // zero parameters: parens required

let pow = (a, b) => {        // braces: statement body
  let result = a ** b;
  return result;             // explicit return needed
};

Arrows are always expressions — there is no arrow declaration. One is created when execution reaches it, and you use it through a variable or pass it inline. That makes them a natural fit for short callbacks:

[1, 2, 3].map(n => n * 2);   // [2, 4, 6]

cards.question: What can arrow functions not do?
cards.answer:
Four things: an arrow function has no own this, no arguments object, cannot be called with new, and has no own super.

No own this. An arrow uses the this of the scope it is written in — the full story is in the this & Binding topic. Because of this, arrows are usually wrong as object methods: the method's this will not be the object it is called on.

No arguments object. Use a rest parameter instead:

const max = (...args) => Math.max(...args);
max(1, 5, 3);   // 5

Not a constructor. An arrow has no prototype property and throws when called with new:

const f = () => {};
f.prototype;    // undefined — a regular function would have one
new f();        // TypeError: f is not a constructor

No own super. Like this, it is taken from the enclosing scope.

cards.question: What is an IIFE and why the parentheses?
cards.answer:
An IIFE (Immediately Invoked Function Expression) is a function expression created and called in one go. The wrapping parentheses force the engine to read it as an expression — at statement position the function keyword would start a declaration instead.

A Function Declaration must have a name and cannot be called in place, so both bare attempts are syntax errors:

function() { ... }();     // SyntaxError: a declaration needs a name
function go() { ... }();   // SyntaxError: the trailing () parses on its own

Anything that puts the function in expression position fixes it. Parentheses are the convention, but a leading unary operator also works, and so does the arrow form:

(function() { console.log("a"); })();   // a — parens around the function
(function() { console.log("b"); }());   // b — parens around the whole call
!function() { console.log("c"); }();    // c — ! starts an expression
+function() { console.log("d"); }();    // d — so does unary +
(() => { console.log("e"); })();        // e — arrow form

This is the same statement-vs-expression parsing rule that makes {} + [] behave oddly — see the Coercion topic.

cards.question: What were IIFEs for, and do you still need them?
cards.answer:
IIFEs were the pre-ES6 way to isolate scope: var has no block scope, so wrapping code in a function was the only way to keep variables private and — in browsers — off the global object. Today let/const blocks and ES modules replace almost all of it.

Variables inside the function exist only there. This trick is the seed of the module pattern (covered in the Modules topic; the var quirks themselves are in the Global Scope & var topic):

(function() {
  var message = "hidden";
  console.log(message);   // "hidden"
})();
console.log(message);     // ReferenceError: message is not defined

Now a plain block with let/const isolates just as well, and every ES module gets its own scope for free:

{
  let message = "hidden";
}
// message is not visible here

Two uses are still legitimate. One-shot initialization — run a few statements right where a const is assigned:

const squares = (() => {
  const map = new Map();
  for (let i = 1; i <= 3; i++) map.set(i, i * i);
  return map;
})();
squares.get(3); // 9

And the async IIFE — the way to use await in a non-module script, since top-level await works only in modules:

(async () => {
  const data = await Promise.resolve("loaded");
  console.log(data); // "loaded"
})();

cards.question: What creates a block scope?
cards.answer:
Any code block { ... } creates a block scope: if/else bodies, loop bodies, try/catch, even a standalone block. let, const, and class declarations are confined to the nearest block; var ignores blocks entirely.

if (true) {
  let secret = 1;
  var leak = 2;
}

leak;   // 2 — var ignored the block and attached to the function (or global) scope
secret; // ReferenceError: secret is not defined

var respects only function boundaries — a block is invisible to it. That behavior, and the pre-let IIFE workaround, are covered in The old var in the Global Scope & var topic.

A standalone block is a legitimate tool: wrap a few lines in bare braces to keep temporaries from leaking into the surrounding scope.

{
  let total = 2 + 3;
  console.log(total); // 5
}
// total is gone — no leftover name out here

cards.question: How does for (let ...) scope each iteration?
cards.answer:
for (let i = ...) creates a new binding of i for every iteration — each turn of the loop gets its own copy of the variable. for (var i = ...) creates a single binding shared by all iterations.

let fns = [];
for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) fns.push(() => i);
fns.map(f => f()); // [0, 1, 2] — each function holds its own i

for (var j = 0; j < 3; j++) setTimeout(() => console.log(j)); // 3, 3, 3 — one shared j

Between iterations the engine copies the latest value into the fresh binding, so i++ still counts normally — the loop works as if there were one variable, but each iteration's functions see a private snapshot.

The header let belongs to the loop itself: after the loop, i is gone (ReferenceError), while var j survives with its final value 3.

Why the var version prints 3, 3, 3 — functions capturing the variable, not the value — is covered in the Closure topic.

cards.question: What is globalThis?
cards.answer:
globalThis is the standardized name for the global object, added in ES2020. It is the same name in every JavaScript environment.

Historically each environment named its global object differently, so cross-environment code had to guess which one existed:

EnvironmentGlobal object name
Browserwindow
Web workerself
Node.jsglobal
Everywhere (ES2020)globalThis

All the names point at the same object in their environment — globalThis is just the portable alias:

globalThis.answer = 42;

console.log(globalThis.answer);     // 42
console.log(globalThis === global); // true (in Node)
console.log(globalThis === window); // true (in a browser)

Use globalThis when code must run across environments — a library loaded in browsers, workers, and Node. In code that only ever runs in one environment, the local name (window in browser scripts) is still fine.

cards.question: When should you actually use the global object?
cards.answer:
Sparingly. There are two legitimate uses: feature detection with polyfills, and making an intentional cross-script global explicit.

Feature detection and polyfills. The global object is where built-in APIs live, so checking it tells you whether the environment supports a feature — and assigning to it fills the gap:

// If the environment lacks an API, provide it
if (!globalThis.myApi) {
  globalThis.myApi = { ready: true };
}

console.log(myApi.ready); // true — global-object properties are readable by bare name

An intentional global. If a value genuinely must be shared between separate scripts, write it as an explicit property (browser example):

window.currentUser = { name: "John" };

Writing window.currentUser instead of declaring a bare variable makes the intent visible: this is global on purpose, not by accident.

For everything else, prefer modules and explicit imports (see the Modules topic). Functions that take inputs and return outputs are easier to read and test than functions reaching into globals — keep global variables to a minimum.

cards.question: How is var scoped?
cards.answer:
var has no block scope: a var is either function-scoped or global. Blocks like if, for and while do not contain it.

Declare a var inside a block and it leaks into the enclosing function — or becomes global if there is no function around it:

if (true) {
  var test = true;
}
console.log(test); // true — var ignored the block

With let test the last line would throw ReferenceError: test is not defined, because let respects the block.

Loops are blocks too, so the counter survives them:

for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {}
console.log(i); // 3

The only wall a var cannot cross is a function. Declared anywhere inside a function — even deep inside nested blocks — it is visible everywhere in that function, but not outside it.

cards.question: How does var hoist and redeclare?
cards.answer:
var declarations are processed at the start of their function and begin as undefined, while assignments stay where they are written. And var silently tolerates declaring the same name twice.

So reading a var before its assignment line gives undefined, not an error:

function sayHi() {
  console.log(phrase); // undefined — declared, not yet assigned
  var phrase = "Hello";
  console.log(phrase); // Hello
}
sayHi();

The engine acts as if var phrase were moved to the top of sayHi, while phrase = "Hello" runs in place. Why declarations get registered before any code runs is covered in Hoisting & TDZ in the Scope topic.

Redeclaration is simply ignored — the second var acts as a plain assignment:

var user = "Pete";
var user = "John"; // no error
console.log(user); // John

With let, the second declaration throws SyntaxError: Identifier 'user' has already been declared.

Both behaviors hide bugs: a read placed too early quietly gives undefined, and a duplicate declaration quietly overwrites a variable that already exists. let/const turn both into errors you see immediately.

cards.question: Why does var exist, and should you use it?
cards.answer:
var is the original declaration from the first versions of JavaScript; let and const replaced it in 2015 (ES6). It survives only for backward compatibility with old code.

Its lack of block scope shaped old code style. Since the only thing that contains a var is a function, programmers wrapped code in a function and called it immediately — the IIFE:

(function () {
  var message = "Hello";
  console.log(message); // Hello
})();
// message is not visible here

The wrapper exists purely to give var a private scope. The pattern and its variants are covered in the IIFE subtopic of the Function Expressions & Scope topic; with block-scoped let/const, a plain { } block does the same job.

The modern rule: const by default, let when the variable is reassigned, var only when maintaining legacy code. In new code there is no case where var is the better choice.

cards.question: What is the global object?
cards.answer:
The global object is the object whose properties are available everywhere, without any import: built-ins like Array and JSON, plus environment-specific members like document and innerHeight in a browser, where it's named window.

In a browser, top-level var declarations and function declarations in a classic (non-module) script become properties of the global object. Top-level let/const are still global in scope — every script sees them — but they live in a separate record, not on window:

// browser, classic <script> (not a module)
var test = 5;
let user = "John";

alert(window.test); // 5 — var became a window property
alert(window.user); // undefined — let did not

alert(user); // "John" — global in scope, just not on window

Top-level declarations in an ES module never reach the global object: var and function declarations stay module-scoped. So the var-becomes-a-property behavior matters mainly for old non-module scripts.

cards.question: How should you treat globals in practice?
cards.answer:
Keep globals rare — a function that takes input and returns output is easier to test and reuse than one that reads ambient state. When a value really must be global, assign it to the global object explicitly, so the intent is visible:

window.currentUser = { name: "John" };

// any other script:
alert(currentUser.name); // John

Property access also works in the other direction — to read the global when a local variable shadows it. A bare name goes through scope lookup and stops at the nearest match, but window.currentUser is a property read, so it always reaches the global:

let currentUser = null;             // local, shadows the global

alert(currentUser);                 // null — scope lookup finds the local
alert(window.currentUser.name);     // John — property access bypasses the shadow

To read a global that may not exist, go through the object: a missing property is just undefined, while a bare read of an undeclared name throws. (typeof analytics is the other safe read.)

alert(globalThis.analytics); // undefined — no error
alert(analytics);            // ReferenceError: analytics is not defined

Node note: in a CommonJS module, top-level var is module-scoped, not a property of global. Node wraps every file in a function, so its "top level" is really a function body — don't expect the browser behavior there:

// app.cjs
var x = 5;
console.log(global.x); // undefined — x belongs to this module

cards.question: What is a closure?
cards.answer:
A closure is a function that remembers its outer variables and can access them.

In JavaScript, all functions are naturally closures (with one exception: functions made with new Function). A function automatically remembers where it was created using a hidden [[Environment]] property, and its code can access the variables of that place. What matters is where the function was defined, not where it is called — and that access survives even after the outer function has returned.

The classic example is a counter factory. makeCounter() declares count and returns an inner function that reads and increments it:

function makeCounter() {
  let count = 0;

  return function() {
    return count++;
  };
}

let counter = makeCounter();

console.log(counter()); // 0
console.log(counter()); // 1
console.log(counter()); // 2

By the time counter() runs, makeCounter() has already finished. Yet the inner function still reads and updates count — it closed over that variable. Each call to makeCounter() makes a fresh, independent count:

let a = makeCounter();
let b = makeCounter();
console.log(a()); // 0
console.log(a()); // 1
console.log(b()); // 0  -- b has its own count

So a closure is not a special kind of function you opt into — it is just what happens when a function carries its birthplace scope with it. See the Scope topic for how lexical scope is resolved.

cards.question: Why does a closure keep working after the outer function returns?
cards.answer:
Because the inner function holds a live reference to the outer function's Lexical Environment. As long as that reference exists, the environment stays reachable and is not garbage-collected, so its variables live on.

A Lexical Environment is the internal object that holds a scope's variables. Every function has a hidden property [[Environment]] that points to the Lexical Environment where the function was created — this is the same [[Environment]] link covered in the Scope topic under execution context. The function carries a hidden link to where it was written.

When makeCounter() runs, it creates a Lexical Environment containing count. The inner function it returns captures that environment in its [[Environment]]. After makeCounter() returns, the call's stack frame is gone, but the environment is not — the returned function still references it:

function makeCounter() {
  let count = 0;
  return function() {
    return count++;
  };
}

let counter = makeCounter(); // makeCounter has returned...
counter();                   // ...but count is still alive here

The inner function keeps the Lexical Environment reachable, so the engine cannot reclaim it: the variables live on for exactly as long as a closure references them.

cards.question: Does a closure capture the value or the variable?
cards.answer:
A closure captures the variable (its binding), not a snapshot of the value at the moment the closure was created.

When the function runs, it reads the variable's current value from the lexical environment it closed over. So any change made to that variable after the closure was created is visible the next time the closure runs.

function makeGreeter() {
  let name = "John";
  function greet() {
    return "Hi, " + name; // reads name when CALLED
  }
  name = "Pete"; // mutate after greet was created
  return greet;
}

const greet = makeGreeter();
greet(); // "Hi, Pete"  — not "John"

It re-reads on every call, so a mutation between calls changes the next result:

let count = 0;
const read = () => count;
read(); // 0
count = 5;
read(); // 5
count++;
read(); // 6

To freeze a snapshot, copy the value into its own variable at creation time (a parameter or a fresh let); the closure then captures that binding instead. The var-in-loop pitfall subtopic covers the case where this distinction bites.

cards.question: What surprises come from closing over the variable?
cards.answer:
Closures over the same variable all read the same latest value — they share one binding. But closures from different calls of the outer function get separate bindings and are independent.

Sharing happens when several inner functions close over one outer variable. They don't get private copies; they all point at the same slot, so a write through one is seen by all:

function makePair() {
  let x = 0;
  const get = () => x;
  const inc = () => { x++; };
  return { get, inc };
}

const p = makePair();
p.get(); // 0
p.inc();
p.get(); // 1  — get sees inc's write; one shared x

This shared-variable behavior is the seed of the var-in-loop pitfall, where every loop closure captures the one same var and all read its final value (covered in the var-in-loop pitfall subtopic).

The flip side: each call to the outer function creates a fresh lexical environment, so its inner functions close over a brand-new variable. Two counters from makeCounter() don't share state:

function makeCounter() {
  let count = 0;
  return function() {
    return count++;
  };
}

let counter = makeCounter();
let counter2 = makeCounter();

counter();  // 0
counter();  // 1
counter2(); // 0  — independent count
counter2(); // 1

Same source code, different invocations, different bindings. That is why closures work as private per-instance state.

cards.question: When is a closed-over variable garbage collected?
cards.answer:
A variable's lexical environment stays alive as long as at least one reachable closure references it. Once every function that closed over it becomes unreachable, the environment is eligible for garbage collection.

Each call to a function creates a new lexical environment holding its local variables. Normally that environment dies when the call returns. But if a function created inside it survives the return (e.g. you return it), it keeps a hidden reference to that environment, so the environment and its variables live on. As javascript.info puts it: a lexical environment exists only while there's at least one nested function referencing it.

So a closure can extend a variable's lifetime well past the textual end of its scope. count below is created when makeCounter() runs and would normally vanish at the return — but the returned function keeps it alive.

function makeCounter() {
  let count = 0;
  return function() {
    return count++;
  };
}

let counter = makeCounter();
counter(); // 0
counter(); // 1
counter(); // 2  -- count still alive, held by counter

Drop the last reference and the environment (with count) becomes collectable, like any other unreachable object.

counter = null;
// nothing references count's environment anymore
// -> eligible for garbage collection

The reachability rules behind this — roots and mark-and-sweep — are covered in the Memory & GC topic.

cards.question: How can closures cause memory leaks?
cards.answer:
Holding onto a closure keeps its entire lexical environment alive — including large captured objects you no longer use. If the closure lives in a long-lived place (event listener, cache, timer), that memory is never released.

A closure captures the whole environment it was created in, not just the variables it reads. So even an unused capture stays reachable as long as the closure does.

function makeHandler() {
  const bigData = new Array(1_000_000).fill('x'); // large captured object
  return function onClick() {
    return 'clicked'; // never touches bigData
  };
}

let handler = makeHandler();
handler(); // 'clicked'
// bigData's environment is retained as long as handler is reachable

The leak happens when handler is parked somewhere persistent — registered as a listener, stored in a cache, or used in setInterval — so it never becomes unreachable.

Engines like V8 optimize by retaining only the variables a closure actually references; an obviously-unused capture may be dropped. But this is an optimization, not a guarantee — do not rely on it across engines (and in V8 it has the side effect that such variables become unavailable in debugging).

Fixes are about removing the reference that keeps the closure alive:

el.removeEventListener('click', handler); // remove listeners
clearInterval(id);                        // clear timers
handler = null;                           // null references
// now the closure and its environment can be collected

The underlying reachability and collection mechanics live in the Memory & GC topic.

cards.question: What are closures actually used for?
cards.answer:
Closures let a function remember variables from where it was created, so they power data privacy, function factories, partial application, stateful callbacks, and memoization.

The common thread: an inner function keeps a live reference to its outer scope, so that data survives after the outer function has returned. The main uses:

Data privacy / encapsulation. Variables in the outer function are reachable only by the functions returned from it. See the next card and the Modules topic.

Function factories. A function that builds and returns other functions, pre-loaded with some values. makeAdder(5) returns a function that adds 5 to whatever you give it:

function makeAdder(x) {
  return function (y) {
    return x + y; // x comes from the outer scope
  };
}
const add5 = makeAdder(5);
add5(2);   // 7
add5(10);  // 15
makeAdder(100)(1); // 101

Each returned function closes over its own x, so functions built by separate makeAdder calls never interfere.

Partial application and currying. Fixing some arguments now and supplying the rest later is the same factory idea generalised. The mechanics are covered in the Advanced Functions topic.

State across callbacks and event handlers. A callback can carry private state between invocations because it closes over a variable that outlives each call:

function makeCounter() {
  let count = 0;
  return function () {
    return count++; // remembers count between calls
  };
}
const next = makeCounter();
next(); // 0
next(); // 1
next(); // 2

Attach next as a click handler and each click bumps the same count — no global needed.

Memoization. A wrapper function closes over a cache object and returns stored results instead of recomputing them.

cards.question: How do closures give you private state?
cards.answer:
Variables declared in an outer function are unreachable from outside — only the functions returned from it can read or write them, which makes those variables effectively private.

This was the pre-class way to get private fields in JavaScript, and it is the basis of the module pattern (see the Modules topic). The returned functions share one hidden variable; nothing else has a reference to it.

function makeCounter() {
  let count = 0; // private
  return {
    increment() { count++; },
    decrement() { count--; },
    value() { return count; },
  };
}

const counter = makeCounter();
counter.increment();
counter.increment();
counter.decrement();
counter.value(); // 1
counter.count;   // undefined - not a property, can't be reached

The three methods all close over the same count, so they cooperate, but there is no counter.count property to read or assign — the only way to change it is through increment and decrement. The variable is invisible and untouchable from the outside.

Each call to makeCounter() builds a fresh scope, so separate counters keep separate state:

const a = makeCounter();
const b = makeCounter();
a.increment();
a.value(); // 1
b.value(); // 0

cards.question: Why does a for loop with var and setTimeout print the same number?
cards.answer:
Because var creates one binding shared by every iteration, and the deferred callbacks all run after the loop ends — by then that single variable holds its final value.

A var declared in a loop is function-scoped, not per-iteration. There is exactly one i. Every callback closes over that same i, not a snapshot of it. The closures captured the variable, not the value it had when they were created.

The setTimeout callbacks don't run during the loop — they're queued and fire afterward. The loop has already run to completion, leaving i at its final value, so all of them read the same number.

for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  setTimeout(() => console.log(i));
}
// 3
// 3
// 3

The loop runs i up to 3 (the value that fails i < 3 and stops it), then the three callbacks fire, each reading the live i, which is now 3. Same effect with any deferred call, not just timers — it's the closure reading a shared variable that matters.

cards.question: How do you fix the var-in-loop pitfall?
cards.answer:
Give each iteration its own variable to close over. Use let (modern), or wrap the body in an IIFE that takes i as an argument, or call a factory function that returns a closure over the passed value.

(a) let — the modern fix. A let in a for header gets a fresh binding each iteration, so each callback closes over a different variable. (Why that per-iteration binding exists is owned by Block scope in Function Expressions & Scope.)

for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  setTimeout(() => console.log(i));
}
// 0
// 1
// 2

(b) IIFE per iteration — the pre-ES6 fix. Before let, you wrapped the body in an immediately-invoked function and passed i in. The parameter is a new local variable each call, holding a copy of i's current value.

for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  (function (j) {
    setTimeout(() => console.log(j));
  })(i);
}
// 0
// 1
// 2

The outer parentheses aren't decoration. At statement position the engine reads a leading function keyword as the start of a Function Declaration, which must have a name — function(){...}() throws SyntaxError: Function statements require a function name. Wrapping in parens forces it to be parsed as a Function Expression instead, which you can call right away. Both placements work, as does prefixing with a unary operator:

(function () { /*...*/ })();   // parens around the function
(function () { /*...*/ }());   // parens around function + call
!function () { /*...*/ }();    // unary ! also forces an expression
+function () { /*...*/ }();    // so does +

This is obsolete now — with let there's no reason to write such code. You'll still meet it in old code.

(c) Function factory. Same idea, named: a function takes the value and returns a closure over it. Calling it per iteration captures that iteration's value.

function makeLogger(value) {
  return () => console.log(value);
}
for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  setTimeout(makeLogger(i));
}
// 0
// 1
// 2

All three work by the same mechanism: create a separate variable per iteration so each closure captures a distinct one. Reach for let unless you're constrained to old code.

cards.question: What problem do modules solve?
cards.answer:
Modules let you split a growing codebase into separate files that share functionality through explicit export and import, instead of one shared global scope.

A module is just a file. One script is one module. The export keyword labels the variables and functions that should be accessible from outside the module; import pulls functionality in from other modules:

// sayHi.js
export function sayHi(user) {
  console.log(`Hello, ${user}!`);
}

// main.js
import { sayHi } from './sayHi.js';
sayHi('John'); // Hello, John!

Without modules, every script shares one global scope: any file can overwrite another's variables, and nothing in the code says which file depends on which. Modules fix both — each file gets its own scope, and its dependencies are written out explicitly at the top.

The language had no modules at first; scripts were small. As applications grew, the community invented its own systems — AMD (require.js), CommonJS (Node.js), UMD (compatible with both). Language-level modules arrived in 2015 and are now supported by all major browsers and Node.js; the older systems survive only in legacy code.

cards.question: How did the module pattern fake modules with closures?
cards.answer:
Before language modules, an IIFE played the role of a module: variables declared inside it were private, and whatever it returned became the public interface.

This is the private-state trick from the Closure topic applied to code organization. The returned methods close over the hidden variables; nothing else can reach them:

const counter = (function () {
  let count = 0; // private

  return {
    increment() { count++; },
    value() { return count; },
  };
})();

counter.increment();
counter.value(); // 1
counter.count;   // undefined — no way in

One IIFE per "module" kept variables out of the global scope and exposed at most a single global name. The pattern is obsolete — export/import give the same privacy at the language level — but it is the direct ancestor of today's module scope, and you'll still meet it in pre-ES6 code.

cards.question: What is CommonJS and how do require and module.exports work?
cards.answer:
CommonJS is the module system built for Node.js: every file is a module, module.exports is the value it exposes, and require() loads another module and returns that value.

// math.js
function add(a, b) { return a + b; }
module.exports = { add };

// main.js
const { add } = require('./math.js');
add(2, 3); // 5

Node gives each file its own scope by wrapping it in a function behind the scenes:

(function (exports, require, module, __filename, __dirname) {
  // your file runs here
});

exports is just a local alias for module.exports — the same object. Attaching properties works (exports.add = ...), but assigning a whole new value (exports = {...}) only rebinds the alias and exports nothing. To replace the export entirely, assign to module.exports.

CommonJS is not part of the JavaScript language — it predates ES modules. Node.js still runs it everywhere, and most existing Node code uses it.

cards.question: How does CommonJS behave — loading, caching, copies?
cards.answer:
require() loads and runs the file synchronously, caches the result, and hands every importer the same module.exports value.

Synchronous. require reads the file from disk and runs it before the next line executes. That was fine for a server where files are local — and it's why require can be called anywhere, even inside an if.

Cached. The module's code runs once, on the first require. Every later require of the same path returns the cached module.exports, so all importers share one instance and its state:

// counter.js
let count = 0;
module.exports = { inc: () => ++count };

// anywhere
require('./counter.js') === require('./counter.js'); // true — same object

Values, not bindings. What you receive is the module.exports value as of require time. Destructure a primitive out of it and you hold a copy — if the module changes its internal variable later, your copy doesn't follow. How ES modules differ on exactly this point is covered in ES Modules.

cards.question: What is an ES module and what makes a file one?
cards.answer:
An ES module is a file that uses the language's own export/import. The browser opts in with <script type="module">; Node.js with the .mjs extension or "type": "module" in package.json.

<script type="module">
  import { sayHi } from './sayHi.js';
  sayHi('John');
</script>

With type="module" the browser automatically fetches and evaluates the imported module (and its imports, recursively), then runs the script.

In the browser, import must get a relative or absolute URL — './sayHi.js', not 'sayHi'. Modules without any path are called bare modules and aren't allowed in browser import; Node.js and bundlers resolve them through node_modules.

Two browser details worth knowing: external module scripts with the same src run only once, and modules fetched from another origin require CORS headers. Old browsers ignore type="module"; a nomodule script provides the fallback.

cards.question: How do export and import work — named, default, re-export?
cards.answer:
A module can have many named exports, imported with curly braces by their exact names, and at most one default export, imported without braces under any name you choose.

Named exports. Put export before a declaration, or export a list separately — both are equivalent:

export const YEAR = 2015;
export function sayHi(user) { /*...*/ }

// or declare first, export apart:
function sayBye(user) { /*...*/ }
export { sayBye };

Import by exact name, rename with as, or grab everything as a namespace object:

import { sayHi, sayBye as bye } from './say.js';
import * as say from './say.js'; // say.sayHi(...)

Default export. One per file, for modules that are "one thing". The entity may even be anonymous, because the importer names it:

// user.js
export default class User { /*...*/ }

// main.js — no braces, any name
import User from './user.js';
NamedDefault
Exportexport class User {}export default class User {}
Importimport { User } from ...import User from ...

Technically default is just a name: export { sayHi as default } equals export default sayHi, and import * as obj exposes it as obj.default. Named exports force every importer to use the right name; with default exports each importer picks its own, so names drift across a team — a common argument for preferring named exports.

Re-export. export ... from imports and immediately re-exports — the standard way to build a package entry point that hides internal files:

// auth/index.js
export { login, logout } from './helpers.js';
export { default as User } from './user.js';

Re-exported names aren't usable in the current file. And the default needs explicit handling: export * from re-exports only named exports and ignores the default.

cards.question: What do ES modules change about how code runs?
cards.answer:
Module code always runs in strict mode, in its own scope, and only once — exports are created once and shared by every importer. In the browser, module scripts are deferred by default, and top-level this is undefined.

Always strict. No opt-in needed — assigning to an undeclared variable throws.

Module-level scope. Top-level variables and functions of a module are not seen in other scripts. Sharing goes through import/export, not globals.

Evaluated once. The code runs on the first import; every importer then gets the same exports. Mutate an exported object in one place and all others see it — which enables setup/configuration patterns:

// admin.js
export let admin = { name: "John" };

// 1.js
import { admin } from './admin.js';
admin.name = "Pete";

// 2.js
import { admin } from './admin.js';
admin.name; // "Pete" — one shared object

Deferred by default. Module scripts behave like defer: they download in parallel, wait until the HTML document is fully ready, and keep their relative order. The async attribute also works on inline module scripts — they run as soon as their imports are ready.

Top-level this is undefined, where a non-module script would see the global object.

cards.question: How do ESM imports differ from CommonJS?
cards.answer:
An ESM import is a live, read-only view of the exported variable; CommonJS hands you a value copied from module.exports at require time. ESM is also static — its structure is known before any code runs.

Live bindings. The import doesn't copy; it points at the module's own variable, so updates are visible:

// counter.mjs
export let count = 0;
export function inc() { count++; }

// main.mjs
import { count, inc } from './counter.mjs';
inc();
count; // 1 — the import sees the update

The CommonJS equivalent — const { count, inc } = require('./counter.js') — leaves count at 0 forever: a primitive copied out of the exports object at require time. The binding is also read-only on the importer's side: assigning to count in main.mjs is an error.

Static structure. import/export live only at the top level, and the path must be a string literal. So the whole module graph is known before execution — missing names fail early, and bundlers can tree-shake unused exports. require is an ordinary runtime function call: it can sit inside an if and compute its path, which is flexible but opaque to tools.

When ESM code genuinely needs conditional or computed loading, that's what import() is for — next subtopic.

cards.question: What happens with circular imports?
cards.answer:
Two modules importing each other isn't an error — but the two systems handle the cycle differently. CommonJS hands one side a partial, frozen copy; ES modules share live bindings, so the cycle usually resolves itself.

In CommonJS, when a.js requires b.js and b.js requires a.js back, the inner require returns whatever a.js has put on module.exports so far — and that snapshot never updates:

// a.js
exports.loaded = false;
const b = require("./b");  // b runs now, sees only {loaded: false}
exports.loaded = true;     // b's copy doesn't change

// b.js
const a = require("./a");
a.loaded; // false — forever, even after a.js finishes

ES modules statically build the whole graph first, then run module bodies. Imports are live views onto the exporting module, so a value read later (inside a function, after init) is current:

// b.mjs
import { loaded } from "./a.mjs";
loaded;                          // undefined or TDZ error — a hasn't run yet
export function check() {
  return loaded;                 // true — read after init, binding is live
}

The rules of thumb: in a cycle, don't use imported values at the top level — defer the use into functions; and treat a cycle as a design smell — usually one module is doing two jobs, and extracting the shared piece into a third module dissolves the loop.

cards.question: What is import() and when do you use it?
cards.answer:
import(modulePath) loads a module on demand and returns a promise that resolves to the module object containing all its exports.

Static import is deliberately rigid: the path must be a primitive string (no function calls), and the statement can't appear inside if or a function. That rigidity is what makes the module graph analyzable. import() is the escape hatch for genuine run-time loading:

let { hi, bye } = await import('./say.js');
hi();
bye();

A default export shows up as the default property of the module object:

let { default: say } = await import('./say.js');
say();

Two details: dynamic imports work in regular scripts — no type="module" needed; and despite the parentheses, import() is not a function call but special syntax (like super()), so you can't copy import to a variable or use call/apply with it.

Typical uses: loading a heavy feature only when the user asks for it, picking a module by condition (a polyfill, a locale file), or splitting a bundle so the first paint stays light.

cards.question: What is import.meta?
cards.answer:
import.meta is an object with information about the current module. Its main field is import.meta.url — the URL of the module file (or of the page, for an inline script).

The common use is resolving a file relative to the module itself, not to the page that loaded it:

const iconURL = new URL('./icon.png', import.meta.url);

In Node.js ES modules there is no __dirname or __filename — those belong to the CommonJS wrapper. import.meta.url replaces them, and recent Node versions add import.meta.dirname and import.meta.filename directly.

Unlike this or globals, import.meta is per-module: every module sees its own metadata, which is exactly what makes it the right home for "where am I" information.

cards.question: What is an object and how do you create one?
cards.answer:
An object stores a keyed collection of data: properties, each a string key paired with a value of any type. The usual way to create one is the object literal — curly braces with key: value pairs.

let user = {
  name: "John",
  age: 30
};

(new Object() creates the same empty object as {}; the literal is what everyone writes.)

Read and write properties with the dot, add new ones by plain assignment, remove with delete:

user.name;          // "John"
user.isAdmin = true; // add
delete user.age;     // remove

Property keys are strings or symbols. A number used as a key becomes a string: { 0: "test" } is the same as { "0": "test" }. Reserved words are fine as keys — { for: 1, let: 2, return: 3 } works, because keys aren't variables.

cards.question: When do you need square brackets instead of the dot?
cards.answer:
The dot works only for keys that are valid identifiers. Square brackets take any string — including multiword keys and, more importantly, keys computed at runtime.

let user = {
  "likes birds": true
};

user["likes birds"]; // true — user.likes birds is a syntax error

The real power is dynamic access — the key can come from a variable or any expression:

let key = "name";
user[key]; // reads user.name
user.key;  // reads the literal property "key" — not what you want

To test whether a property exists, use the in operator. Comparing with undefined lies when the property exists but stores undefined:

let obj = { test: undefined };
obj.test === undefined; // true — looks absent, but isn't
"test" in obj;          // true — the property is there

cards.question: What are computed properties and the value shorthand?
cards.answer:
Computed properties let an object literal take its key from an expression: [expr]: value. The value shorthand lets you write name instead of name: name when the variable and the key share a name.

Computed key. Square brackets inside the literal evaluate the expression and use the result as the key:

let fruit = "apple";

let bag = {
  [fruit]: 5,                 // key from a variable -> { apple: 5 }
  [fruit + 'Computers']: 10,  // any expression works -> appleComputers
};

It's the literal-syntax twin of bag[fruit] = 5 — same brackets, same meaning, just done at creation time.

Value shorthand. Building an object from existing variables is so common that repeating the name is unnecessary:

function makeUser(name, age) {
  return { name, age }; // same as { name: name, age: age }
}

Shorthand and normal pairs mix freely in one literal.

One special key to know: __proto__ can't be assigned a non-object value in a literal — such an assignment is silently ignored, because the key is wired to the prototype mechanism (see Prototypes).

cards.question: How are objects stored and copied?
cards.answer:
A variable never holds an object itself — it holds a reference, the object's "address in memory". Assignment copies that reference, so two variables end up pointing at one object. Primitives, by contrast, are copied as whole values.

let user = { name: "John" };
let admin = user;   // copies the reference, not the object

admin.name = "Pete";
user.name; // "Pete" — there is only one object

Comparison follows the same rule: == and === are true only when both sides reference the same object. Two separately created objects are never equal, even with identical contents:

let a = {};
let b = a;
a === b; // true — same object

{} === {}; // false — two different objects

This also explains why a const object can change. The constant is the reference; the object behind it stays mutable:

const user2 = { name: "John" };
user2.name = "Pete"; // fine — the reference didn't change
user2 = {};          // Error — reassigning the reference

cards.question: How do you clone an object — shallow and deep?
cards.answer:
For a flat object, Object.assign({}, obj) or spread { ...obj } makes an independent copy. Both are shallow: nested objects are copied as references. For a real deep copy, use structuredClone(obj).

Shallow copy. Top-level properties are duplicated, so changing clone.name doesn't touch the original. But a nested object is one shared thing:

let user = {
  name: "John",
  sizes: { height: 182, width: 50 }
};

let clone = Object.assign({}, user); // or { ...user }

clone.sizes === user.sizes; // true — same nested object
user.sizes.width = 60;
clone.sizes.width;          // 60 — the "copy" followed

Deep copy. structuredClone duplicates the whole structure, nested objects and arrays included — even circular references:

let clone2 = structuredClone(user);
clone2.sizes === user.sizes; // false — truly separate

let obj = {};
obj.me = obj;                  // circular
structuredClone(obj).me;       // works, points at the clone

Limits: structuredClone can't copy functions or DOM nodes — it throws. For such cases reach for a library implementation like lodash's cloneDeep.

cards.question: What are property flags and descriptors?
cards.answer:
Besides its value, every data property has three flags: writable (can the value change), enumerable (does it show up in loops), configurable (can it be deleted or have its flags changed). A property's value plus its flags is called its descriptor.

Properties created the normal way get all three flags true. The tools:

let user = { name: "John" };

Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor(user, "name");
// { value: "John", writable: true, enumerable: true, configurable: true }

Object.defineProperty(user, "name", { writable: false });

The trap: when defineProperty creates a property, every flag you don't supply defaults to false — the opposite of normal creation:

let obj = {};
Object.defineProperty(obj, "hidden", { value: 42 });
// writable: false, enumerable: false, configurable: false

Object.defineProperties(obj, {...}) defines several at once, and Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptors(obj) returns all descriptors — the pair gives a flag-aware clone, which a plain Object.assign copy is not (assignment copies values only and resets flags to defaults):

let clone = Object.defineProperties({}, Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptors(obj));

cards.question: What happens when each flag is off?
cards.answer:
writable: false makes the value read-only. enumerable: false hides the property from for..in and Object.keys. configurable: false is a one-way door: the property can no longer be deleted or have its flags changed.

Non-writable. Assignment is ignored in sloppy mode and throws in strict mode:

Object.defineProperty(user, "name", { writable: false });
user.name = "Pete"; // TypeError in strict mode

Non-enumerable. The property still works, it just stops appearing in listings — the same reason built-ins like toString don't clutter your loops:

Object.defineProperty(user, "secret", { value: 1, enumerable: false });
Object.keys(user); // no "secret"
user.secret;       // 1 — still readable

Non-configurable. Once set, there's no way back — configurable: false can't be undone, not even by defineProperty. Math.PI is the classic example: non-writable, non-enumerable, non-configurable. One exception exists: on a non-configurable property you may still flip writable from true to false — tightening only.

Object.defineProperty(user, "name", { configurable: false });
delete user.name;                                    // ignored / throws in strict
Object.defineProperty(user, "name", { enumerable: false }); // TypeError

cards.question: Which keys do for..in and Object.keys actually list, and in what order?
cards.answer:
Object.keys lists the object's own enumerable string keys. for..in lists those too, plus enumerable keys inherited from the prototype chain. Order is fixed: integer-like keys sorted ascending first, all other keys in creation order.

The own-vs-inherited difference is the one that bites:

let animal = { eats: true };
let rabbit = Object.create(animal);
rabbit.jumps = true;

Object.keys(rabbit);            // ["jumps"] — own only
for (let key in rabbit) { ... } // jumps, eats — walks the chain

To test ownership, use Object.hasOwn(obj, key) (or the older obj.hasOwnProperty(key)) — how inheritance works is the Prototypes topic.

Ordering. "Integer properties are sorted, others appear in creation order." An integer property is a string that converts to an integer and back unchanged — "49" is one, "+49" and "1.2" are not:

let codes = { "49": "Germany", "41": "Switzerland", "1": "USA" };
Object.keys(codes); // ["1", "41", "49"] — sorted, not creation order

Need creation order for numeric-looking keys? Make them non-integer, e.g. prefix with "+".

cards.question: What are getter and setter properties?
cards.answer:
Accessor properties are functions that run on property read and write, but from the outside look like ordinary properties. get runs when the property is read, set when it is assigned.

let user = {
  name: "John",
  surname: "Smith",

  get fullName() {
    return `${this.name} ${this.surname}`;
  },

  set fullName(value) {
    [this.name, this.surname] = value.split(" ");
  }
};

user.fullName;                // "John Smith" — no parentheses
user.fullName = "Alice Cooper";
user.name;                    // "Alice"

That's the point: callers read and assign fullName like a data property; the functions run invisibly.

In defineProperty terms, an accessor descriptor has get and set in place of value and writable (plus the usual enumerable and configurable). A property is either a data property or an accessor — supplying both get and value in one descriptor is an error.

cards.question: What are getters and setters used for in practice?
cards.answer:
Two main jobs: wrapping a real property to validate or control access, and keeping old code working when a property's implementation changes.

Validation wrapper. The value lives in an underscore-prefixed backing property (a convention meaning "internal, don't touch"), and the setter guards it:

let user = {
  get name() {
    return this._name;
  },

  set name(value) {
    if (value.length < 4) {
      console.log("Name is too short, need at least 4 characters");
      return;
    }
    this._name = value;
  }
};

user.name = "Pete"; // stored
user.name = "";     // rejected

Compatibility. Suppose old code reads user.age, but you now store birthday instead. A getter computes age on the fly, and nothing breaks:

function User(name, birthday) {
  this.name = name;
  this.birthday = birthday;

  Object.defineProperty(this, "age", {
    get() {
      let todayYear = new Date().getFullYear();
      return todayYear - this.birthday.getFullYear();
    }
  });
}

let john = new User("John", new Date(1992, 6, 1));
john.age; // calculated from birthday — old code unchanged

cards.question: How do you lock down a whole object?
cards.answer:
Three levels, each stricter: Object.preventExtensions forbids adding properties; Object.seal also forbids removing them; Object.freeze additionally makes every value read-only.

In flag terms: seal sets configurable: false on all existing properties; freeze sets configurable: false, writable: false.

adddeletechange value
preventExtensions✗✓✓
seal✗✗✓
freeze✗✗✗
 
const config = Object.freeze({ retries: 3 });
config.retries = 5;  // ignored (TypeError in strict mode)
config.delay = 100;  // ignored
config.retries;      // 3

Each has a test: Object.isExtensible(obj), Object.isSealed(obj), Object.isFrozen(obj).

Freezing is shallow: a nested object stays mutable — Object.freeze({ a: { b: 1 } }) still allows obj.a.b = 2. Deep-freezing means walking the structure and freezing each level.

cards.question: What is optional chaining?
cards.answer:
?. stops the evaluation if the value before it is undefined or null, and returns undefined instead of throwing.

The problem it solves — reading a nested property when an intermediate object may not exist:

let user = {};
user.address.street;  // TypeError — user.address is undefined
user.address?.street; // undefined — no error

Before ?. this took repetitive guards: user.address && user.address.street or a ternary. The chain reads naturally now.

Three forms, one rule each:

FormMeaning
obj?.propproperty, if obj exists
obj?.[key]bracket access, if obj exists
obj.method?.()call, if the method exists
 
let admin = { sayHi() { console.log("Hi"); } };
let guest = {};

admin.sayHi?.(); // Hi
guest.sayHi?.(); // nothing — no error

cards.question: What are the limits of ?. and when should you not use it?
cards.answer:
?. works for reading and deleting, not for assignment; the variable before it must be declared; and it should guard only the things that are genuinely allowed to be absent.

Short-circuit. When ?. stops, everything to the right is skipped — including side effects:

let user = null;
let x = 0;
user?.sayHi(x++); // sayHi isn't called...
x;                // 0 — ...and x++ never ran

No writing. ?. produces a value, not an assignment target:

user?.name = "John"; // SyntaxError
delete user?.name;   // fine — deletes if user exists

Declared variable required. user?.address still throws ReferenceError if user was never declared — ?. guards against null/undefined values, not missing variables.

Don't overuse. Put ?. only where absence is a legitimate state. If user must exist by your logic, write user.address?.street, not user?.address?.street — otherwise a real bug (no user) is silenced into undefined and surfaces somewhere far away, harder to debug.

cards.question: What determines the value of this?
cards.answer:
this is not lexical. It is set by how a function is called (the call-site), not by where the function is written.

The same function gets a different this depending on the call. JavaScript decides at runtime by checking the call-site against four rules, in a fixed precedence order. The highest rule that applies wins.

RuleCall-site looks likethis becomes
new bindingnew Foo()a brand-new object
explicit bindingfoo.call(o) / .apply / .bindthe object you pass
implicit bindingobj.foo()the object left of the dot
default bindingfoo()undefined (strict) / global (sloppy)

Precedence, highest first: new > explicit > implicit > default. One function, called four ways:

function whoAmI() { return this; }
const obj = { name: "obj", whoAmI };

obj.whoAmI() === obj            // true   (implicit: left of the dot)
whoAmI.call({ name: "x" })      // {name:"x"}  (explicit)
new whoAmI()                    // a fresh object (new)
whoAmI()                        // undefined / global (default)

The next three cards work through each rule and how they rank.

cards.question: What are default and implicit binding?
cards.answer:
Default binding is a plain call foo(): this is the global object in sloppy mode and undefined in strict mode. Implicit binding is a method call obj.foo(): this is the object left of the dot.

Default binding applies when nothing else does — a bare function call with no object, no call, no new. The mode decides what you get:

function show() { return this; }

// sloppy mode
show() === globalThis     // true

// strict mode
"use strict";
show()                    // undefined

Strict mode is the safe default to assume: code inside ES modules and class bodies always runs strict, so this is undefined there. Reading this.name in that case throws.

Implicit binding kicks in when the call goes through an object. this is whatever sits immediately left of the dot at the call-site:

const obj = { name: "obj", greet() { return this.name; } };
obj.greet()               // "obj"

Only the last link in the chain counts. For a.b.c(), this is b — not a:

const a = { b: { name: "b", c() { return this.name; } } };
a.b.c()                   // "b"

cards.question: What are explicit and new binding, and how do they rank?
cards.answer:
Explicit binding uses call, apply, or bind to force this to an object you choose. new binding makes a fresh object and binds this to it. Full precedence: new > explicit (bind) > implicit > default.

With explicit binding you pass the this yourself. call and apply invoke immediately; bind returns a new function with this locked in:

function greet() { return this.name; }
const user = { name: "John" };

greet.call(user)          // "John"
greet.apply(user)         // "John"
const bound = greet.bind(user);
bound()                   // "John"

A bind is a hard binding — permanent. Explicit beats implicit, but a later call cannot override an existing bind:

bound.call({ name: "Other" })   // "John" — bind wins, call ignored

new binding: calling a function with new creates a fresh empty object and sets it as this inside the function. Assigning to this populates that new object, which is returned:

function Person(name) { this.name = name; }
const p = new Person("Alice");
p.name                    // "Alice"

new sits at the top: it overrides even a hard bind. The bound this is thrown away and the new object is used instead:

function P(name) { this.name = name; }
const BP = P.bind({ name: "preset" });   // try to lock this
const made = new BP("FromNew");
made.name                 // "FromNew" — new won, "preset" ignored
made instanceof P         // true

The call/apply/bind argument and partial-application details live in the call / apply / bind topic. The arrow-function exception (it has no own this at all) is in the Arrow this topic, and why implicit binding is so easy to break is in the The Reference Type topic.

cards.question: What do call and apply do?
cards.answer:
Both invoke a function immediately with a this you choose explicitly. They differ only in how you pass arguments: call takes them one by one, apply takes them as an array.

Normally this is decided by how a function is called (obj.fn() sets this = obj). call and apply override that: you hand the function a function to run and a this to run it with, and it runs right away.

The only difference is the argument shape. Mnemonic: A for Array — apply takes an array.

function greet(greeting, punct) {
  return greeting + ", " + this.name + punct;
}
const user = { name: "John" };

greet.call(user, "Hi", "!");      // "Hi, John!"  — args one by one
greet.apply(user, ["Hi", "!"]);   // "Hi, John!"  — args as an array

With spread, the two are interchangeable: fn.call(ctx, ...args) equals fn.apply(ctx, args).

Method borrowing. Because you pick this, you can run one object's method against a different object — even an array-like one that isn't a real array. A common case is borrowing an array method for the arguments object:

function hash() {
  return [].join.call(arguments, "-");
}
hash(1, 2, 3); // "1-2-3"

// or borrow a method onto another plain object
const counter = { count: 0, inc() { this.count++; return this.count; } };
const other = { count: 10 };
counter.inc.call(other); // 11  — ran inc with this = other

Call forwarding. Passing both the current this and all arguments straight on to another function is called call forwarding. The idiom is func.apply(this, arguments) (or func.call(this, ...arguments)). This is what makes a wrapper this-transparent: it forwards the live this from however the wrapper was called, rather than locking in a fixed one like bind does.

function makeWrapper(func) {
  return function() {
    return func.apply(this, arguments); // forward live this + all args
  };
}
const user = {
  name: "John",
  say(g) { return g + ", " + this.name; },
};
user.say = makeWrapper(user.say);
user.say("Hello"); // "Hello, John" — this is still user

Both call and run now. To get a reusable pre-bound function instead, see bind in this topic.
