cards.question: What does bind do?
cards.answer:
bind returns a new function permanently locked to a given this; it does not call now. Calling that new function always runs the original with the fixed this.

This fixes the classic "losing this" problem. When you pass a method as a callback, the method gets detached from its object and this is lost (in strict mode it becomes undefined):

const user = {
  firstName: "John",
  sayHi() { console.log("Hello, " + this.firstName); }
};

setTimeout(user.sayHi, 0);            // "Hello, undefined" — this lost
setTimeout(user.sayHi.bind(user), 0); // "Hello, John"      — this fixed

The bound function is a separate object — user.sayHi.bind(user) is not user.sayHi. Its this is hard-fixed: call and apply cannot re-point it.

function whoami() { return this.name; }
const boundToA = whoami.bind({ name: "A" });
boundToA.call({ name: "B" });  // "A" — call ignored
boundToA.apply({ name: "B" }); // "A" — apply ignored

The one thing that overrides a bound this is new: constructing the bound function makes a fresh object the this, ignoring what bind set.

Partial application. Extra arguments after the context are pre-filled (locked as the leading arguments), producing a more specific function from a general one:

function mul(a, b) { return a * b; }
const double = mul.bind(null, 2); // a is fixed to 2; this unused, so null
double(3); // 6
double(5); // 10

So call/apply run now with a chosen this; bind hands back a function for later with that this baked in.

cards.question: How does this work in an arrow function?
cards.answer:
An arrow function has no own this. When you read this inside it, the value is taken from the enclosing lexical scope, fixed at the place the arrow is written.

The lookup works exactly like an ordinary variable lookup: this isn't found in the arrow, so the search continues outward to the nearest enclosing function (or the top-level scope). Whatever this meant there is what the arrow uses.

Because the binding is lexical and decided at write-time, call, apply, and bind cannot change it. Passing a different this is silently ignored.

const outer = {
  x: 10,
  getArrow() {
    return () => this.x;   // this = whatever it is in getArrow
  }
};

const arrow = outer.getArrow();
console.log(arrow());              // 10  (this === outer)
console.log(arrow.call({ x: 999 })); // 10  (call is ignored)

This makes arrows the right tool for callbacks written inside a method: the callback inherits the method's this automatically, so the old const self = this or .bind(this) workaround is no longer needed. It also makes them the wrong tool for a standalone object method, where you actually want this to be the object, not the surrounding scope.

The other arrow facts (no arguments, can't be used with new) belong to Function Expressions & Scope.

cards.question: When do arrows fix this, and when do they break it?
cards.answer:
Use an arrow for a callback written inside a method when you want the surrounding this. Do not use an arrow as the object method itself, because then this is the outer scope, not the object.

Fix. Inside a method, a callback passed to setTimeout, an array iterator, or an event handler normally loses the method's this. An arrow keeps it, because it inherits this from the method it's written in.

const counter = {
  x: 42,
  start() {
    setTimeout(() => {
      console.log(this.x); // 42  (this === counter)
    }, 0);
  }
};
counter.start();

const group = {
  title: "Our Group",
  students: ["John", "Pete", "Alice"],
  showList() {
    this.students.forEach(s => console.log(this.title + ": " + s));
  }
};
group.showList(); // "Our Group: John" ...  (this === group)

A regular function in those same spots would get its own this (in strict mode undefined; the array iterator and timer pass their own value), so this.title would fail.

Break. Write the object method itself as an arrow and this is the scope where the object literal sits, not the object. So it reads the wrong value or throws.

const obj = {
  x: 5,
  getXArrow: () => this.x,      // this = enclosing scope, NOT obj
  getXMethod() { return this.x; } // this = obj
};

console.log(obj.getXMethod()); // 5
console.log(obj.getXArrow());  // undefined  (or throws, see below)

What the arrow's this is depends on the surrounding scope. In a browser <script> or a CommonJS module the top-level this is an object (window / module.exports), so this.x is just undefined. In an ES module the top-level this is undefined, so obj.getXArrow() throws TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined. Either way it never sees obj.

cards.question: How do you lose this?
cards.answer:
You lose this by detaching a method from its object: obj.method() binds this to obj, but obj.method alone is a plain function value that carries no link back to obj.

The value of this is decided at call time by how the function is called, not where it was defined. The dot in obj.method() is what binds this to obj. Pull the function out of that call and the binding is gone.

'use strict';
const user = {
  firstName: "John",
  sayHi() {
    console.log(`Hello, ${this.firstName}`);
  }
};

user.sayHi();          // Hello, John   (this = user)

const f = user.sayHi;  // just a function value, no object attached
f();                   // TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined

Once detached, a standalone call gets the default binding: this is undefined in strict mode (so this.firstName throws), or the global object in sloppy mode (so this.firstName is silently undefined).

// sloppy mode
const f = user.sayHi;
f();  // this = global object, this.firstName = undefined

Passing a method as a callback detaches it the exact same way. setTimeout(user.sayHi, 1000) hands over the function user.sayHi separately from user, so when it later runs there is no dot and no binding.

setTimeout(user.sayHi, 1000);
// later: called with no object -> this lost, firstName undefined

Same for button.addEventListener("click", obj.method) in the browser: the listener stores the bare function, so this is no longer your object. (An event listener actually sets this to the element, which is still not your object.)

cards.question: How do you preserve this?
cards.answer:
Keep this attached when you detach a method in three ways: bind it, wrap it in an arrow that calls the method, or define the method as a class-field arrow.

1. bind. obj.method.bind(obj) returns a new function whose this is permanently fixed to obj. It can be called alone or passed anywhere and the binding holds.

const user = {
  firstName: "John",
  sayHi() { console.log(`Hello, ${this.firstName}!`); }
};

const bound = user.sayHi.bind(user);
bound();                  // Hello, John!
setTimeout(bound, 1000);  // Hello, John!

2. Arrow wrapper. Wrap the call in an arrow: () => obj.method(). The arrow has no this of its own; inside it you write a normal obj.method() call, so the dot does the binding at call time.

setTimeout(() => user.sayHi(), 1000);  // Hello, John!

One difference: the arrow re-reads user when it runs, so if user is reassigned before then, it calls the new value's method. bind locks the object in now.

3. Class-field arrow. Defining a method as an arrow in a class field binds this to the instance at construction, so it survives detaching.

class Button {
  text = "Click";
  handleClick = () => {
    console.log("clicked:", this.text);
  };
}
const b = new Button();
const detached = b.handleClick;
detached();                    // clicked: Click
setTimeout(b.handleClick, 0);  // clicked: Click

cards.question: Why does obj.method() keep this but a detached call loses it?
cards.answer:
Because the dot doesn't return the function — it returns an internal Reference Type that remembers the base object, and the () right after uses that base as this. Read the function out of the reference first and the link is gone.

When you write user.hi, the dot operator evaluates to a special internal value the spec calls a Reference Type. It is a triple — (base, name, strict), here (user, "hi", true). It is not the function value itself; it carries which object the property came from.

The call () is the only operation that knows how to read a Reference Type: it takes the base and uses it as this. So in user.hi(), the () sees base user and this becomes user. Square-bracket access does the same: user["hi"]() also produces a Reference Type, so this is still user — the mechanism is property access, not the dot specifically.

user["hi"](); // "John" — brackets produce a reference too, base = user

Almost any other operation reads the plain function value out of the reference and throws the base away. Assignment, passing as an argument, a ternary, a || — each yields just the function, with no memory of where it came from. Call that bare function and there is no base, so this is undefined in strict mode (the global object in sloppy mode).

let user = {
  name: "John",
  hi() { return this.name; },
  bye() { return "bye " + this.name; },
};

user.hi(); // "John" — () reads the reference, base = user

// ternary returns the plain function value, reference discarded:
let cond = true;
(cond ? user.hi : user.bye)();
// TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'name')

// same for assignment:
let f = user.hi;
f(); // TypeError — this is undefined

In sloppy mode that detached call doesn't throw: this falls back to the global object instead of undefined, so this.name reads undefined silently.

How to restore the lost binding with bind is covered in the this & Binding topic.

cards.question: Which operations discard the Reference Type?
cards.answer:
Everything that produces the plain function value out of obj.method: assignment, passing as an argument, returning it, and logical/ternary operators. this survives only when the call is syntactically a method call at the moment of invocation.

The Reference Type lives between the dot and the call. The instant any other operation consumes obj.method, it unwraps to the bare function and the base object is gone:

OperationReference kept?
obj.m()yes — call reads it directly
obj['m']()yes — bracket access also produces a reference
(obj.m)()yes — parens don't change order
let f = obj.mno — assignment
run(obj.m)no — passed as argument
return obj.mno — returned out
(cond ? obj.m : obj.n)()no — ternary
(obj.m || obj.n)()no — logical operator

Plain parentheses do not discard it. In (obj.m)() the dot still runs first and the parens just wrap the result — no extra operation touches the value, so the Reference Type passes straight through to the call.

let obj = {
  name: "Obj",
  go() { return "go " + this.name; },
  stop() { return "stop " + this.name; },
};

(obj.go)();              // "go Obj"  — reference survives
(obj.go || obj.stop)();  // TypeError — || reads obj.go to its
                         // plain value, so the base is gone

So the test is purely syntactic: is () applied directly to obj.method (or a parenthesized form of it)? If yes, this binds to obj. If the function value first flows through a variable, argument, return, or operator, the base is lost. To re-attach it deliberately, use bind — see the this & Binding topic.

cards.question: What is prototypal inheritance?
cards.answer:
Every object has a hidden link, [[Prototype]], to another object called its prototype. When you read a property the object doesn't have, the engine follows that link and looks on the prototype, then up the chain until it finds the property or reaches null.

This means objects inherit from objects directly. There are no classes underneath: a plain object just points at another plain object, and missing reads are delegated upward. Methods live on the prototype and get shared; each object still holds its own data.

You set the link with Object.create, read and change it with Object.getPrototypeOf / Object.setPrototypeOf, or use the legacy __proto__ (handy inside object literals). Here rabbit has no walk of its own, so the engine finds it on animal:

let animal = {
  eats: true,
  walk() { return "Animal walk"; }
};

let rabbit = {
  jumps: true,
  __proto__: animal   // rabbit's prototype is animal
};

rabbit.walk();  // "Animal walk"  -- found on animal
rabbit.eats;    // true           -- found on animal
rabbit.jumps;   // true           -- own property

Object.getPrototypeOf(rabbit) === animal;  // true

The link has limits. A [[Prototype]] can only be an object or null — assign anything else and it is silently ignored. You cannot form a cycle (make A's prototype B and B's prototype A) — the engine throws. And an object has exactly one [[Prototype]], so there is no multiple inheritance.

let obj = {};
obj.__proto__ = 5;                       // ignored — not an object/null
Object.getPrototypeOf(obj) === Object.prototype; // true (unchanged)

let a = {}, b = {};
Object.setPrototypeOf(a, b);
Object.setPrototypeOf(b, a);             // TypeError: cyclic prototype

The chain can be many levels deep, and the search always runs from the object upward. (The __proto__ accessor vs the prototype function property, and where built-in objects sit on the chain, are covered separately under Prototypes.)

cards.question: How do reads and writes differ with the prototype?
cards.answer:
Reads walk up the prototype chain; writes and delete act on the own object only. The prototype is used for reading, never written through.

So assigning obj.x when x is inherited does not mutate the prototype. It creates an own property on obj that shadows the prototype's. Later reads of obj.x find the own copy first and stop. The prototype stays exactly as it was, and other objects sharing it are unaffected.

let animal = {
  walk() { return "Animal walk"; }
};

let rabbit = { __proto__: animal };

rabbit.walk = function() { return "Rabbit bounce"; };  // own property

rabbit.walk();  // "Rabbit bounce"  -- own shadows prototype
animal.walk();  // "Animal walk"    -- prototype untouched

rabbit.hasOwnProperty("walk");  // true

delete rabbit.walk;             // removes the OWN copy only
rabbit.walk();  // "Animal walk" -- inherited again

The one exception is an inherited accessor. If the prototype defines a setter for that name, the assignment runs the setter instead of creating an own data property:

let user = {
  name: "John",
  set fullName(value) { [this.name, this.surname] = value.split(" "); },
  get fullName() { return `${this.name} ${this.surname}`; }
};

let admin = { __proto__: user };

admin.fullName = "Alice Cooper";   // inherited setter runs, on admin
admin.fullName;                    // "Alice Cooper"
admin.hasOwnProperty("fullName");  // false -- no own data prop created
admin.name;                        // "Alice" (own)
user.name;                         // "John"  -- prototype untouched

Why the setter still writes to admin and not user: inside the method this is the object before the dot (admin), so this.name = ... lands on admin. Methods are shared via the prototype; the state they set stays on the calling object. (See this & Binding.)

cards.question: What is the difference between __proto__ and prototype?
cards.answer:
__proto__ is the link every object has to its prototype. prototype is a property only functions have, used to set __proto__ on objects you build with new.

They sound the same but live on different things. __proto__ is on every object and is its [[Prototype]] link — the object that property lookups fall through to. prototype is a regular property that exists on functions (so they can act as constructors); it holds the object that becomes the __proto__ of every instance made with new.

So the connection is: new Foo() gives you an object whose __proto__ is Foo.prototype.

function Foo() {}
let inst = new Foo();

inst.__proto__ === Foo.prototype;  // true

typeof Foo.prototype;              // "object"
({}).prototype;                    // undefined — plain objects have no .prototype
({}).__proto__ === Object.prototype; // true — but every object has __proto__

This pair is the most common confusion in JS OOP: prototype belongs to the constructor function; __proto__ belongs to the instance and points back at that prototype. How the whole link gets walked is covered in The prototype chain.

cards.question: How does new wire up the prototype?
cards.answer:
new Foo() creates an empty object, sets its __proto__ to Foo.prototype, runs Foo with this bound to that object, and returns it.

Because every instance gets the same Foo.prototype as its __proto__, methods you put on Foo.prototype are shared by all instances — one function in memory, not one copy per object.

function Rabbit(name) {
  this.name = name;
}
Rabbit.prototype.sayHi = function() {
  return "hi " + this.name;
};

let a = new Rabbit("White");
let b = new Rabbit("Black");

a.__proto__ === Rabbit.prototype; // true
a.sayHi();                        // "hi White"
b.sayHi();                        // "hi Black"
a.sayHi === b.sayHi;              // true — same shared function

Foo.prototype is read only at the moment new runs. Reassigning it later does not touch instances already made — they keep the old prototype; only objects created afterward get the new one.

function Rabbit() {}
let old = new Rabbit();
Rabbit.prototype = { jumps: true }; // replace after old exists
let fresh = new Rabbit();

old.jumps;    // undefined — old keeps its original prototype
fresh.jumps;  // true      — created after the swap

By default a function's prototype is an object with a single constructor property pointing back at the function, so an instance can find what built it:

Rabbit.prototype.constructor === Rabbit; // true
let r = new Rabbit();
r.constructor === Rabbit;                // true (found via the prototype)

That link is fragile, and nothing enforces it. Replacing prototype wholesale drops the default constructor, so instances report the wrong one. Keep it by adding properties to the existing prototype, or restore constructor by hand. Because JS never checks this value, constructor is only a convention — not a reliable answer to "what built this object".

function Rabbit() {}
Rabbit.prototype = { jumps: true };      // wholesale replace
new Rabbit().constructor === Rabbit;     // false — now points to Object

Rabbit.prototype = { jumps: true, constructor: Rabbit }; // restore it
new Rabbit().constructor === Rabbit;     // true

One caveat: __proto__ is a legacy accessor (a getter/setter inherited from Object.prototype), kept for compatibility. In real code, read and write the link with Object.getPrototypeOf(obj) and Object.setPrototypeOf(obj, proto) instead:

Object.getPrototypeOf(a) === Rabbit.prototype; // true

How this binds during the constructor call is covered in this & Binding; class syntax that wraps all of this is the Classes topic.

cards.question: What is the prototype chain?
cards.answer:
The prototype chain is the series of [[Prototype]] links from an object up through its prototypes, ending at Object.prototype and finally null. Reading a property walks this chain until a match is found, or it reaches null and the read returns undefined.

Every object has a hidden internal slot [[Prototype]] that points to another object (or null). When you read obj.x and obj has no own x, the engine looks on obj's prototype, then that object's prototype, and so on up the chain.

function Foo() {}
Foo.prototype.greet = function () { return "hi"; };
const obj = new Foo();

Object.getPrototypeOf(obj) === Foo.prototype;             // true
Object.getPrototypeOf(Foo.prototype) === Object.prototype; // true
Object.getPrototypeOf(Object.prototype);                  // null

obj.greet();          // "hi"        -- found on Foo.prototype
typeof obj.toString;  // "function"  -- found on Object.prototype
obj.nope;             // undefined   -- walked to null, not found

So the chain for obj is obj -> Foo.prototype -> Object.prototype -> null. greet sits on Foo.prototype; shared methods like toString and hasOwnProperty live on Object.prototype, which is why almost every object has them. new Foo() is what wired obj's [[Prototype]] to Foo.prototype (see Classes for how class builds the same chain).

When an inherited method runs, this is the object you called it on, not the prototype that holds the method. That is how shared methods operate on per-object state.

const animal = { walk() { return this.name + " walks"; } };
const rabbit = Object.create(animal);
rabbit.name = "Rabbit";
rabbit.walk();   // "Rabbit walks"  -- this === rabbit

cards.question: How do lookup, own-vs-inherited, and performance work on the chain?
cards.answer:
Lookup stops at the first match, so an own property shadows an inherited one of the same name. obj.hasOwnProperty(k) tells you whether a key is the object's own or inherited from the chain.

Writing and deleting act directly on the object, never on the prototype. Assigning a key that exists on a prototype creates an own property that shadows the inherited one; the prototype is untouched. Deleting that own key exposes the inherited value again.

const proto = { kind: "animal" };
const dog = Object.create(proto);

dog.kind;                    // "animal"  -- inherited
dog.kind = "dog";            // creates an OWN property
dog.kind;                    // "dog"     -- own shadows inherited
proto.kind;                  // "animal"  -- prototype untouched

dog.hasOwnProperty("kind");  // true
delete dog.kind;
dog.hasOwnProperty("kind");  // false
dog.kind;                    // "animal"  -- inherited again

Because hasOwnProperty is itself inherited from Object.prototype, the lookup distinction matters: a property you read might come from anywhere up the chain, while hasOwnProperty checks only the object itself. (Listing keys has its own own-vs-inherited split via for...in and Object.keys -- see Objects Basics.)

An object made with Object.create(null) has no prototype at all -- a clean dictionary. It inherits nothing, so there is no toString and no chance a user-supplied key like "toString" or "__proto__" collides with an inherited member. Object.keys and friends are static methods on Object, so they still work.

const dict = Object.create(null);
Object.getPrototypeOf(dict);   // null
dict.toString;                 // undefined -- nothing inherited
dict["toString"] = "any value";
dict.toString;                 // "any value" -- safe, no clash
Object.keys(dict);             // [ 'toString' ]

Engines optimize property access by assuming an object's prototype is fixed after creation. Changing it at runtime with Object.setPrototypeOf(obj, p) or obj.__proto__ = p breaks those optimizations and is a major deopt -- set the prototype at creation (via Object.create, a literal, or new) instead of mutating it later.

cards.question: What are native prototypes?
cards.answer:
Native prototypes are the built-in objects that hold the methods for each type. arr.map lives on Array.prototype, "x".toUpperCase on String.prototype, (5).toFixed on Number.prototype. The value itself stores only data; the methods sit one level up on its prototype.

An array doesn't carry a copy of map — its [[Prototype]] points to Array.prototype, which is where map actually is. The dot lookup walks up to find it.

let arr = [1, 2, 3];

arr.__proto__ === Array.prototype;        // true
typeof Array.prototype.map;               // "function"
arr.map === Array.prototype.map;          // true — same function

"x".toUpperCase === String.prototype.toUpperCase; // true
(5).toFixed === Number.prototype.toFixed;         // true

Every native prototype chains up to Object.prototype, which sits at the top with __proto__ === null. That's why every value has toString and hasOwnProperty — they come from Object.prototype at the end of the chain.

let arr = [1, 2, 3];

arr.__proto__.__proto__ === Object.prototype; // true
arr.__proto__.__proto__.__proto__;            // null

Object.prototype.__proto__;                   // null — top of the chain

The chain for an array is arr → Array.prototype → Object.prototype → null. Array.prototype.toString shadows Object.prototype.toString, so arrays stringify to a comma-joined list instead of [object Object].

Primitives are not objects, yet "x".toUpperCase() works: the engine reaches the method through a temporary wrapper that exposes the matching prototype. null and undefined have no wrapper, so no methods. See Coercion / Boxing for the mechanics.

cards.question: Can you reuse a native method on an unrelated object?
cards.answer:
Yes. Many built-in methods are generic — they only need the right indexed keys and a length, not a real array. So you can borrow them onto an array-like object with call/apply, or by assigning the method directly.

An array-like is any object with numeric keys and a length (a function's arguments, a DOM NodeList, a custom object). Array.prototype.join just walks indices 0..length-1, so it works on one even though it isn't an array.

let arrayLike = { 0: "a", 1: "b", length: 2 };

Array.prototype.join.call(arrayLike, "-");   // "a-b"  -- borrow via call

Array.prototype.map.call(arrayLike, s => s.toUpperCase()); // ["A", "B"]

Or copy the method onto the object as an own property — then call it normally. The method still only looks at indices and length, so the object never has to be an array:

let obj = { 0: "a", 1: "b", length: 2 };
obj.join = Array.prototype.join;
obj.join("+");   // "a+b"

This is "method borrowing". It works because the method reads this as whatever object it's called on, and never checks the type — only the shape. (How call/apply set this is in this & Binding.)

cards.question: Should you modify native prototypes?
cards.answer:
No. Adding your own methods to Array.prototype, Object.prototype, etc. — monkey-patching — is a well-known anti-pattern. The prototypes are global and shared by everything, so your additions leak everywhere.

Say you add a convenience last() to every array:

Array.prototype.last = function () {
  return this[this.length - 1];
};

[1, 2, 3].last(); // 3 — works, but now every array has it

The problems:

Collisions. Prototypes are global. If a future language version, or another library, also defines Array.prototype.last, one silently overwrites the other — and code that relied on the other behavior breaks in confusing ways.

Array.prototype.last = function () { return "lib A"; };
Array.prototype.last = function () { return "lib B"; }; // clobbers A
[1].last(); // "lib B"

Enumeration pollution. Added methods are enumerable, so they show up in for...in over every object or array of that type, not just the ones you wrote.

let arr = [10, 20];
for (let key in arr) console.log(key); // "0", "1", "last"

The one historically accepted use is a polyfill: adding a standardized method that an old engine lacks, guarded so it only runs when missing. You're filling a known gap, not inventing new API.

if (!String.prototype.repeat) {
  String.prototype.repeat = function (n) {
    return new Array(n + 1).join(this);
  };
}

"La".repeat(3); // "LaLaLa"

For your own helpers, write a plain function — last(arr) — not a prototype method. Same convenience, no global side effects.

cards.question: How do you create an object with a chosen prototype?
cards.answer:
Object.create(proto) makes a new object whose [[Prototype]] is proto. Pass null to get a clean object with no prototype at all.

This is the explicit, modern way to set up inheritance, replacing the legacy __proto__. The new object is empty but inherits everything from proto through the chain.

let animal = {
  eats: true,
  walk() { return "walking"; }
};

let rabbit = Object.create(animal);
rabbit.eats;       // true   (inherited)
rabbit.walk();     // "walking"
Object.getPrototypeOf(rabbit) === animal; // true

An optional second argument defines properties on the new object using property descriptors (the same { value, writable, ... } shape covered in Objects Basics):

let rabbit = Object.create(animal, {
  jumps: { value: true }
});
rabbit.jumps; // true

That two-argument form gives the canonical full clone: read the source's prototype and all its property descriptors, and feed both in. Unlike a spread or Object.assign, this preserves the prototype and exact descriptors (getters/setters, non-enumerable, non-writable) instead of flattening them to plain data:

let clone = Object.create(
  Object.getPrototypeOf(obj),
  Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptors(obj)
);
// clone has the same prototype and every property exactly as on obj

Object.create(null) gives a prototype-less object, useful as a pure dictionary. It has no inherited methods like toString, and a key named __proto__ behaves as an ordinary property instead of a magic accessor:

let dict = Object.create(null);
Object.getPrototypeOf(dict); // null
dict.toString;               // undefined
dict["__proto__"] = 123;
dict["__proto__"];           // 123  (just data, no accessor)

cards.question: What is the modern prototype API?
cards.answer:
Object.getPrototypeOf(obj) reads an object's prototype and Object.setPrototypeOf(obj, proto) changes it. Together with Object.create they are the standard prototype API, replacing the legacy __proto__ getter/setter.

__proto__ still works in browsers and Node, but it lives in the spec's Annex B (legacy, for web compatibility). Prefer the Object.* methods in real code.

let animal = { eats: true };
let rabbit = Object.create(animal);

Object.getPrototypeOf(rabbit) === animal; // true

Object.setPrototypeOf(rabbit, { hops: true });
rabbit.hops; // true
rabbit.eats; // undefined (prototype swapped)

Avoid setPrototypeOf on a live object. Engines optimize for a fixed prototype chain, so changing it after creation is a deopt that slows down every later property access on that object. When you know the prototype up front, create the object with it via Object.create instead of mutating it later.

One enumeration gotcha: for..in walks inherited enumerable keys too, not just own ones. Guard with hasOwnProperty, or use Object.keys, which only returns own keys (the full enumeration story is in Objects Basics):

let proto = { inherited: 1 };
let obj = Object.create(proto);
obj.own = 2;

for (let k in obj) k; // "own", then "inherited"

for (let k in obj) {
  if (obj.hasOwnProperty(k)) k; // "own" only
}

Object.keys(obj); // ["own"]

cards.question: What is a class and what does the syntax give you?
cards.answer:
A class is a template for creating objects: a constructor that initializes each instance, plus methods shared by all of them. new ClassName(...) creates the object and runs the constructor.

class User {
  constructor(name) {
    this.name = name;
  }

  sayHi() {
    console.log(this.name);
  }
}

let user = new User("John");
user.sayHi(); // John

Under the hood, a class is a kind of function. class User {...} does two things: creates a function named User from the constructor code, and puts the methods (sayHi) into User.prototype. So instances reach their methods exactly the way the Prototypes topic describes — through the prototype chain.

typeof User;                          // "function"
User.prototype.sayHi;                 // the method
Object.getPrototypeOf(user) === User.prototype; // true

Classes also support getters/setters and computed method names, both written to the prototype:

class Greeter {
  ['say' + 'Hi']() { console.log("Hello"); }
}
new Greeter().sayHi(); // Hello

cards.question: Is class just syntactic sugar over constructor functions?
cards.answer:
Mostly — but not entirely. A class is a function with three real differences: it can't be called without new, its methods are non-enumerable, and all its code runs in strict mode.

Must use new. A class function carries the internal flag [[IsClassConstructor]]: true, and calling it without new throws — a plain constructor function would just run:

class User { constructor() {} }
User(); // TypeError: Class constructor User cannot be invoked without 'new'

Non-enumerable methods. Class methods get enumerable: false, so for..in over an instance doesn't drag the methods in.

Always strict. Code inside the class construct is automatically in strict mode.

Like functions, classes are values — there are class expressions, anonymous or named (the name visible only inside), and a function can build classes on the fly:

function makeClass(phrase) {
  return class {
    sayHi() { console.log(phrase); }
  };
}

let User = makeClass("Hello");
new User().sayHi(); // Hello

cards.question: What are class fields?
cards.answer:
A class field — name = value written directly in the class body — creates an own property on each instance, not on the prototype.

class User {
  name = "John"; // field: every instance gets its own copy

  sayHi() {      // method: shared, lives on the prototype
    console.log(`Hello, ${this.name}!`);
  }
}

new User().name;          // "John"
User.prototype.name;      // undefined — not on the prototype

The field value can be any expression, including function calls, evaluated per instance at construction.

The headline use is the bound method pattern. A method passed as a callback loses this (see this & Binding → Losing this). An arrow-function field fixes it, because the arrow takes this from where it was created — the instance under construction:

class Button {
  constructor(value) {
    this.value = value;
  }

  click = () => {
    console.log(this.value);
  };
}

let button = new Button("hello");
setTimeout(button.click, 1000); // hello — this survived

The trade-off: each instance carries its own copy of the function instead of sharing one prototype method.

cards.question: How does extends work?
cards.answer:
class Rabbit extends Animal sets Rabbit.prototype.[[Prototype]] to Animal.prototype. Inheritance is just the prototype chain: a method not found on the instance or Rabbit.prototype is looked up on Animal.prototype.

class Animal {
  constructor(name) {
    this.speed = 0;
    this.name = name;
  }
  run(speed) {
    this.speed = speed;
    console.log(`${this.name} runs with speed ${this.speed}.`);
  }
}

class Rabbit extends Animal {
  hide() {
    console.log(`${this.name} hides!`);
  }
}

let rabbit = new Rabbit("White Rabbit");
rabbit.run(5);  // found on Animal.prototype
rabbit.hide();  // found on Rabbit.prototype

To override a method, define one with the same name in the child — lookup stops at the first match. To build on the parent version instead of replacing it, call it with super:

class Rabbit extends Animal {
  stop() {
    super.stop(); // parent logic first
    this.hide();  // then extend it
  }
}

Arrow functions have no own super — like this, they take it from the enclosing method, which is exactly what you want in a callback inside a method. Any expression can follow extends, e.g. a function call returning a class.

cards.question: Why must a derived constructor call super() before using this?
cards.answer:
Because in a derived class the parent constructor is the one that creates the object. Until super(...) runs, there is no this yet — touching it throws.

A regular function called with new creates an empty object and assigns it to this. A derived-class constructor is internally marked [[ConstructorKind]]: "derived" and skips that step — it expects the base constructor to do it. So the rule: call super() first, then use this:

class Animal {
  constructor(name) {
    this.name = name;
  }
}

class Rabbit extends Animal {
  constructor(name, earLength) {
    super(name);               // creates this
    this.earLength = earLength; // now safe
  }
}

new Rabbit("White Rabbit", 10);

Skip the super() call (or use this above it) and you get ReferenceError: this is not defined.

If a derived class declares no constructor at all, the engine generates one that passes everything through: constructor(...args) { super(...args); } — which is why simple subclasses work without writing a constructor.

cards.question: Why does the parent constructor see its own field value, not the overridden one?
cards.answer:
Because class fields initialize at different moments: in the base class before its constructor body, in a derived class only after super() returns. While the parent constructor runs, the child's fields don't exist yet.

class Animal {
  name = 'animal';
  constructor() {
    console.log(this.name);
  }
}

class Rabbit extends Animal {
  name = 'rabbit';
}

new Rabbit(); // animal — not rabbit

The sequence for new Rabbit(): derived constructor starts → super() → base fields initialize (name = 'animal') → base constructor body runs (logs animal) → control returns → then Rabbit's fields initialize (name = 'rabbit').

Overridden methods don't behave this way — a method lives on the prototype and lookup finds the child's version immediately:

class Animal2 {
  showName() { console.log('animal'); }
  constructor() { this.showName(); }
}
class Rabbit2 extends Animal2 {
  showName() { console.log('rabbit'); }
}
new Rabbit2(); // rabbit

So: if the parent constructor needs a value the child customizes, expose it via a method (or constructor parameter), not a field.

cards.question: How does super actually find the parent method?
cards.answer:
Through [[HomeObject]] — a hidden property set on functions written with method syntax, remembering the object or class they were defined in. super.method() looks in [[HomeObject]].[[Prototype]], independent of this.

Why not just this.__proto__.method.call(this)? Because this always points at the original instance — with a chain two levels deep, the "parent" computed from this is the same object every time, and the call loops forever. super needs an anchor fixed at definition time, and that's [[HomeObject]]:

let animal = {
  eat() { console.log("Animal eats"); } // [[HomeObject]] = animal
};

let rabbit = {
  __proto__: animal,
  eat() {            // [[HomeObject]] = rabbit
    super.eat();     // looks in rabbit's prototype -> animal
  }
};

rabbit.eat(); // Animal eats

Two consequences:

Methods with super aren't free. [[HomeObject]] is permanent — copy such a method onto another object and its super still resolves against the original home, giving the wrong parent.

Only method syntax counts. method() {...} gets a [[HomeObject]]; a function property method: function() {...} does not, so super inside it is an error.

cards.question: What are static methods and properties?
cards.answer:
Static members belong to the class itself, not to instances: User.staticMethod(), not user.staticMethod(). Inside a static method, this is the class.

They implement logic that concerns the class as a whole rather than any particular object. Two classic uses — a comparator and a factory:

class Article {
  constructor(title, date) {
    this.title = title;
    this.date = date;
  }

  static compare(a, b) {
    return a.date - b.date;
  }

  static createTodays() {
    return new this("Today's digest", new Date()); // this == Article
  }
}

articles.sort(Article.compare);
let article = Article.createTodays();

Static properties look the same with a value: static publisher = "Ilya Kantor" → Article.publisher.

Statics are inherited. extends actually builds two prototype chains:

class Animal { static planet = "Earth"; }
class Rabbit extends Animal {}

Rabbit.planet; // "Earth"

Rabbit.__proto__ === Animal;                    // true — statics chain
Rabbit.prototype.__proto__ === Animal.prototype; // true — instance methods chain

One link between the classes themselves (static members), one between their prototypes (instance methods).

cards.question: How do you make class members protected or private?
cards.answer:
Two levels: an underscore prefix (_water) marks a member protected by convention only, while #water is a private field the language itself enforces — readable only inside the class that declared it.

Protected — convention. _name says "internal, access through the accessors". Nothing stops outside code, but the contract is visible, and subclasses can still use it — which is the point of "protected":

class CoffeeMachine {
  _waterAmount = 0;

  set waterAmount(value) {
    if (value < 0) value = 0;
    this._waterAmount = value;
  }

  get waterAmount() {
    return this._waterAmount;
  }
}

A getter without a setter makes the property effectively read-only from outside.

Private — language-level. # fields are checked by the engine. Access from outside the class — or from a subclass — is a syntax error, and there's no dynamic back door: this['#name'] doesn't work.

class CoffeeMachine2 {
  #waterLimit = 200;

  fill(amount) {
    if (amount > this.#waterLimit) amount = this.#waterLimit;
  }
}

new CoffeeMachine2().#waterLimit; // SyntaxError

Why encapsulate at all: users can't break internal state, you can refactor internals freely, and the public surface stays small and documented.

cards.question: What are private methods and static initialization blocks?
cards.answer:
Private methods extend the # rule from fields to behavior — #method() is callable only from inside the class. A static {} block runs once, when the class is defined, for static setup too complex for a single initializer.

Private methods. Same enforcement as private fields: invisible outside, unavailable to subclasses, no dynamic access. Use them for internal steps that shouldn't be part of the API. Private getters/setters and static #members work too:

class CoffeeMachine {
  #waterAmount = 0;

  #checkWater(value) {       // private method
    if (value < 0) throw new Error("Negative water");
    return value;
  }

  fill(amount) {
    this.#waterAmount = this.#checkWater(amount);
  }
}

new CoffeeMachine().#checkWater; // SyntaxError

Static blocks (ES2022). A static property handles static x = 1; a static block handles initialization that needs statements — try/catch, loops, deriving several values together. this inside is the class:

class Config {
  static mode;
  static retries;

  static {
    let env = globalThis.process?.env ?? {};
    this.mode = env.MODE ?? "dev";
    this.retries = this.mode === "dev" ? 0 : 3;
  }
}

Config.mode; // computed once, at class definition

A class may have several static blocks; they run in order, top to bottom, exactly once.

cards.question: How does instanceof work?
cards.answer:
obj instanceof Class walks the object's prototype chain and asks at each step: is this Class.prototype? One match → true. So it respects inheritance — an instance of a subclass is an instance of the parent too.

// the actual check:
// obj.__proto__ === Class.prototype?
// obj.__proto__.__proto__ === Class.prototype?
// ...until null

class Animal {}
class Rabbit extends Animal {}

let rabbit = new Rabbit();
rabbit instanceof Rabbit; // true — first link
rabbit instanceof Animal; // true — second link

Note what it does not check: the constructor function itself. Only Class.prototype matters. The same test can be written as Class.prototype.isPrototypeOf(obj).

That leads to the classic caveat — reassign the prototype and existing instances stop matching:

function Rabbit2() {}
let r = new Rabbit2();

Rabbit2.prototype = {};
r instanceof Rabbit2; // false — r's chain still points at the old prototype

cards.question: How do you customize type checks beyond instanceof?
cards.answer:
A class can take over instanceof with a static Symbol.hasInstance method. And Object.prototype.toString.call(x) works as a "typeof on steroids" that names the type of anything, customizable via Symbol.toStringTag.

Symbol.hasInstance. If the class defines it, instanceof calls it instead of walking the chain:

class Animal {
  static [Symbol.hasInstance](obj) {
    if (obj.canEat) return true;
  }
}

let obj = { canEat: true };
obj instanceof Animal; // true — duck-typing check, no chain involved

toString as a type tag. The built-in Object.prototype.toString returns [object Type] for primitives, built-ins, even null:

let s = Object.prototype.toString;
s.call([]);   // [object Array]
s.call(123);  // [object Number]
s.call(null); // [object Null]

let user = { [Symbol.toStringTag]: "User" };
s.call(user); // [object User]

Picking the tool:

works forreturns
typeofprimitivesstring
{}.toStringprimitives, built-ins, tagged objectsstring
instanceofobjects, with inheritanceboolean

cards.question: What is a mixin and when do you reach for one?
cards.answer:
A class can extend only one class. A mixin is an object of methods that other classes copy in — added behavior without joining the inheritance chain.

The mechanism is one line: copy the methods into the prototype.

let sayHiMixin = {
  sayHi() { console.log(`Hello ${this.name}`); },
  sayBye() { console.log(`Bye ${this.name}`); }
};

class User {
  constructor(name) {
    this.name = name;
  }
}

Object.assign(User.prototype, sayHiMixin);

new User("Dude").sayHi(); // Hello Dude

extends stays free — class User extends Person plus the mixin works fine, because the mixin isn't inheritance, just copied methods.

Mixins can even have their own internal inheritance and use super: a mixin method's [[HomeObject]] points at the mixin, so super resolves through the mixin's prototype, not the consuming class's.

The practical classic is an event mixin that gives any class on/off/trigger:

Object.assign(Menu.prototype, eventMixin);

let menu = new Menu();
menu.on("select", value => console.log(`Selected: ${value}`));
menu.choose("123"); // fires trigger("select", "123") internally

The risk: a mixin method can silently overwrite an existing class method with the same name — name carefully. Reach for mixins when several unrelated classes need the same capability and inheritance can't express it.

cards.question: How does JavaScript store numbers?
cards.answer:
Regular numbers are stored in 64-bit IEEE-754 format — "double precision" floats. There is no separate integer type: 5 and 5.0 are the same kind of value. The 64 bits split into a sign bit, 11 exponent bits, and 52 bits for the digits.

Those 52 bits are why integers are exact only up to 253-1 (about 9 quadrillion) — beyond that, integers need BigInt (last subtopic).

The sign being its own bit also produces two zeros, 0 and -0 — equal in almost every operation (see Types → Negative zero).

Ways to write numbers:

let billion = 1e9;          // e-notation: 1 and 9 zeros
let ms = 1e-6;              // 0.000001 — divide by 10^6
let readable = 1_000_000_000; // underscores are ignored, purely visual

0xff;       // 255 — hexadecimal
0b11111111; // 255 — binary
0o377;      // 255 — octal

cards.question: Why is 0.1 + 0.2 not equal to 0.3?
cards.answer:
Because 0.1 and 0.2 have no exact binary representation — in base 2 they are endless repeating fractions, like 1/3 in decimal. The stored values are tiny approximations, and their sum lands just off 0.3.

0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3; // false
0.1 + 0.2;        // 0.30000000000000004

0.1.toString(2);  // "0.000110011001100110011..." — repeats forever, gets cut

IEEE-754 rounds each number to the nearest representable value. 0.1 is stored slightly over, 0.2 slightly over — added together the error becomes visible.

Fixes — make the rounding explicit:

let sum = 0.1 + 0.2;
sum.toFixed(2);   // "0.30" — string, good for display
+sum.toFixed(2);  // 0.3 — back to a number

Working in integer units (cents instead of dollars) avoids fractions entirely — that's the standard approach for money. Multiplying and dividing by 10s ((0.1*10 + 0.2*10) / 10) reduces the error but doesn't eliminate it: the scaled values are still floats.

cards.question: What is a safe integer?
cards.answer:
An integer that fits exactly in the 52 digit bits: anything up to Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER = 253-1 = 9007199254740991. Beyond it, not every integer exists — values silently snap to the nearest representable one.

9999999999999999; // 10000000000000000 — off by one, no error

Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER;     // 9007199254740991
Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER + 1; // 9007199254740992
Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER + 2; // 9007199254740992 — same! the gap began

"Safe" means every integer in the range has its own unique representation, so arithmetic and comparison behave as expected. Past the limit, adjacent integers start sharing one stored value, and equality checks lie.

Number.isSafeInteger(x) tests for it. Real-world hits: database 64-bit IDs and timestamps in JSON — parse them as strings or BigInt, not numbers.

Related constant: Number.EPSILON — the smallest gap above 1, handy as a tolerance for float comparison: Math.abs(a - b) < Number.EPSILON.

cards.question: How do you round numbers?
cards.answer:
Four Math methods for whole numbers — floor (down), ceil (up), round (nearest), trunc (drop the fraction) — and toFixed(n) or multiply-round-divide for n decimal places.

3.13.6-1.1-1.6
Math.floor33-2-2
Math.ceil44-1-1
Math.round34-1-2
Math.trunc33-1-1

Note the negatives: floor goes toward minus infinity, trunc toward zero — they differ exactly there.

To n decimal places:

let num = 1.23456;

Math.round(num * 100) / 100; // 1.23 — number
num.toFixed(2);              // "1.23" — string!

toFixed rounds (not truncates), pads with zeros (12.34.toFixed(5) → "12.34000"), and returns a string — convert back with unary plus when you need a number.

cards.question: How do you convert between numbers and strings in different bases?
cards.answer:
num.toString(base) writes a number in any base from 2 to 36. parseInt and parseFloat read a number from the start of a string, stopping at the first character that doesn't fit.

let num = 255;
num.toString(16); // "ff"
num.toString(2);  // "11111111"

Calling a method directly on a literal needs two dots — the first dot reads as a decimal point: 123456..toString(36), or parentheses: (123456).toString(36).

Number("100px") gives NaN — strict conversion fails on any junk. The parse functions are the lenient readers for exactly that case:

parseInt('100px');    // 100
parseFloat('12.5em'); // 12.5
parseInt('12.3');     // 12 — integer only
parseFloat('12.3.4'); // 12.3 — stops at the second dot
parseInt('a123');     // NaN — nothing readable at the start

parseInt takes a radix as its second argument: parseInt('ff', 16) → 255.

Everyday Math extras: Math.random() — random in [0, 1); Math.max(a, b, ...) / Math.min(...); Math.pow(2, 10) → 1024 (same as 2 ** 10).

cards.question: What's the difference between isNaN and Number.isNaN?
cards.answer:
The global isNaN(x) converts x to a number first, so any unparseable string reads as "NaN". Number.isNaN(x) converts nothing — it's true only when x already is the value NaN. Same split for isFinite vs Number.isFinite.

isNaN("str");        // true  — "str" coerces to NaN
Number.isNaN("str"); // false — it's a string, not NaN

isFinite("15");        // true  — coerces to 15
Number.isFinite("15"); // false — not a number type

Why these functions exist at all: NaN is the one value not equal to itself, so x === NaN can never detect it:

NaN === NaN; // false

The strict Number.* versions are the modern defaults — the coercing globals answer a vaguer question ("does this fail to be a number?") and surprise you with strings.

Object.is is the third detector worth knowing: Object.is(NaN, NaN) is true, and it also distinguishes Object.is(0, -0) → false — the SameValue semantic from the Equality topic.

cards.question: What is BigInt and how does it behave?
cards.answer:
BigInt is a separate numeric type for integers of arbitrary length — no 253 ceiling. Create one with the n suffix or BigInt().

const big = 1234567890123456789012345678901234567890n;
const fromString = BigInt("1234567890123456789012345678901234567890");
const fromNumber = BigInt(10); // 10n

Arithmetic works like regular numbers, with three rules to remember:

No mixing. A bigint and a number can't meet in one operation — convert explicitly:

1n + 2; // TypeError: Cannot mix BigInt and other types

1n + BigInt(2);   // 3n
Number(1n) + 2;   // 3

Integer division. Division drops the fraction, rounding toward zero: 5n / 2n → 2n.

No unary plus. +big throws — use Number(big) to convert.

Comparisons cross the type line fine: 2n > 1 is true, 1n == 1 is true — but 1n === 1 is false, different types. In boolean context 0n is falsy, everything else truthy.

BigInt can't be polyfilled directly (operators can't be overridden); the JSBI library is the workaround for old targets.

cards.question: What are the essential string basics?
cards.answer:
Strings are immutable sequences of UTF-16 code units. Every "modifying" method returns a new string — the original never changes.

let str = "Hi";
str[0] = "h";   // silently fails (TypeError in strict mode)
str;            // "Hi" — unchanged

str = "h" + str.slice(1); // the way: build a new string

The everyday toolkit:

let s = "  Interface  ";

s.length;          // 13 — a property, not a method: no parentheses
s.trim();          // "Interface"
s.toUpperCase();   // "  INTERFACE  "
"ho".repeat(3);    // "hohoho"

Character access: str[i] works for plain indexes but returns undefined for negatives; str.at(i) accepts negatives, so str.at(-1) is the last character.

let word = "Hello";
word[0];     // "H"
word[-1];    // undefined
word.at(-1); // "o"

cards.question: How do you search in and slice strings?
cards.answer:
Search with includes/startsWith/endsWith for yes-or-no, indexOf when you need the position. Extract with slice(start, end) — the one substring method worth defaulting to.

The indexOf trap: it returns -1 for "not found", and position 0 is falsy — so always compare, never truthy-check:

let str = "Widget";
if (str.indexOf("Widget")) { ... }       // wrong — 0 is falsy, branch skipped
if (str.indexOf("Widget") !== -1) { ... } // right
if (str.includes("Widget")) { ... }       // best — boolean by design

The three extractors:

methodselectsnegatives
slice(start, end)from start to end (end excluded)allowed — count from the end
substring(start, end)between the indexestreated as 0; swaps reversed args
substr(start, length)length chars from startstart only; legacy, avoid
 
"stringify".slice(0, 5); // "strin"
"stringify".slice(-4);   // "gify"

And the string→array exit: str.split(delimiter) cuts by a substring or regexp — "12-34-56".split("-") gives ["12", "34", "56"]; its mirror is arr.join.

Comparison uses UTF-16 codes, so "a" > "Z" — lowercase codes are higher. For human-correct, language-aware ordering use str.localeCompare(other) (negative/zero/positive), which also handles accented letters.

cards.question: What do template literals give you?
cards.answer:
Backtick strings with two superpowers: ${expression} embeds any expression directly in the text, and line breaks are allowed as-is — real multiline strings.

let name = "John";
let age = 30;

`Hello, ${name}!`;            // expression in place
`1 + 2 = ${1 + 2}.`;           // any expression, not just variables
`${age >= 18 ? "adult" : "minor"}`; // even a ternary

Versus concatenation, the text reads in one piece — no + seams, no quote juggling:

// before
let msg = "User " + name + " is " + age + " years old";
// after
let msg2 = `User ${name} is ${age} years old`;

Multiline without \n gymnastics:

let list = `Guests:
 * John
 * Pete
 * Mary`;

Both quote characters are free inside backticks, and templates nest: `outer ${ `inner ${x}` }`. The embedded expression is converted to a string the usual way (ToString — see Coercion).

cards.question: What is a tagged template?
cards.answer:
A function name placed before a template literal — tag`...` — makes the engine call that function with the string parts and the embedded values separately, letting the tag decide how to combine them.

The tag receives the literal chunks as an array, then each ${} value as its own argument:

function tag(strings, ...values) {
  // strings: ["Hello ", ", you are ", "!"]
  // values:  ["John", 30]
  return strings[0] + values[0].toUpperCase() + strings[1] + values[1] + strings[2];
}

let name = "John";
tag`Hello ${name}, you are ${30}!`; // "Hello JOHN, you are 30!"

The point: the tag sees the values before they're merged into the string, so it can escape, transform, or route them. That's how HTML-escaping templates, SQL parameter binding, i18n libraries, and styled-components work — the syntax of a string, the safety of a function.

strings.raw holds the chunks with escapes untouched (\n as two characters). The built-in String.raw tag uses it — handy for Windows paths and regex sources:

String.raw`C:\new\table`; // "C:\\new\\table" — no newline, no tab

cards.question: Why do emoji break length, and what handles them correctly?
cards.answer:
A string is a sequence of 16-bit code units, but Unicode has more characters than 16 bits can hold. Rare symbols and emoji are stored as a surrogate pair — two code units — so .length counts them as 2, and index access returns half a character.

'😂'.length; // 2 — one symbol, two code units
'𝒳'[0];      // garbage — half of a surrogate pair
'𝒳'.charCodeAt(0).toString(16); // "d835" — only the first unit

The pair-aware tools: codePointAt reads the full character, String.fromCodePoint builds one, and for..of iterates by real characters:

'𝒳'.codePointAt(0).toString(16); // "1d4b3" — the whole character

[...'Hi😂'].length;              // 3 — spread uses for..of
for (let ch of '😂') { ... }     // one iteration, whole emoji

A second wrinkle: combining marks. The same visible glyph can be one precomposed character or a base letter plus combining accents — different code units, so == says false. normalize() brings both to one canonical form:

let s1 = 'S\u0307\u0323'; // Ṩ — S + dot above + dot below
let s2 = 'S\u0323\u0307'; // Ṩ — same look, different order

s1 == s2;                         // false
s1.normalize() == s2.normalize(); // true

Rule of thumb: any code that slices or measures user text by index is wrong for emoji and accents — iterate with for..of or normalize first.

cards.question: Which modern string methods should you know?
cards.answer:
The recent additions that remove old workarounds: replaceAll (all occurrences without regex), at (negative indexes), padStart/padEnd (fixed-width output), trimStart/trimEnd (one-sided trim).

replaceAll. Plain replace with a string replaces only the first match — the classic surprise. Before ES2021 you needed a /g regex; now:

"a-b-c".replace("-", "+");    // "a+b-c" — first only!
"a-b-c".replaceAll("-", "+"); // "a+b+c"

at. Index from the end without str[str.length - 1]:

"Hello".at(-1); // "o"

padStart / padEnd. Pad to a target length — clocks, tables, invoice numbers:

"5".padStart(2, "0");    // "05"
"7".padStart(4, "0");    // "0007"
"abc".padEnd(6, ".");    // "abc..."

trimStart / trimEnd. trim for one side only:

"  hi  ".trimStart(); // "hi  "
"  hi  ".trimEnd();   // "  hi"

All of these work on the immutable principle — each returns a new string.

cards.question: What is a regular expression and how do you create one?
cards.answer:
A regular expression is a search pattern plus optional flags. There are two ways to create one: the literal /pattern/flags and the constructor new RegExp("pattern", "flags").

let re1 = /ab+c/i;                  // literal — pattern known at write time
let re2 = new RegExp("ab+c", "i");  // constructor — pattern built at runtime

The literal uses slashes as delimiters, the way quotes delimit strings — and like a string literal, it's fully static: you can't insert variables into it. When the pattern depends on data, build it as a string:

let tag = prompt("What tag?", "h2");
let re = new RegExp(`<${tag}>`);   // /<h2>/ from user input

Regexps are used through string methods (match, replace, split…) and regexp methods (test, exec):

"I love JavaScript".search(/love/); // 2
/love/.test("I love JavaScript");   // true

cards.question: What do the six flags do?
cards.answer:
Flags change how the pattern searches. There are exactly six: i, g, m, s, u, y.

flageffect
icase-insensitive — A and a are the same
gfind all matches, not just the first
mmultiline — ^ and $ work per line
s"dotall" — lets . match newlines
ufull Unicode support — surrogate pairs, \p{...}
ysticky — match at an exact position only
 
"We will, we will".match(/we/gi); // ["We", "we"] — all matches, any case
"We will, we will".match(/we/i);  // ["We"] + index/input — first only

g is the one you'll reach for most — without it, methods stop at the first match. The others each get their own card where they matter: m with anchors, s with the dot, u with Unicode classes, y with methods.

cards.question: Which characters need escaping — and why does new RegExp need double backslashes?
cards.answer:
These have special meaning and need a backslash to be matched literally: [ ] { } ( ) \ ^ $ . | ? * +. In a regex literal, the slash / too — it's the delimiter.

/\d\.\d/.test("5.1");  // true  — \. is a literal dot
/\d.\d/.test("5A1");   // true too — unescaped . matches anything

"function g()".match(/g\(\)/); // "g()"
"/usr/bin".match(/\/usr/);     // slash escaped in a literal

With new RegExp there's a second layer: string quotes consume backslashes first. In the string "\d", the quotes see an unknown escape and strip the backslash — the constructor receives just "d". So every regex backslash must be doubled:

new RegExp("\d\.\d").test("5.1");   // false — pattern became d.d
new RegExp("\\d\\.\\d").test("5.1"); // true — \\ survives as \

Rule: literal syntax → single escaping; constructor syntax → double escaping.

cards.question: What are the main character classes?
cards.answer:
A character class is shorthand for "any character from a certain set". The three core ones: \d — a digit, \s — a space character (space, tab \t, newline \n…), \w — a "wordly" character: a Latin letter, digit, or underscore.

"+7(903)-123-45-67".match(/\d/);   // "7" — first digit
"+7(903)-123-45-67".match(/\d/g);  // all digits
"+7(903)-123-45-67".replace(/\D/g, ""); // "79031234567"

Each class has an uppercase inverse: \D — any non-digit, \S — any non-space, \W — any non-wordly character.

Classes mix freely with regular characters — \d\d:\d\d matches a time like 09:30. Two things to keep in mind:

"1 - 5".match(/\d-\d/);   // null — the spaces aren't in the pattern
"1 - 5".match(/\d - \d/); // works: in a regexp all characters matter, spaces too

And \w means Latin letters only — Cyrillic or hieroglyphs don't count as wordly.

cards.question: What does the dot match?
cards.answer:
The dot . is "any character except a newline". It's a class like \d — just the broadest one.

"CSS4".match(/CS.4/);  // "CSS4"
"CS-4".match(/CS.4/);  // "CS-4"
"CS 4".match(/CS.4/);  // "CS 4" — space is a character too

It matches "any character", not "absence of a character" — there must be something there:

"CS4".match(/CS.4/); // null — no character between S and 4

The newline exception trips people up with multiline text. Two fixes — the s flag makes the dot truly match anything, or the older idiom [\s\S] ("a space or not a space" — anything):

"A\nB".match(/A.B/);        // null
"A\nB".match(/A.B/s);       // "A\nB"
"A\nB".match(/A[\s\S]B/);   // "A\nB" — works without the flag

cards.question: How do sets and ranges work?
cards.answer:
Square brackets [...] match one character from those listed. [eao] means "e, a, or o"; a dash makes a range: [a-z], [0-5].

"Mop top".match(/[tm]op/gi);  // ["Mop", "top"]
"Voila".match(/V[oi]la/);     // null — a set is exactly one character

"Exception 0xAF".match(/x[0-9A-F][0-9A-F]/g); // ["xAF"] — ranges combine

Character classes work inside sets too — [\d\s] is "a digit or a space". In fact \d is just shorthand for [0-9], and \w for [a-zA-Z0-9_].

A caret first in the brackets excludes: [^...] matches any character except the listed ones:

"alice15@gmail.com".match(/[^\d\sA-Z]/gi); // ["@", "."]

Inside brackets most special characters lose their powers — [.,+()] needs no escaping. Only watch the dash (put it first or last so it can't form a range), a caret anywhere but the start, and ], which always needs escaping to be literal.

cards.question: What do the u flag and \p{...} give you?
cards.answer:
The u flag turns on full Unicode handling: 4-byte characters (emoji, rare symbols) are treated as one character instead of two, and Unicode property classes \p{...} become available.

Without it, a surrogate-pair character is seen as two broken halves — sets and ranges over such characters misbehave or throw. With it, things just work:

"𝒳".match(/[𝒳𝒴]/);   // wrong / error — pair split in half
"𝒳".match(/[𝒳𝒴]/u);  // "𝒳"

\p{...} matches characters by their Unicode property — far beyond Latin-only \w:

"число: 123".match(/\p{L}/gu);     // all letters, any language
"x05 xAF".match(/x\p{Hex_Digit}\p{Hex_Digit}/gu); // ["xAF", "x05"]
"Prices: $2, €1".match(/\p{Sc}\d/gu); // ["$2", "€1"] — currency symbols
"Hello 你好".match(/\p{sc=Han}/gu);   // ["你", "好"] — by script

Main categories: L letters, N numbers, P punctuation, S symbols, Z separators, M combining marks. The gotcha: \p{...} silently does nothing without the u flag.

cards.question: What do ^ and $ do?
cards.answer:
The caret ^ matches at the beginning of the text, the dollar $ at the end. They are tests of position, not characters — they have zero width.

/^Mary/.test("Mary had a little lamb"); // true — starts with Mary
/snow$/.test("its fleece was white as snow"); // true — ends with snow

Together, ^...$ means "the whole string must match the pattern" — the standard shape for validation:

/^\d\d:\d\d$/.test("12:34");     // true
/^\d\d:\d\d$/.test("12:345");    // false — extra character
/^\d\d:\d\d$/.test("ok 12:34");  // false — extra prefix

Because anchors match positions rather than characters, the empty string passes /^$/ — the engine finds the start and is immediately at the end.

cards.question: What does the m flag change about anchors?
cards.answer:
With the m flag, ^ and $ match at the start and end of every line, not just the whole text.

let str = `1st place: Winnie
2nd place: Piglet
3rd place: Eeyore`;

str.match(/^\d/g);   // ["1"] — one match, start of text only
str.match(/^\d/gm);  // ["1", "2", "3"] — start of each line

"Start of a line" means "immediately after a line break \n" — the anchor tests the position around the newline without consuming it. That's the difference from matching \n directly, which grabs the actual character into the result:

str.match(/\d$/gm);  // ["1", "2", "3"] — positions before each break + text end
str.match(/\d\n/g);  // ["1\n", "2\n"] — real characters; no \n after the last line

Use anchors to test positions; use \n when you actually want the newline in the match.

cards.question: What is a word boundary \b?
cards.answer:
\b is a position test, like ^ and $: it matches where a wordly character (\w) meets a non-wordly one. It's how you match a whole word, not a fragment of a longer one.

Three positions qualify: the start of the string if it begins with \w, between a \w and a non-\w character, and the end of the string if it ends with \w.

"Hello, Java!".match(/\bJava\b/);       // "Java"
"Hello, JavaScript!".match(/\bJava\b/); // null — no boundary after "Java"

Works for numbers too — \b\d\d\b finds standalone two-digit numbers:

"1 23 456 78".match(/\b\d\d\b/g); // ["23", "78"]
"12,34,56".match(/\b\d\d\b/g);    // ["12", "34", "56"] — commas are boundaries

The limitation is inherited from \w: it only knows Latin letters, digits, and underscore — \b gives wrong results on Cyrillic or hieroglyphs.

cards.question: What are lookahead and lookbehind?
cards.answer:
Lookarounds match something only if it's (not) followed or preceded by something else — without including that context in the match. Four forms:

patternmeaning
X(?=Y)X if followed by Y
X(?!Y)X if not followed by Y
(?<=Y)XX if preceded by Y
(?<!Y)XX if not preceded by Y
 
let str = "1 turkey costs 30€";

str.match(/\d+(?=€)/);    // "30" — the € is checked but not captured
str.match(/(?<=\$)\d+/);  // price after a dollar sign
"2 turkeys cost 60€".match(/\d+\b(?!€)/g); // ["2"] — quantity, not price

Like anchors, lookarounds are zero-width: the contents of the parentheses is a test, not part of the result. If you do want the context, add a capturing group inside it:

str.match(/\d+(?=(€|kr))/); // captures "30" and "€" separately

cards.question: How do capturing groups work?
cards.answer:
Parentheses (...) do two jobs: they let a quantifier apply to a whole chunk, and they capture that chunk as a separate item in the match result.

"Gogogo now!".match(/(go)+/i); // "Gogogo" — quantifier on the group

let m = "<h1>".match(/<(.*?)>/);
m[0]; // "<h1>" — full match
m[1]; // "h1"     — what group 1 captured

Groups are numbered left to right by their opening paren — nesting doesn't change the rule. An optional group that didn't participate still holds its slot, as undefined:

let r = "ac".match(/a(z)?(c)?/);
r[1]; // undefined — (z)? matched nothing
r[2]; // "c"

Counting parens gets fragile, so two refinements:

let dateRe = /(?<year>[0-9]{4})-(?<month>[0-9]{2})/;
"2026-06".match(dateRe).groups.year; // "2026" — named group

/(?:go)+ (\w+)/i  // (?:...) groups without capturing — no slot in the result

In replace, captured content comes back as $1, $2… or $<name>:

"John Bull".replace(/(\w+) (\w+)/, "$2, $1"); // "Bull, John"

cards.question: What are backreferences \1 and \k<name>?
cards.answer:
A backreference reuses what a group already captured, inside the pattern itself: \1 means "the exact text group 1 matched", \k<name> the same for a named group.

The classic case — quoted strings where the closing quote must match the opening one. Without a backreference, mixed quotes slip through:

let str = `He said: "She's the one!".`;

str.match(/['"](.*?)['"]/);      // `"She'` — wrong: opened with ", closed with '
str.match(/(['"])(.*?)\1/);      // `"She's the one!"` — \1 = same quote as opened
str.match(/(?<quote>['"])(.*?)\k<quote>/); // same, readable

Two gotchas: a non-capturing group (?:...) isn't memorized, so it can't be referenced. And the syntax depends on where you are — \1 in the pattern, $1 in the replacement string.

cards.question: How does alternation | work?
cards.answer:
The pipe | is OR. Unlike square brackets, which choose between single characters, alternation chooses between whole expressions.

"Java or JavaScript?".match(/Java|PHP|C\+\+/g); // ["Java", "Java"]

Its scope is the gotcha: without parentheses, | splits the entire pattern. Parentheses bound it:

/I love HTML|CSS/   // matches "I love HTML" or just "CSS"
/I love (HTML|CSS)/ // matches "I love HTML" or "I love CSS"

Alternatives are tried left to right, and the first that fits wins — so put longer variants first, or factor them:

"JavaScript".match(/Java|JavaScript/); // "Java" — never reaches the longer one
"JavaScript".match(/JavaScript|Java/); // "JavaScript"
/Java(Script)?/                        // same idea, factored

A practical combo — validating hours 00–23 needs alternation, because no single range covers it:

/^([01]\d|2[0-3]):[0-5]\d$/.test("23:59"); // true
/^([01]\d|2[0-3]):[0-5]\d$/.test("25:99"); // false

cards.question: What quantifiers exist?
cards.answer:
A quantifier says how many times the thing before it may repeat. The explicit form is {...}; the three shorthands +, *, ? cover the common cases.

quantifiermeanssame as
{5}exactly 5—
{3,5}3 to 5—
{3,}3 or more—
+one or more{1,}
*zero or more{0,}
?zero or one (optional){0,1}
 
"I'm 12345 years old".match(/\d{5}/);    // "12345"
"+7(903)-123-45-67".match(/\d+/g);       // ["7", "903", "123", "45", "67"]
"color colour".match(/colou?r/g);        // ["color", "colour"]
"100 10 1".match(/\d0*/g);               // ["100", "10", "1"]

Quantifiers apply to one character, a class, or a parenthesized group. The general tradeoff: the more precise the regexp, the longer and more complex it gets — /\d+/ happily matches the 12 in #12; full precision needs anchors or boundaries around it.

cards.question: How do greedy and lazy quantifiers differ?
cards.answer:
By default quantifiers are greedy: they grab as many characters as possible, then backtrack — give characters back one by one — until the rest of the pattern fits. A ? after the quantifier makes it lazy: take as few as possible, expanding only when the rest can't match.

let str = 'a "witch" and her "broom" is one';

str.match(/".+"/g);  // ['"witch" and her "broom"'] — greedy .+ ran to the
                     // last quote and backtracked just enough
str.match(/".+?"/g); // ['"witch"', '"broom"'] — lazy stops at the first closing quote

Often the better tool is neither — an excluding set states the intent directly and doesn't backtrack at all:

str.match(/"[^"]+"/g); // ['"witch"', '"broom"'] — "anything but a quote" inside

Lazy is not a universal fix. Searching <a href="..." class="doc"> with /<a href=".*?" class="doc">/ can glue together pieces of two different tags, because .*? will expand across a quote if that's what makes the rest match. href="[^"]*" can't leave the attribute — it's the reliable shape.

cards.question: How do match and matchAll work?
cards.answer:
str.match(re) has two modes: without g it returns the first match as an array — full match, then capturing groups, plus index and input. With g it returns all matches as plain strings — and drops the groups.

let m = "12-34".match(/(\d+)-(\d+)/);
m[0], m[1], m[2]; // "12-34", "12", "34"  + m.index

"12-34, 56-78".match(/(\d+)-(\d+)/g); // ["12-34", "56-78"] — no groups

The trap: no matches returns null, not an empty array — .length on it throws. Guard with || []:

let results = str.match(re) || [];

str.matchAll(re) fixes both problems: it requires g, returns an iterable of full match-arrays — every one with its groups and index — and yields an empty iterable instead of null:

for (let m of "12-34, 56-78".matchAll(/(\d+)-(\d+)/g)) {
  m[1], m[2]; // "12","34" then "56","78"
}
// or: [...str.matchAll(re)]

Rule of thumb: all matches with groups → matchAll; just the strings → match with g.

cards.question: How does replace work with regexps?
cards.answer:
str.replace(re, replacement) replaces matches with a string or with whatever a function returns. With a regexp it replaces the first match — add g for all.

"12-34-56".replace(/-/, ":");  // "12:34-56"
"12-34-56".replace(/-/g, ":"); // "12:34:56"

The replacement string understands special tokens:

tokeninserts
$&the whole match
$1, $2…contents of group N
$<name>contents of a named group
$` / $'text before / after the match
$$a literal dollar
 
"John Smith".replace(/(\w+) (\w+)/, "$2, $1"); // "Smith, John"

For logic, pass a function — it gets the match, the groups, and the position, and its return value is inserted:

"html and css".replace(/html|css/g, s => s.toUpperCase()); // "HTML and CSS"

replaceAll exists mainly for plain strings (replaces all occurrences without a regexp); with a regexp it demands the g flag and behaves like replace.

cards.question: What do test, exec, search, and split do?
cards.answer:
re.test(str) — boolean "is there a match". re.exec(str) — the power method: with g, each call returns the next match, advancing re.lastIndex. str.search(re) — position of the first match or -1. str.split(re) — split by a pattern.

/love/.test("I love JavaScript"); // true
"I love JavaScript".search(/love/); // 2 — first match only, no g mode

"12, 34, 56".split(/,\s*/); // ["12", "34", "56"]

let re = /(\d+)-(\d+)/g, m;
while (m = re.exec("12-34, 56-78")) {
  m[1], m[2], m.index; // groups + position, one match per call
}

The shared trap: a regexp with g (or y) is stateful — it carries the search position in lastIndex. Reusing one across strings, or calling test twice, continues from where it stopped:

let r = /java/g;
r.test("java");  // true  — lastIndex is now 4
r.test("java");  // false — searched from position 4!

Fix: don't share global regexps between searches, or reset re.lastIndex = 0 first.

cards.question: What does the sticky y flag do?
cards.answer:
The y flag makes the regexp match exactly at lastIndex — not "starting from it, searching onward" like g does.

let str = 'let varName = "value"';
let re = /\w+/y;

re.lastIndex = 4;
re.exec(str); // "varName" — a word starts exactly at 4

re.lastIndex = 3;
re.exec(str); // null — position 3 is a space; g would have kept searching

That's the tool for parsers and lexers — "read the token at this position": you advance through the text, and a failed match means "no such token here", not somewhere later.

It's also faster: on a long text with no match nearby, g scans to the end while y checks one position and stops.

cards.question: What is catastrophic backtracking and how do you prevent it?
cards.answer:
A simple-looking regexp can take practically forever and hang the engine. It happens when a failing match forces the engine to try an exponential number of ways to split the text between nested quantifiers.

let re = /^(\w+\s?)*$/; // "words separated by optional spaces"

re.test("A good string"); // true, instant
re.test("An input string that takes a long time or even makes this regexp hang!");
// hangs — the ! at the end can't match, so the engine backtracks…

The problem is ambiguity: \w+ inside (...)* means one word can be split between repetitions many ways — input as (input), (inpu)(t), (inp)(ut)… A word of n letters splits 2n-1 ways; across several words the combinations multiply into millions. On success the first try fits and nobody notices; on failure the engine dutifully tries them all.

Fix 1 — rewrite to remove the ambiguity. Make the structure unambiguous so there's only one way to consume each word:

/^(\w+\s)*\w*$/ // word + mandatory space, repeated; then one last word

Fix 2 — forbid backtracking with a lookahead. JavaScript has no possessive quantifiers, but a capture inside a lookahead emulates one — the lookahead grabs the whole word as a test, and the backreference consumes it whole, never giving letters back:

/^((?=(\w+))\2\s?)*$/ // \2 = the full word the lookahead saw — no re-splitting

The interview takeaway: nested quantifiers over overlapping patterns ((\w+\s?)*, (.*)*, (a+)+) are the red flag — this is also how ReDoS attacks work, feeding a crafted non-matching string to such a pattern.

cards.question: What is an array and how do you add or remove elements at the ends?
cards.answer:
An array is an ordered collection, indexed from zero. Create it with the literal []; read and write by index; at(-1) reads from the end.

let fruits = ["Apple", "Orange", "Plum"];
fruits[0];      // "Apple"
fruits.at(-1);  // "Plum" — fruits[-1] would be undefined
fruits[3] = "Lemon"; // arrays grow on assignment

Four methods work at the ends — and their speed differs sharply:

fruits.push("Pear");    // add to the end       — fast
fruits.pop();           // remove from the end  — fast
fruits.unshift("Kiwi"); // add to the start     — slow
fruits.shift();         // remove from the start — slow

push/pop only touch the last slot. shift/unshift must renumber every element — index 1 becomes 0, 2 becomes 1, and so on — which makes them 10–100× slower on big arrays. Queue-like code that shifts in a hot loop pays for it.

cards.question: What is an array under the hood, and what's special about length?
cards.answer:
An array is a regular object whose keys happen to be numbers, plus engine optimizations that assume you use it as an ordered collection. Break that assumption and the optimizations switch off.

let arr = [];
arr[9999] = 5;       // huge gap — engine falls back to slow dictionary mode
arr.age = 25;        // legal (it's an object!) but kills array optimizations

length isn't "the count of elements" — it's the greatest numeric index plus one, kept up to date automatically:

let a = [];
a[123] = "x";
a.length; // 124

And it's writable. Decreasing it truncates the array, and the cut is irreversible:

let b = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
b.length = 2;  // [1, 2]
b.length = 5;  // [1, 2, empty × 3] — the values are gone
b.length = 0;  // the simplest way to clear an array
