cards.question: How should you loop over an array — and how not to?
cards.answer:
Use for..of when you need the values, a classic for when you need the index. Never for..in.

let fruits = ["Apple", "Orange", "Plum"];

for (let fruit of fruits) { ... }          // values, clean
for (let i = 0; i < fruits.length; i++) { ... } // index + value

for..in is built for plain objects: it walks all enumerable keys — including non-numeric ones an array-like or a careless library may have added — and it's 10–100× slower than the array-aware loops.

Also remember arrays are objects, so == compares references — two arrays are never equal unless they're the same array:

[] == [];        // false — different objects
[0] == [0];      // false

let a = [1], b = a;
a == b;          // true — same reference

To compare contents, loop and compare item by item. And don't lean on coercion: [1,2] + 1 gives the string "1,21", because arrays convert to strings by joining with commas.

cards.question: Which array methods mutate, and which return a new array?
cards.answer:
Half the array API changes the array in place, the other half leaves it alone and returns a new one. Knowing which side a method is on prevents the classic "where did my data go" bugs.

mutate in placereturn a new array
push, pop, shift, unshiftslice, concat
splicemap, filter, flat, flatMap
sort, reversetoSorted, toReversed, toSpliced, with
fill, copyWithinArray.from(arr), [...arr]

The sneaky part: sort and reverse mutate and return the same array — the return value looks like a copy but isn't:

let arr = [3, 1, 2];
let sorted = arr.sort();
sorted === arr; // true — same object, original reordered too

When the original must survive (React state, function arguments), copy first or use the immutable counterparts: [...arr].sort() or arr.toSorted().

cards.question: How do splice, slice, and concat differ?
cards.answer:
splice edits the array in place — delete, insert, replace anywhere. slice copies a portion into a new array. concat builds a new array from this one plus more items.

splice(start, deleteCount, ...items) — the swiss army knife. Removes deleteCount elements at start, inserts items in their place, returns what it removed:

let arr = ["I", "study", "JavaScript", "right", "now"];

arr.splice(0, 2);            // removed ["I", "study"]
arr.splice(1, 0, "and", "TS"); // deleteCount 0 — pure insertion
arr.splice(-1, 1, "today");  // negative index — from the end

slice(start, end) — copies from start up to (not including) end, negatives allowed; arr.slice() with no arguments is the idiomatic full copy:

["t", "e", "s", "t"].slice(1, 3); // ["e", "s"]
["t", "e", "s", "t"].slice(-2);   // ["s", "t"]

concat(...items) — appends values and spreads arrays one level:

[1, 2].concat([3, 4], 5); // [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

cards.question: How does sort actually work?
cards.answer:
By default sort() converts elements to strings and compares them lexicographically — which mangles numbers. Pass a comparison function for any real ordering.

[1, 2, 15].sort();            // [1, 15, 2] — "15" < "2" as strings!
[1, 2, 15].sort((a, b) => a - b); // [1, 2, 15]

The comparator's contract: return a negative number if a goes first, positive if b goes first, zero if order doesn't matter. For numbers, a - b ascending and b - a descending fall out of that contract for free.

For human-language strings, delegate to localeCompare — it already returns the right sign and handles accents and non-Latin letters:

["Österreich", "Andorra", "Vietnam"].sort((a, b) => a.localeCompare(b));
// ["Andorra", "Österreich", "Vietnam"]

And remember from the mutation card: sort reorders the array itself and returns that same array — toSorted is the copy-returning variant.

cards.question: How do you search in an array?
cards.answer:
For primitives: includes for yes/no, indexOf for the position. For objects or conditions: find for the first hit, filter for all of them.

[1, 0, false].indexOf(0);     // 1 — uses strict ===, so false ≠ 0
[1, 0, false].includes(false); // true

[NaN].indexOf(NaN);   // -1 — the one place === lets you down
[NaN].includes(NaN);  // true — includes handles NaN correctly

The condition-based family takes a callback and stops at the first match (except filter):

let users = [{id: 1, name: "John"}, {id: 2, name: "Pete"}, {id: 3, name: "Mary"}];

users.find(u => u.id == 2);       // {id: 2, name: "Pete"} — or undefined
users.findIndex(u => u.id == 2);  // 1 — or -1
users.findLastIndex(u => u.name == "Mary"); // searches right to left
users.filter(u => u.id < 3);      // array of all matches (maybe empty)

Rule of thumb: find returns the element, findIndex the position, filter everything that fits.

cards.question: What's the difference between map and forEach?
cards.answer:
map transforms: it calls the function for every element and collects the results into a new array. forEach just runs the function for each element and returns undefined — it's a loop, not a transformation.

let lengths = ["Bilbo", "Gandalf", "Nazgul"].map(item => item.length);
// [5, 7, 6]

["Bilbo", "Gandalf"].forEach((item, index, array) => {
  alert(`${item} is at index ${index}`);
});

Both callbacks receive (item, index, array). The common mistake is chaining off forEach or returning from inside it expecting a result:

let result = arr.forEach(x => x * 2); // undefined — always
let doubled = arr.map(x => x * 2);    // the actual transform

Pick by intent: need a resulting array → map; need a side effect per element (logging, DOM updates) → forEach. Using map purely for side effects allocates an array nobody reads.

cards.question: What do some, every, flat, and flatMap do?
cards.answer:
some asks "does at least one element pass?", every asks "do they all pass?" — both return a boolean and stop as soon as the answer is known. flat unnests arrays; flatMap is map-then-flatten in one step.

let users = [{age: 16}, {age: 25}];

users.some(u => u.age < 18);  // true  — found one, stopped
users.every(u => u.age < 18); // false
[1, [2, [3]]].flat();    // [1, 2, [3]] — one level by default
[1, [2, [3]]].flat(Infinity); // [1, 2, 3] — all the way down

["it was cold", "very cold"].flatMap(s => s.split(" "));
// ["it", "was", "cold", "very", "cold"] — map would give nested arrays

Rounding out the toolkit: arr.join(glue) builds a string from the elements (the mirror of str.split), and Array.isArray(value) is the reliable array check — typeof says "object" for arrays, so it can't tell them apart.

cards.question: How does reduce work?
cards.answer:
reduce boils an array down to a single value. It carries an accumulator through the elements: each call gets the previous result and the current element, and whatever it returns becomes the accumulator for the next call.

let sum = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].reduce((acc, item) => acc + item, 0); // 15
// acc: 0 → 1 → 3 → 6 → 10 → 15

The second argument is the initial accumulator. It's optional — without it, reduce uses the first element as the start — but skipping it is a trap:

[].reduce((acc, x) => acc + x);    // TypeError — empty array, nothing to start from
[].reduce((acc, x) => acc + x, 0); // 0 — always pass the initial value

The single value can be anything — an object, a Map, another array — which makes reduce the general "build something from a list" tool:

let byId = users.reduce((map, user) => {
  map[user.id] = user;
  return map;
}, {});

reduceRight does the same, walking from the end. If a plain loop reads clearer than a clever reduce, use the loop.

cards.question: What are the immutable array methods (toSorted, toReversed, toSpliced, with)?
cards.answer:
ES2023 added copy-returning twins for the four mutating operations: toSorted, toReversed, toSpliced, and with (immutable index assignment). Same arguments, same behavior — but the original array is untouched.

let arr = [3, 1, 2];

arr.toSorted();        // [1, 2, 3]   arr is still [3, 1, 2]
arr.toReversed();      // [2, 1, 3]
arr.toSpliced(1, 1);   // [3, 2]      splice semantics, new array
arr.with(0, 99);       // [99, 1, 2]  arr[0] = 99, as a copy

Before these, the idiom was copy-then-mutate — still everywhere in older code:

[...arr].sort((a, b) => a - b);
arr.slice().reverse();

Why it matters: code that shares arrays — React state, props, function arguments — must not mutate them, because every holder of the reference sees the change. setItems(items.toSorted(...)) expresses that directly, with no manual copy to forget.

cards.question: What is a sparse array and why avoid it?
cards.answer:
A sparse array has "holes" — indexes with no element at all, not even undefined. They appear when you skip indexes, stretch length, or call new Array(n) with a single number.

let a = new Array(3); // length 3, zero elements — three holes
let b = [1, , 3];     // hole in the middle
let c = [];
c[5] = "x";           // holes at 0..4

Holes behave inconsistently: reading one gives undefined, but iteration methods skip them:

a[0];                    // undefined
a.map(x => 1);           // [empty × 3] — callback never ran!
a.forEach(x => count++); // count stays 0

They also push the engine off its fast path — a sparse array is stored like a dictionary, not a contiguous block. When you need "an array of n slots", fill it:

new Array(3).fill(0);                    // [0, 0, 0]
Array.from({length: 3}, (_, i) => i * 2); // [0, 2, 4] — no holes, map works

cards.question: What are array-like objects, and how do you convert them to real arrays?
cards.answer:
An array-like is an object with numeric indexes and a length — but none of the array methods. arguments, DOM collections, and strings are the everyday examples.

let arrayLike = {0: "Hello", 1: "World", length: 2};

arrayLike.map;  // undefined — it's a plain object
// querySelectorAll(...) — same story: indexes + length, no map/filter

Array.from makes a true array out of it — it accepts both array-likes and iterables, with an optional mapping function:

Array.from(arrayLike);               // ["Hello", "World"]
Array.from(arrayLike, s => s + "!"); // ["Hello!", "World!"]
Array.from("𝒳😂");                   // ["𝒳", "😂"] — by characters, pairs intact

Spread [...x] looks equivalent but isn't: it works through iteration, so it handles iterables only. A plain array-like has no iterator:

[...arrayLike];           // TypeError — not iterable
Array.from(arrayLike);    // works — from accepts both kinds

Rule: converting something index-y but methodless → Array.from; copying or merging real arrays → spread.

cards.question: Why use a Map instead of a plain object?
cards.answer:
A Map is a keyed collection where keys can be of any type — objects, numbers, even NaN — without conversion. A plain object turns every key into a string.

let john = {name: "John"};
let visits = {};
visits[john] = 123;
Object.keys(visits); // ["[object Object]"] — every object key collapses to one string

let map = new Map();
map.set(john, 123);
map.get(john); // 123 — the object itself is the key

Keys keep their type — the number 1 and the string "1" are two different keys. Comparison uses SameValueZero: like ===, except NaN equals NaN, so even NaN works as a key.

Other wins over objects: a real size property instead of counting keys, guaranteed insertion-order iteration, and no collisions with inherited keys like toString.

map.set("1", "str").set(1, "num"); // set returns the map — chainable
map.get(1);   // "num"
map.get("1"); // "str"
map.size;     // 3 (john is still there)

cards.question: How do you iterate a Map and convert it to/from an object?
cards.answer:
Three iterators — keys(), values(), entries() — all walking in insertion order. for..of over the Map itself gives [key, value] pairs.

let recipe = new Map([
  ["cucumber", 500],
  ["tomatoes", 350],
]);

for (let [food, amount] of recipe) { ... }  // entries, destructured
for (let food of recipe.keys()) { ... }
recipe.forEach((value, key) => { ... });    // value first, like arrays

The constructor takes any iterable of pairs — which is exactly what Object.entries produces, so object → Map is one line. Object.fromEntries goes the other way:

let map = new Map(Object.entries({name: "John", age: 30}));

let obj = Object.fromEntries(map);          // back to a plain object
let obj2 = Object.fromEntries(map.entries()); // same thing, explicit

That round-trip is the interop story: keep a Map internally, hand a plain object to code that expects one (JSON, third-party libraries).

cards.question: What is a Set and when do you reach for it?
cards.answer:
A Set is a collection of unique values — each value may occur only once. Adding the same value again does nothing.

let set = new Set();
let john = {name: "John"}, mary = {name: "Mary"};

set.add(john).add(mary).add(john).add(john);
set.size; // 2 — duplicates ignored, no check-then-push dance

The API: add, has, delete, clear, size; iteration with for..of or forEach in insertion order. Uniqueness uses the same SameValueZero rule as Map.

The everyday idiom — deduplicating an array:

let values = ["a", "b", "a", "c", "b"];
let unique = [...new Set(values)]; // ["a", "b", "c"]

Reach for a Set whenever the question is "have I seen this before?" — set.has(x) answers in constant time, while arr.includes(x) scans the whole array. Visited nodes, processed ids, allowed values: Set.

cards.question: What problem do WeakMap and WeakSet solve?
cards.answer:
A regular Map holds its keys alive: as long as an object sits in the map, the garbage collector can't reclaim it. A WeakMap doesn't prevent garbage collection — when a key object loses all other references, both the object and its entry vanish automatically.

let john = {name: "John"};
let visitCounts = new WeakMap();
visitCounts.set(john, 123);

john = null; // john leaves — the entry is cleaned up with him
// with a Map, {name:"John"} would stay in memory forever

Two constraints follow from the design:

— Keys must be objects. A primitive like "john" can't be garbage-collected, so weak references to it make no sense.

— No iteration, no size, no keys(). The engine decides when cleanup happens, so the current contents are simply unknowable. You can only get/set/has/delete with a key you already hold. That's by design, not an oversight.

Use cases: attaching data to objects whose lifetime someone else controls — per-user counters, per-request context — and caching computed results per object, where the cache entry should die with the object:

let cache = new WeakMap();
function process(obj) {
  if (!cache.has(obj)) cache.set(obj, heavyCompute(obj));
  return cache.get(obj); // no manual cache invalidation, ever
}

WeakSet is the same idea for membership only — objects in, add/has/delete, no iteration. It stores yes/no facts: "has this user visited?", "has this object been processed?"

cards.question: What are the Set composition methods?
cards.answer:
Sets now do set theory natively: union, intersection, difference, symmetricDifference, plus the checks isSubsetOf, isSupersetOf, isDisjointFrom. Each composition returns a new Set; neither operand changes.

let frontend = new Set(["js", "css", "react"]);
let backend  = new Set(["js", "node", "sql"]);

frontend.union(backend);               // {js, css, react, node, sql}
frontend.intersection(backend);        // {js}
frontend.difference(backend);          // {css, react} — in A, not in B
frontend.symmetricDifference(backend); // {css, react, node, sql} — in one, not both
new Set(["js"]).isSubsetOf(frontend);   // true
frontend.isSupersetOf(new Set(["css"])); // true
frontend.isDisjointFrom(new Set(["go"])); // true — nothing in common

Before these, every one was a hand-rolled filter + has dance:

// the old way
let common = new Set([...frontend].filter(x => backend.has(x)));

The arguments don't have to be Sets — anything "set-like" (with size, has, keys) works, including a Map. Shipped across all modern engines in 2024–2025; for older targets, keep the filter idiom.

cards.question: What is an ArrayBuffer?
cards.answer:
An ArrayBuffer is a reference to a fixed-length contiguous memory area — raw bytes. Despite the name it is not an array: it has no indexes to read, no methods to call, and its size never changes.

let buffer = new ArrayBuffer(16); // 16 bytes, all zeroes
buffer.byteLength; // 16
buffer[0];         // undefined — no direct access

To do anything with the bytes you put a view on top — a TypedArray or DataView that interprets them. The view is like eyeglasses: the buffer is the same memory, the view decides what it means.

let view16 = new Uint16Array(buffer); // same 16 bytes as 8 numbers of 2 bytes
let view8  = new Uint8Array(buffer);  // same 16 bytes as 16 numbers of 1 byte

view16[0] = 65535;
view8[0], view8[1]; // 255, 255 — one write, visible through both views

That's the whole model: ArrayBuffer = the memory, views = typed windows into it. Everything binary in the platform — file contents, network responses, image pixels — bottoms out here.

cards.question: What typed arrays exist and how do you create one?
cards.answer:
A TypedArray is an array-like view over an ArrayBuffer where every element has the same fixed binary type. The family: Uint8/16/32Array (unsigned integers), Int8/16/32Array (signed), Float32/64Array, BigInt64/BigUint64Array, and the special Uint8ClampedArray.

Five ways to construct one:

new Uint8Array(buffer, byteOffset, length); // view over existing memory
new Uint8Array([1, 2, 3]);   // from an array — copies the values
new Uint8Array(otherTyped);  // from another typed array — converts values
new Uint8Array(16);          // allocates its own buffer of 16 elements
new Uint8Array();            // zero length

Only the first form shares memory; the rest create their own buffer, reachable as arr.buffer (with arr.byteLength alongside the element count arr.length).

let arr = new Uint16Array(4);
arr.length;     // 4 elements
arr.byteLength; // 8 bytes
arr.buffer;     // the underlying ArrayBuffer

Indexing, iteration, and most array methods work as expected — typed arrays feel like regular arrays with a fixed element type.

cards.question: What happens on overflow, and which array methods are missing?
cards.answer:
A value that doesn't fit is silently truncated: only the lowest bits are stored — the number modulo 28 for Uint8Array. No error, no warning.

let arr = new Uint8Array(1);
arr[0] = 256; // binary 100000000 — the ninth bit falls off
arr[0];       // 0
arr[0] = 257; // 100000001
arr[0];       // 1

Uint8ClampedArray behaves differently — it clamps instead of wrapping: anything over 255 becomes 255, negatives become 0. That's the right behavior for pixel data, which is why canvas image data uses it.

Methods: map, slice, find, reduce, fill, sort all work. Missing are the ones that would change the length — no splice, no concat, no push/pop: the underlying buffer is fixed memory, you can't "delete" a byte. Two extras replace them:

arr.set(otherArr, offset);   // bulk-copy values into the array
arr.subarray(begin, end);    // a new view on the SAME memory — no copying
arr.slice(begin, end);       // looks similar but COPIES the data

cards.question: How do you convert between strings and bytes — TextDecoder and TextEncoder?
cards.answer:
TextDecoder turns binary data into a string; TextEncoder goes the other way, producing a Uint8Array of UTF-8 bytes. They're the bridge between the typed-array world and text.

let uint8Array = new Uint8Array([72, 101, 108, 108, 111]);
new TextDecoder().decode(uint8Array); // "Hello" — utf-8 by default

new TextEncoder().encode("Hello");    // Uint8Array [72, 101, 108, 108, 111]

The decoder takes the encoding in the constructor — new TextDecoder("windows-1251") for legacy data — and decode accepts any BufferSource, so a slice of a larger buffer works without copying:

let binaryString = new TextDecoder().decode(buffer.subarray(0, len));

Where this shows up: reading a text file from file.arrayBuffer(), decoding network chunks from a stream, parsing a binary format with embedded text fields. The wrong way is building strings from bytes with String.fromCharCode in a loop — it mangles any multi-byte character; the decoder handles UTF-8 sequences correctly, including ones split mid-character across chunks ({stream: true} option).

cards.question: What is DataView and when do you need it instead of a typed array?
cards.answer:
A DataView is an untyped view over a buffer: instead of fixing one element type at construction, you choose the format per read or write — getUint8(offset), getUint16(offset), getFloat64(offset)…

let view = new DataView(buffer);

view.getUint8(0);   // 1 byte at offset 0
view.getUint16(0);  // the same bytes, read as one 16-bit number
view.setUint32(4, 12345);

That's exactly what mixed-format binary data needs — a file header like "2 bytes version, then 4 bytes length, then pixels" can't be expressed as a single typed array, but reads naturally offset by offset with a DataView.

Its second job is endianness. Typed arrays always use the platform's byte order; DataView lets you say which order the data is in — the third argument, littleEndian, defaults to false (big-endian):

view.getUint16(0);       // big-endian — network protocols, many file formats
view.getUint16(0, true); // little-endian

Rule of thumb: uniform data (audio samples, pixel arrays) → typed array; structured or foreign-endian data (file formats, protocol frames) → DataView.

cards.question: What is a Blob and what is a Blob URL?
cards.answer:
A Blob is binary data plus a MIME type — the unit the browser passes around as "a file's worth of content". It's built from parts (strings, buffers, other blobs) and is immutable: you can slice out new blobs, never edit one.

let blob = new Blob(["<html>…</html>"], {type: "text/html"});
blob.size, blob.type;        // bytes, "text/html"
blob.slice(0, 100);          // a new Blob with the first 100 bytes

URL.createObjectURL(blob) registers the blob in a browser-internal map and returns a blob:<origin>/<uuid> URL — usable anywhere a URL goes: <img src>, <a download>:

let link = document.createElement("a");
link.download = "hello.txt";
link.href = URL.createObjectURL(blob);
link.click();
URL.revokeObjectURL(link.href); // free it — the mapping otherwise lives until page unload

The gotcha is that memory note: each object URL pins its blob in memory until you revoke it or the document unloads. The alternative — encoding the blob as a base64 data: URL — needs no revocation but bloats the data by ~33% and costs encoding time.

Modern conversions are promise-based: await blob.text(), await blob.arrayBuffer(), blob.stream() for piece-by-piece processing of huge files.

cards.question: What are File and FileReader?
cards.answer:
A File is a Blob with two extra properties: name and lastModified. You rarely construct one — the browser hands them to you from <input type="file"> or drag-and-drop.

input.onchange = () => {
  let file = input.files[0]; // files is array-like — could be several
  file.name, file.size, file.type;
};

FileReader reads a Blob/File asynchronously, event-style, into one of three shapes:

let reader = new FileReader();
reader.readAsText(file);         // string (UTF-8 by default)
// readAsArrayBuffer(file)       // binary, for processing
// readAsDataURL(file)           // base64 data: URL, for src attributes

reader.onload  = () => console.log(reader.result);
reader.onerror = () => console.log(reader.error);

In modern code you mostly skip it: await file.text() / file.arrayBuffer() replace the first two, and for showing a picked image, URL.createObjectURL(file) is simpler and faster than reading into a data URL. FileReader survives where you need progress events or broad legacy support.

cards.question: What are SharedArrayBuffer and Atomics?
cards.answer:
A SharedArrayBuffer is an ArrayBuffer whose memory can be shared between threads — the main page and Web Workers (or Node worker threads) see the same bytes. Atomics provides race-free operations on that memory.

A regular ArrayBuffer sent to a worker via postMessage is either copied or transferred (detached from the sender). A SharedArrayBuffer is neither — both sides keep a live view:

let sab = new SharedArrayBuffer(1024);
worker.postMessage(sab);          // not copied — shared
let counter = new Int32Array(sab); // a normal typed-array view over it

With true sharing comes the classic problem: counter[0]++ is read-modify-write, and two threads doing it together lose updates. Atomics makes operations indivisible:

Atomics.add(counter, 0, 1);    // safe concurrent increment
Atomics.load(counter, 0);      // safe read
Atomics.store(counter, 0, 42); // safe write

Atomics.wait(counter, 0, 42);  // worker sleeps while counter[0] is 42
Atomics.notify(counter, 0, 1); // wake one waiter — no busy-polling

The deployment gotcha: after Spectre, browsers only expose SharedArrayBuffer to cross-origin-isolated pages — the server must send Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin and Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp. Without those headers it's simply absent. Niche in app code, but it's what powers multithreaded WebAssembly, ffmpeg-in-the-browser, and the like.

cards.question: What do Object.keys, Object.values, and Object.entries give you?
cards.answer:
The three ways to pull a plain object apart: Object.keys(obj) — array of property names, Object.values(obj) — array of values, Object.entries(obj) — array of [key, value] pairs.

let user = {name: "John", age: 30};

Object.keys(user);    // ["name", "age"]
Object.values(user);  // ["John", 30]
Object.entries(user); // [["name", "John"], ["age", 30]]

for (let [key, value] of Object.entries(user)) { ... }

Note the shape: they're called Object.keys(obj), not obj.keys() — static methods, so they work even when the object defines its own keys method. And they return real arrays, not iterators like map.keys() — array methods apply directly.

Like for..in, all three ignore symbol keys; when those matter, there's Object.getOwnPropertySymbols or Reflect.ownKeys.

cards.question: How do you map or filter an object?
cards.answer:
Objects have no map or filter — so you take a round trip through entries: Object.entries → array methods → Object.fromEntries.

let prices = {banana: 1, orange: 2, meat: 4};

let doubled = Object.fromEntries(
  Object.entries(prices).map(([key, value]) => [key, value * 2])
);
// {banana: 2, orange: 4, meat: 8}

The same pattern filters, renames keys, or inverts the object — any array transform works in the middle:

let cheap = Object.fromEntries(
  Object.entries(prices).filter(([, value]) => value < 3)
);
// {banana: 1, orange: 2}

Object.fromEntries accepts any iterable of pairs, so the same line converts a Map to a plain object — the entries round trip is the universal adapter between keyed shapes.

cards.question: How does array destructuring work?
cards.answer:
Destructuring "unpacks" an array (or any iterable) into variables, positionally. The array itself is unchanged — it's just a shorter way to read items out.

let [firstName, surname] = "John Smith".split(" ");

let [, , third] = ["a", "b", "c"];   // commas skip elements
let [a, b, ...rest] = [1, 2, 3, 4];  // rest gathers the tail: [3, 4]
let [name = "Guest"] = [];           // default kicks in for undefined

It works with any iterable — strings, Sets, Maps — and the left side can be anything assignable, not just fresh variables:

let user = {};
[user.name, user.age] = ["John", 30];

for (let [key, value] of map) { ... } // entries destructured in the loop head

The party trick — swapping without a temp variable:

[guest, admin] = [admin, guest];

cards.question: How does object destructuring work?
cards.answer:
Object destructuring pulls properties out by name — order doesn't matter. Colon renames, = sets a default, ...rest collects what's left.

let options = {title: "Menu", width: 100, height: 200};

let {title, width, height} = options;       // by name
let {width: w, height: h} = options;        // rename: property → variable
let {title: t = "Untitled", size = 10} = options; // defaults, combined
let {title: only, ...rest} = options;       // rest: {width: 100, height: 200}

Patterns nest — mirror the structure on the left to reach inside:

let {size: {width: innerW}, items: [item1]} = {
  size: {width: 100, height: 200},
  items: ["Cake", "Donut"],
};

The syntax gotcha: assigning to existing variables without let needs parentheses, because a statement starting with { reads as a code block:

let title, width;
{title, width} = options;   // SyntaxError
({title, width} = options); // works

cards.question: What are smart function parameters?
cards.answer:
Destructure an options object right in the parameter list — callers pass one object with only the fields they care about, and defaults fill the rest. This is the standard cure for functions with many optional arguments.

The problem shape — positional parameters force callers to pass placeholders:

function showMenu(title = "Untitled", width = 200, height = 100, items = []) {}
showMenu("My Menu", undefined, undefined, ["Item1"]); // ugly and fragile

The fix — take one object, destructure it with defaults and renames:

function showMenu({title = "Untitled", width: w = 100, items = []} = {}) {
  // title, w, items ready to use
}

showMenu({title: "My Menu", items: ["Item1"]}); // named, order-free, skippable

Note the trailing = {} — the default for the whole parameter. Without it, calling showMenu() with no arguments tries to destructure undefined and throws.

cards.question: What's the difference between rest and spread?
cards.answer:
Same three dots, opposite directions. Rest appears in a function's parameter list and gathers arguments into an array. Spread appears in calls and literals and expands an iterable into individual items.

function sumAll(...args) {        // rest: gathers
  return args.reduce((s, n) => s + n, 0);
}
sumAll(1, 2, 3); // 6

let arr = [3, 5, 1];
Math.max(...arr);          // spread: expands → Math.max(3, 5, 1)
let merged = [0, ...arr, 6]; // [0, 3, 5, 1, 6]
let chars = [..."Hello"];    // any iterable spreads

Rest must be last in the parameter list — it takes "everything remaining", so nothing can follow it. Regular parameters before it are fine:

function showName(first, last, ...titles) {} // ok
function broken(...args, last) {}            // SyntaxError

Rest replaced the legacy arguments object, which is array-like but has no array methods and can't skip leading parameters. (Arrow functions don't have their own arguments at all — they see the enclosing function's.)

cards.question: How does spread copy arrays and objects?
cards.answer:
[...arr] and {...obj} are the idiomatic copies — shorter than Object.assign, same result: a shallow copy.

let arr = [1, 2, 3];
let copy = [...arr];
copy.push(4);     // arr untouched

let user = {name: "John"};
let clone = {...user, age: 30}; // copy + override in one literal

Merging is the same move with several sources — later spreads win on key conflicts:

let merged = {...defaults, ...userOptions}; // userOptions overrides defaults

Shallow means one level: nested objects are copied by reference, so the copy and the original share them:

let a = {sizes: {w: 100}};
let b = {...a};
b.sizes.w = 9;
a.sizes.w; // 9 — same inner object

One asymmetry to remember: array spread iterates, so it needs an iterable; object spread copies own properties, so it works on anything. Deep copies are the next card's job.

cards.question: How do you clone an object — shallow vs deep?
cards.answer:
Shallow: {...obj} or Object.assign({}, obj) — copies the top level, nested objects stay shared. Deep: structuredClone(obj) — clones the whole tree.

let user = {name: "John", sizes: {height: 182}};

let shallow = Object.assign({}, user);
shallow.sizes === user.sizes; // true — same nested object

let deep = structuredClone(user);
deep.sizes === user.sizes;    // false — fully independent

Object.assign(dest, ...sources) is also the mutating merge — it copies properties into its first argument and returns it; spread builds a new object instead, which is usually what you want.

structuredClone handles most data — objects, arrays, primitives, Dates, Maps, Sets, typed arrays — and even circular references:

let obj = {};
obj.me = obj;
structuredClone(obj).me; // points at the clone, not the original

Its limit: functions. A property holding a function makes it throw — class instances and callback-bearing configs need a hand-written clone or lodash's _.cloneDeep. And the old JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)) idiom is strictly worse: it drops undefined and functions silently, mangles Dates to strings, and dies on circular references.

cards.question: What do Object.freeze, Object.hasOwn, and Object.groupBy do?
cards.answer:
Three utilities worth knowing cold: Object.freeze makes an object read-only, Object.hasOwn is the safe own-property check, Object.groupBy turns a list into a keyed object of groups.

freeze — no adding, removing, or changing properties. Writes fail silently (throw in strict mode), and it's shallow — nested objects stay mutable:

let config = Object.freeze({api: "/v1", retries: 3});
config.retries = 5;          // ignored (TypeError in strict mode)
Object.isFrozen(config);     // true

The softer sibling Object.seal blocks add/remove but allows changing existing values.

hasOwn — "does the object itself have this key", ignoring the prototype chain. It replaces obj.hasOwnProperty(key), which breaks on objects created with Object.create(null):

let dict = Object.create(null);
dict.hasOwnProperty;        // undefined — no prototype, no method
Object.hasOwn(dict, "key"); // works on anything

groupBy — classify an iterable by a key function:

let people = [{name: "Ann", age: 17}, {name: "Bob", age: 30}];
Object.groupBy(people, p => p.age >= 18 ? "adult" : "minor");
// {minor: [{Ann}], adult: [{Bob}]}

cards.question: What makes an object iterable?
cards.answer:
An object is iterable if it has a Symbol.iterator method that returns an iterator — an object with next(), where each call returns {done: Boolean, value: any}. That contract is all for..of needs.

When a for..of starts, it calls obj[Symbol.iterator]() once, then calls next() on the result for each step, stopping when done is true:

for (let item of obj) { ... }
// under the hood:
// let it = obj[Symbol.iterator]();
// while (true) {
//   let res = it.next();
//   if (res.done) break;
//   let item = res.value; ...
// }

Arrays, strings, Maps, Sets, and most DOM collections are iterable out of the box. The same protocol powers everything iteration-shaped: spread [...x], destructuring, Array.from, new Map(pairs), Promise.all — implement it once and the whole language opens up.

A nice detail: string iteration walks real characters, keeping surrogate pairs intact — for (let ch of "😂!") sees the emoji as one character, where indexing would split it.

cards.question: How do iterables differ from array-likes?
cards.answer:
Two different contracts, often confused. Iterable: has Symbol.iterator — works with for..of and spread. Array-like: has indexes and length — looks like an array but may not be iterable at all.

let arrayLike = {0: "Hello", 1: "World", length: 2};

for (let x of arrayLike) {} // TypeError — no Symbol.iterator
arrayLike[0];               // "Hello" — indexing works fine

A thing can be either, both, or neither: strings are both; a custom range object is iterable but not array-like; arguments and old DOM collections are array-like (modern ones are also iterable). Neither contract gives you array methods.

Array.from is the universal converter — it accepts both kinds and returns a real array, which is why it's the safer choice over spread when the input's nature is unclear:

Array.from(arrayLike); // ["Hello", "World"] — spread would have thrown

cards.question: How do you make your own object iterable?
cards.answer:
Give it a Symbol.iterator method that returns an object with next(). The classic example — a range that works in for..of:

let range = {
  from: 1,
  to: 5,

  [Symbol.iterator]() {
    return {
      current: this.from,
      last: this.to,
      next() {
        return this.current <= this.last
          ? {done: false, value: this.current++}
          : {done: true};
      }
    };
  }
};

for (let num of range) alert(num); // 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
[...range];                        // [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] — spread works too

Note the separation: range is the iterable, the object returned from Symbol.iterator is the iterator holding the iteration state. Because each for..of asks for a fresh iterator, two loops over range don't interfere. (Merging both into one object is possible, but then a second simultaneous loop would share state.)

You can also drive an iterator by hand — useful for pausing or splitting iteration:

let it = "Hello"[Symbol.iterator]();
it.next(); // {value: "H", done: false}
it.next(); // {value: "e", done: false} — take values at your own pace

In practice, a generator usually replaces the hand-written iterator — *[Symbol.iterator]() { for (let i = this.from; i <= this.to; i++) yield i; } does the same in three lines (see Generators).

cards.question: What are iterator helpers?
cards.answer:
The array methods, available directly on iterators: .map, .filter, .take, .drop, .flatMap, .reduce, .some, .every, .find, .forEach, .toArray. The difference from array methods: they're lazy — nothing is computed until values are pulled, and no intermediate arrays are built.

function* numbers() { let n = 0; while (true) yield n++; } // infinite!

numbers()
  .map(n => n * n)
  .filter(n => n % 2 === 0)
  .take(3)
  .toArray(); // [0, 4, 16] — only computed what take(3) needed

With arrays, the same chain would materialize a full new array per step — and couldn't run on an infinite source at all. Iterator helpers process one value at a time through the whole chain.

They work on anything that produces an iterator: generator results, map.values(), set.keys(), str.matchAll(...). For a plain iterable, Iterator.from(obj) wraps it into a helper-capable iterator:

let firstBig = map.values().find(v => v.size > 100); // no [...map.values()] copy

Shipped in all modern engines (2024–2025). Rule of thumb: data already in an array → array methods; data coming from an iterator, or huge/infinite → helpers, straight on the source.

cards.question: How do you create a Date and read its components?
cards.answer:
Four constructors: new Date() — now; new Date(ms) — a timestamp, milliseconds since Jan 1 1970 UTC; new Date(string) — parsed; new Date(year, month, date, hours, …) — components.

new Date();                  // current date and time
new Date(0);                 // 01.01.1970 UTC
new Date("2026-06-12");      // parsed from string
new Date(2026, 5, 12);       // 12 June 2026 — month 5 is June!

The two classic traps live in the components: months count from 0 (0 = January, 11 = December), and getDay() returns the weekday with 0 = Sunday through 6 = Saturday.

let d = new Date(2026, 0, 1); // 1 January 2026
d.getMonth();    // 0 — January
d.getFullYear(); // 2026 — never getYear(), it's broken legacy
d.getDate();     // 1 — day of month
d.getDay();      // weekday, 0 = Sunday

All getters return local time zone values; each has a UTC twin — getUTCHours(), getUTCDay()… Two have no UTC variant because they're zone-independent: getTime() (the timestamp) and getTimezoneOffset() (UTC-minus-local, in minutes).

cards.question: How does date arithmetic work?
cards.answer:
Two mechanisms do nearly everything: autocorrection — out-of-range components roll over correctly — and numeric conversion — a Date becomes its timestamp, so dates subtract.

Autocorrection means you just add to a component and let the Date fix itself, month lengths and leap years included:

let date = new Date(2026, 0, 28);
date.setDate(date.getDate() + 5); // 2 February 2026 — Jan 33 corrected
new Date(2025, 1, 29);            // 1 March 2025 — no Feb 29 that year

Subtraction gives milliseconds, because date - date converts both to timestamps:

let start = new Date();
// ... work ...
let elapsed = new Date() - start; // ms

Date.now();  // current timestamp without creating an object — for benchmarks

Date.parse(str) reads the ISO format YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss.sssZ (shorter variants allowed) and returns a timestamp, or NaN if unreadable. Anything fancier — formatting, time zones, calendars — is Intl's or Temporal's job, not Date's.

cards.question: How does JSON.stringify work and what does it skip?
cards.answer:
JSON.stringify(value) turns a value into a JSON string. It handles objects, arrays, strings, numbers, booleans, and null — and silently drops what JSON can't express: functions, symbol keys, and undefined values.

JSON.stringify({
  name: "John",
  sayHi() {},          // skipped
  [Symbol("id")]: 1,   // skipped
  age: undefined       // skipped
});
// '{"name":"John"}' — no error, just gone

The format is stricter than a JS literal: strings and property names use double quotes only, and there are no comments, no new, no trailing commas — that strictness is what makes it parse fast in every language.

{"name": "John", "isAdmin": false} // valid JSON
{name: 'John'}                     // not JSON

The one hard failure: circular references throw —

let room = {};
let meetup = {place: room};
room.occupiedBy = meetup;
JSON.stringify(meetup); // TypeError: Converting circular structure to JSON

Nested objects serialize automatically, any depth — as long as the structure is a tree.

cards.question: How do you customize JSON.stringify and JSON.parse?
cards.answer:
Three hooks on the way out — replacer, space, toJSON — and one on the way in: reviver.

The full signature is JSON.stringify(value, replacer, space). replacer is an array of keys to keep, or a (key, value) function returning the value to use (undefined to skip) — the standard way around circular references. space indents for readability:

JSON.stringify(meetup, ["title", "participants", "name"]);
JSON.stringify(meetup, (key, value) => key == "occupiedBy" ? undefined : value);
JSON.stringify(user, null, 2); // pretty-printed for logs

An object with a toJSON() method serializes as whatever that method returns — Date has one built in, which is why dates become ISO strings:

JSON.stringify({date: new Date()}); // '{"date":"2026-06-12T..."}'

And that's exactly the round-trip problem reviver solves: parsed JSON gives you the date back as a plain string, unless you revive it —

let str = '{"title":"Conference","date":"2026-06-12T10:00:00.000Z"}';

let meetup = JSON.parse(str, (key, value) =>
  key == "date" ? new Date(value) : value
);
meetup.date.getDate(); // works — a real Date again

cards.question: What is Temporal and what does it fix?
cards.answer:
Temporal is the replacement for Date — a modern date/time API with immutable values, real time-zone support, 1-based months, and separate types for separate concepts.

The type per concept is the core idea — Date crams everything into one timestamp; Temporal names what you actually mean:

Temporal.PlainDate.from("2026-06-12");      // a calendar date, no time, no zone
Temporal.PlainTime.from("13:30");            // a wall-clock time
Temporal.PlainDateTime.from("2026-06-12T13:30");
Temporal.ZonedDateTime.from("2026-06-12T13:30[Europe/Berlin]"); // exact moment + zone
Temporal.Instant.from("2026-06-12T11:30:00Z"); // a precise moment, like a timestamp
Temporal.Duration.from({hours: 2, minutes: 30});

Everything is immutable — arithmetic returns new values, no setDate mutation surprises — and the everyday pains of Date disappear: months are 1–12, parsing is strictly defined instead of engine-dependent, and DST-aware math works because the zone is part of the value:

let date = Temporal.PlainDate.from("2026-01-31");
date.add({months: 1});  // 2026-02-28 — clamped, well-defined
date.month;             // 1 = January, finally
date.until("2026-12-31").days; // differences as Durations

Status: the proposal is finalized and shipping — Firefox has it enabled, Chrome and Safari are implementing; for production today you use the @js-temporal/polyfill. Worth knowing now: new code and interview answers increasingly assume it.

cards.question: How does Intl.NumberFormat work?
cards.answer:
Intl.NumberFormat formats numbers the way a given locale expects — separators, currency symbols, percent signs — so you never hand-build "$1,234.50" with string concatenation.

let n = 1234567.891;

new Intl.NumberFormat("en-US").format(n); // "1,234,567.891"
new Intl.NumberFormat("de-DE").format(n); // "1.234.567,891" — swapped separators
new Intl.NumberFormat("ru-RU").format(n); // "1 234 567,891"

The second argument configures the style. The three you'll actually use: currency, percent, and unit:

new Intl.NumberFormat("en-US", {style: "currency", currency: "EUR"}).format(1234.5);
// "€1,234.50"

new Intl.NumberFormat("en-US", {style: "percent"}).format(0.25); // "25%"
new Intl.NumberFormat("en-US", {style: "unit", unit: "megabyte"}).format(512);
// "512 MB"

Digit control via minimumFractionDigits/maximumFractionDigits, compact notation via notation: "compact" ("1.2M"). One performance note that applies to the whole Intl family: constructing a formatter is expensive, format() is cheap — create once, reuse in loops.

cards.question: How does Intl.DateTimeFormat work?
cards.answer:
Intl.DateTimeFormat renders a Date for a locale — names of months and weekdays, the right order of parts, 12- vs 24-hour clock — all from the user's conventions instead of yours.

let date = new Date(2026, 5, 12);

new Intl.DateTimeFormat("en-US").format(date); // "6/12/2026"
new Intl.DateTimeFormat("de-DE").format(date); // "12.6.2026"

Options pick which components appear and how verbose each is — "numeric", "2-digit", "long", "short":

new Intl.DateTimeFormat("en-US", {
  weekday: "long", year: "numeric", month: "long", day: "numeric",
}).format(date); // "Friday, June 12, 2026"

new Intl.DateTimeFormat("en-GB", {
  dateStyle: "medium", timeStyle: "short", // the shortcut presets
}).format(date); // "12 Jun 2026, 00:00"

It also does time zones — the only built-in way to display a moment in another zone: {timeZone: "Asia/Tashkent"}. The everyday shorthand for one-off use is date.toLocaleString(locale, options) — same engine, no explicit formatter. And Intl.RelativeTimeFormat covers the "3 days ago" style:

new Intl.RelativeTimeFormat("en").format(-3, "day"); // "3 days ago"

cards.question: What is Intl.Collator for?
cards.answer:
Intl.Collator compares strings by the rules of a language, not by character codes. Code-order comparison puts "Z" < "a" and mishandles accents; a collator sorts the way a dictionary in that language would.

let words = ["résumé", "zebra", "apple", "Über"];

words.sort();
// ["Über", "apple", "résumé", "zebra"] — wrong: codes, not language

words.sort(new Intl.Collator("en").compare);
// ["apple", "résumé", "Über", "zebra"]

collator.compare returns the negative/zero/positive contract sort expects, so it plugs straight in. Useful options:

new Intl.Collator("en", {sensitivity: "base"}).compare("a", "Á"); // 0 — case and accents ignored
new Intl.Collator("en", {numeric: true}).compare("file10", "file2"); // > 0 — "natural" sort
new Intl.Collator("en", {caseFirst: "upper"}); // uppercase sorts first

You've already met its shorthand: str.localeCompare(other, locale, options) is a one-shot collator. The explicit object wins when sorting big arrays — one collator reused beats a fresh comparison setup per pair.

cards.question: What do Intl.Segmenter and Intl.PluralRules do?
cards.answer:
Intl.Segmenter splits text into real graphemes, words, or sentences — the correct answer to "how do I split a string with emoji?". Intl.PluralRules tells you which plural form a number takes in a language.

Segmenter. split("") and indexing break surrogate pairs, and even [...str] splits composed emoji like 👨‍👩‍👦 into parts. The segmenter knows what a human sees as one character:

let seg = new Intl.Segmenter("en", {granularity: "grapheme"});
[...seg.segment("Hi👨‍👩‍👦!")].map(s => s.segment);
// ["H", "i", "👨‍👩‍👦", "!"] — the family is one grapheme

new Intl.Segmenter("en", {granularity: "word"});     // word boundaries
// also the only way to segment Chinese/Japanese text, which has no spaces

PluralRules. English has two plural forms; Russian has three, Arabic six. select(n) returns the category — "one", "few", "many", "other"… — and you map categories to strings:

let pr = new Intl.PluralRules("en");
pr.select(1); // "one"
pr.select(5); // "other"

let forms = {one: "1 item", other: `${n} items`};
forms[pr.select(n)]; // correct in any language, no `n === 1 ? ... :` hack

Together with ListFormat ("a, b, and c") these are the "stop hand-rolling language rules" APIs — the browser ships the linguistic data so you don't.

cards.question: How does recursion work under the hood — what is the execution stack?
cards.answer:
Every function call gets an execution context — where the engine keeps its local variables and the current position. A nested call pushes the running context onto the stack, runs in a fresh one, and pops back when done. Recursion is just that machinery feeding the same function.

function pow(x, n) {
  return n == 1 ? x : x * pow(x, n - 1);
}
pow(2, 3);
// stack: pow(2,3) paused → pow(2,2) paused → pow(2,1) returns 2
// then unwinds: 2 → 4 → 8

Each level costs memory, so depth is limited — engines reliably allow around 10 000 nested calls. The number of calls including the first is the recursion depth; blow past the limit and you get RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded.

Any recursion can be rewritten as a loop, and the loop is usually lighter — one context instead of a stack of them. Recursion earns its cost on variable-depth structures, where the data is itself recursive:

function sumSalaries(department) {
  if (Array.isArray(department)) {
    return department.reduce((sum, p) => sum + p.salary, 0); // a team — base case
  }
  return Object.values(department)
    .reduce((sum, sub) => sum + sumSalaries(sub), 0); // subdepartments — recurse
}

Nested departments, file trees, linked lists, HTML elements — the recursive call mirrors the shape of the data, and the iterative version needs a hand-managed stack to do the same.

cards.question: How do you write a function that takes any number of arguments?
cards.answer:
Declare a rest parameter: function f(...args) gathers all (remaining) arguments into a real array. To pass a collection into such a function, spread it: f(...arr).

function sumAll(...args) {
  return args.reduce((s, n) => s + n, 0);
}
sumAll(1, 2, 3);   // 6

function showName(first, last, ...titles) {
  // first two named, the rest gathered — rest must be last
}

The two sides combine into the universal forwarding shape — a wrapper that accepts anything and hands it on unchanged:

function logged(f) {
  return function(...args) {
    console.log(args);
    return f.apply(this, args); // everything forwarded, this included
  };
}

Spread also replaces apply for the old "array into arguments" trick:

Math.max(...numbers);          // modern
Math.max.apply(null, numbers); // the pre-spread way

(The general rest/spread syntax — copies, merges, destructuring — lives in Spread & rest under object utilities; this is the function-signature side of it.)

cards.question: How do default parameters behave?
cards.answer:
A default fills in when the argument is missing or undefined — and it's an expression, evaluated fresh on each call that needs it.

function showMessage(from, text = "no text given") {
  alert(from + ": " + text);
}

showMessage("Ann");            // "Ann: no text given"
showMessage("Ann", undefined); // same — undefined triggers the default
showMessage("Ann", null);      // "Ann: null" — null is a value, no default!
showMessage("Ann", "");        // "Ann: " — empty string is a value too

That undefined-only rule is the trap: falsy values like null, 0, "" pass through. (Old code uses text = text || "default", which swallows all falsy values; the modern equivalent of that intent is ??.)

Because defaults are evaluated at call time, they can call functions and use earlier parameters:

function log(message, time = new Date()) {}      // fresh Date per call
function greet(name, greeting = `Hi, ${name}`) {} // left-to-right access

function required(name) { throw new Error(name + " is required"); }
function save(data = required("data")) {}          // mandatory-argument idiom

cards.question: What are arguments and new.target?
cards.answer:
Two special values available inside a function: arguments — an array-like holding everything the caller passed (legacy), and new.target — set to the function itself when called with new, undefined otherwise.

arguments predates rest parameters. It has indexes and length but no array methods, and it always grabs everything — you can't skip named parameters. Rest (...args) wins on every axis; arguments matters mainly for reading old code:

function showName() {
  arguments.length, arguments[0];   // works
  arguments.map(...);               // TypeError — not a real array
}

One real behavioral difference: arrow functions don't have their own arguments — they see the enclosing function's, same as with this. That's what makes arrows transparent for forwarding wrappers.

new.target answers "was I called as a constructor?":

function User(name) {
  if (!new.target) {            // called without new
    return new User(name);      // fix it — or throw, like built-in classes do
  }
  this.name = name;
}

Class constructors use this internally — calling a class without new throws precisely because of a new.target check.

cards.question: What is currying and what is it for?
cards.answer:
Currying transforms a function callable as f(a, b, c) into one callable as f(a)(b)(c). It doesn't call the function — it restructures how arguments arrive, so you can supply them in stages.

The payoff is partial application — fix the first arguments once, get a specialized function back:

function log(date, importance, message) { ... }
log = curry(log);

let logNow = log(new Date());          // date fixed
let debugNow = logNow("DEBUG");        // date + importance fixed

debugNow("message");  // logs with both presets — no duplicated logic
log(new Date(), "INFO", "hi");         // advanced curry still allows normal calls

The standard implementation compares collected arguments against the function's arity (func.length): enough → call; not yet → return a collector for more:

function curry(func) {
  return function curried(...args) {
    if (args.length >= func.length) return func.apply(this, args);
    return (...args2) => curried.apply(this, [...args, ...args2]);
  };
}

Limitation baked into that check: it needs a fixed arity — a function declared with rest parameters has no usable length, so it can't be curried this way. And when you just need one partial without the whole transform, bind does it directly: send.bind(null, "admin@site.com").

cards.question: What are the core functional programming ideas in JavaScript?
cards.answer:
Four habits: pure functions (same input → same output, no side effects), immutability (produce new values, don't mutate), higher-order functions (functions taking or returning functions), and composition (build big behavior from small pieces).

A pure function touches nothing outside itself — no shared state, no I/O, no mutation of arguments. That's what makes it trivially testable and safe to cache, reorder, or parallelize:

let total = 0;
function addToTotal(x) { total += x; }     // impure — hidden state, order matters

function add(a, b) { return a + b; }       // pure — a fact, not an event

Immutability is the same idea applied to data — and you already use it: map/filter/toSorted over splice/sort, spread over assignment. Shared references stop being a source of spooky action at a distance.

Higher-order functions are JS's bread and butter — map takes a function, bind returns one, a decorator does both. Composition chains them into pipelines where data flows through transformations:

let activeNames = users
  .filter(u => u.active)
  .map(u => u.name)
  .toSorted();

JavaScript isn't a pure FP language and doesn't want to be — the practical rule is a functional core with side effects pushed to the edges: compute with pure functions, then do the DOM update / DB write in one obvious place.

cards.question: What is a decorator, and why does call forwarding need apply?
cards.answer:
A decorator is a function that takes another function and returns a wrapper with altered behavior — caching, logging, rate-limiting — without touching the original. One decorator then serves any number of functions.

function cachingDecorator(func) {
  let cache = new Map();
  return function(x) {
    if (cache.has(x)) return cache.get(x);
    let result = func.call(this, x); // ← the important line
    cache.set(x, result);
    return result;
  };
}
slow = cachingDecorator(slow); // callers notice nothing — except the speed

The trap is decorating a method: inside the wrapper, plain func(x) calls the original as a standalone function, so its this is lost and property access explodes. call/apply fix it by setting this explicitly:

func.call(context, arg1, arg2); // arguments listed
func.apply(context, args);      // arguments as one array-like

Same thing, different argument packaging. Together they give call forwarding — the canonical wrapper that passes everything through unchanged:

let wrapper = function() {
  return func.apply(this, arguments); // this + all args, transparently
};

A related trick, method borrowing: [].join.call(arguments) runs an array method on an array-like — join only needs indexes and length, so it doesn't care what this really is.

cards.question: How do debounce and throttle differ, and how do you implement debounce?
cards.answer:
Both tame a flood of calls. Debounce waits for a pause: run once, ms after the calls stop. Throttle enforces a rhythm: run at most once per ms while the calls continue.

Match the tool to the question. "What did the user end up with?" → debounce — search-as-you-type fires one request after typing stops. "Where are things now, periodically?" → throttle — scroll position updates keep flowing, just capped.

function debounce(func, ms) {
  let timeout;
  return function(...args) {
    clearTimeout(timeout);                 // a new call cancels the pending run
    timeout = setTimeout(() => func.apply(this, args), ms);
  };
}

input.oninput = debounce(sendRequest, 1000); // fires once, 1s after typing stops

It's a decorator: each call resets the timer, so only the last call in a burst survives, with its arguments and this forwarded. Throttle's contract differs in both directions — it runs the first call immediately, then ignores calls during the cooldown, remembering the latest ignored one to run when the cooldown ends (so the final state is never lost).

Interview phrasing: debounce = "after they stop", throttle = "no more often than". Burst of 10 calls in one second with ms = 1000: debounce runs 1 time, throttle runs 2 — first and last.

cards.question: How does single-threaded JavaScript handle work — what is the call stack?
cards.answer:
JavaScript runs one thing at a time. The call stack holds the function calls in flight; the event loop feeds it: take the oldest task from the queue, run it to completion, then take the next — sleeping when there's nothing to do.

"To completion" is the key phrase: a task is never interrupted halfway. Whatever lands on the stack — a click handler, a setTimeout callback, the script itself — runs until the stack is empty again:

button.onclick = () => {
  heavyLoop();   // 3 seconds of CPU
};
// for those 3 seconds: no clicks handled, no rendering, page frozen

Rendering never happens while a task runs. DOM changes accumulate and paint only after the task finishes — which is why el.textContent = "Loading..." followed by heavy synchronous work shows nothing until the work is done, and why a long task earns the "Page Unresponsive" dialog.

What counts as a task (a macrotask): the initial script, event handler runs, setTimeout/setInterval callbacks, network responses arriving. They queue up while the stack is busy and run one per loop turn.

cards.question: What's the difference between macrotasks and microtasks?
cards.answer:
Two queues with very different priority. Macrotasks (timer callbacks, events, script) run one per loop turn. Microtasks (promise handlers, queueMicrotask) run all of them, immediately after the current code finishes — before rendering, before the next macrotask.

One loop turn: run a macrotask → drain the entire microtask queue → render if needed → next macrotask. Hence the canonical ordering puzzle:

setTimeout(() => alert("timeout"));            // macrotask — next turn
Promise.resolve().then(() => alert("promise")); // microtask — right after this code
alert("code");

// code → promise → timeout

Promise handlers are always asynchronous this way — even an already-resolved promise queues its .then rather than running it inline; the current code always finishes first.

Because the queue drains fully, a microtask that queues another microtask keeps the loop in microtask-land — an infinite chain starves rendering and events just like a long task would. Microtasks are "finish this logical job before the world changes", not "do this later".

queueMicrotask(() => { ... }); // join that queue directly, no promise needed

cards.question: How do setTimeout and setInterval really behave?
cards.answer:
setTimeout(func, delay, ...args) runs once after at least delay ms; setInterval repeats. Both return an id for clearTimeout/clearInterval. The fine print is where the bugs live.

The delay is a minimum, not a promise. The callback is a macrotask — it waits for the stack to clear, and the browser adds its own constraints: after five nested timers the minimum becomes ~4ms, and background tabs may be throttled to once a second or less.

setInterval doesn't wait for your function. It fires on schedule regardless of how long the callback took, so the real gap between runs shrinks — and can vanish. Nested setTimeout guarantees the gap, and lets you adjust it on the fly:

// gap measured from end of one run to start of the next:
function tick() {
  doWork();
  setTimeout(tick, 100); // schedule the next only when this one is done
}
setTimeout(tick, 100);

Zero delay ≠ immediately. setTimeout(f, 0) means "as soon as the current script and pending microtasks finish" — the standard trick for splitting CPU-heavy work into chunks so events and rendering squeeze in between:

function countChunk() {
  // ...do a slice of the work...
  if (!done) setTimeout(countChunk); // yield, then continue
}

And a memory note: a scheduled function (plus its closure) is referenced until it runs or is cancelled — forgotten intervals are a classic leak.

cards.question: Where does requestAnimationFrame fit in the event loop?
cards.answer:
requestAnimationFrame(callback) schedules the callback to run right before the next repaint — a third timing lane, distinct from macrotasks and microtasks, synced to the display's refresh (~60 times a second).

requestAnimationFrame(function draw(time) {
  // move the element a step…
  requestAnimationFrame(draw);  // re-schedule for the next frame
});

That placement is why it's the tool for JS animation. A setTimeout(draw, 16) loop drifts: timers fire on their own schedule, sometimes twice between paints (wasted work), sometimes missing one (visible stutter). requestAnimationFrame fires exactly once per frame, pauses in background tabs for free, and passes a timestamp for computing the animation position.

In the loop-turn picture: macrotask → drain microtasks → rAF callbacks → render. Two practical consequences: rAF is the last chance to read or change the DOM before the user sees it, and batching all DOM writes into one rAF callback avoids layout thrashing between scattered updates.

let id = requestAnimationFrame(cb);
cancelAnimationFrame(id);        // same cancel pattern as timers

cards.question: Can you predict the output order of mixed sync, promise, and timer code?
cards.answer:
Apply the loop turn mechanically: 1) run the current script to the end, 2) drain all microtasks (promise handlers), including ones queued by other microtasks, 3) take the next macrotask (timer). The full classic:

console.log(1);

setTimeout(() => console.log(2));

Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log(3));
Promise.resolve().then(() => setTimeout(() => console.log(4)));
Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log(5));

setTimeout(() => console.log(6));

console.log(7);
// 1 7 3 5 2 6 4

Walk it: sync code prints 1, 7. Microtask drain prints 3, 5 (the middle one just schedules a timer — onto the macrotask queue, behind the existing ones). Then macrotasks in order: 2, 6, and finally the late-scheduled 4.

Each await is a microtask boundary too — everything after it is effectively a .then handler (see async/await).

One subtle consequence of the drain rule: unhandled rejections are detected when the microtask queue empties. A .catch attached later in the same drain still counts; one attached from a setTimeout arrives a turn too late — unhandledrejection has already fired.

cards.question: What is a promise — executor, state, result?
cards.answer:
A promise is an object representing a value that will arrive later. The function you pass to new Promise — the executor — runs immediately and reports its outcome through the two callbacks it receives: resolve(value) or reject(error).

let promise = new Promise(function(resolve, reject) {
  // starts running right now
  setTimeout(() => resolve("done"), 1000);
  // or: setTimeout(() => reject(new Error("whoops")), 1000);
});

Internally a promise holds state and result: it starts pending with result undefined, then moves to fulfilled (with the value) or rejected (with the error). A settled promise is done forever — any state change is final, only the first resolve/reject counts, the rest are ignored.

You can't read state/result directly — you subscribe:

promise.then(result => ..., error => ...); // both outcomes
promise.catch(error => ...);  // = .then(null, fn) — errors only
promise.finally(() => ...);   // cleanup either way — passes result/error through

Versus callbacks: the order flips — start the action first, decide what happens after, attach as many handlers as you want, even long after it settled (they still run, with the stored result).

cards.question: How does promise chaining work?
cards.answer:
Every .then returns a new promise, resolved with whatever the handler returns — so handlers form a pipeline where each result feeds the next.

new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(() => resolve(1), 1000))
  .then(result => result * 2)   // 1 → 2
  .then(result => result * 2)   // 2 → 4
  .then(result => alert(result)); // 4

The crucial rule: if a handler returns a promise, the chain waits for it and continues with its result. That's how async steps sequence without nesting:

fetch("/user.json")
  .then(response => response.json())        // returns a promise — chain waits
  .then(user => fetch(`/avatars/${user.name}`)) // another async step
  .then(response => ...);

(Anything with a .then method — a "thenable" — is treated like a promise here, which is how libraries interoperate.)

The classic mistake is attaching instead of chaining. Several .then on one promise don't form a sequence — each independently gets the same original result:

promise.then(r => r * 2);
promise.then(r => r * 2); // not 4 — both got the original r

promise.then(r => r * 2).then(r => r * 2); // this is the chain

House rule from the chapter: an async action should always return its promise — even if nobody chains on it today, someone will tomorrow.

cards.question: How do errors travel through a promise chain?
cards.answer:
Executors and handlers run inside an invisible try..catch: a throw (or any programming error) becomes a rejection, and a rejection skips forward past every .then to the nearest .catch. One .catch at the end covers the whole chain.

fetch("/user.json")
  .then(response => response.json())
  .then(user => { JSON.parse("{bad json"); }) // throw → rejection
  .then(...)                                   // skipped
  .catch(error => alert(error));               // everything lands here

.catch can recover: if it returns normally, the chain continues into the next .then as if nothing happened. If it can't handle this particular error, it rethrows, and the error travels on to the next .catch:

.catch(error => {
  if (error instanceof NetworkError) return cachedData; // handled — chain resumes
  throw error;                                          // not mine — pass it on
})

Two boundaries to know. The invisible try..catch only covers synchronous code in the handler — a throw inside a setTimeout escapes the chain entirely. And a rejection nobody catches isn't silent: when the microtask queue drains with the error still unhandled, the browser fires unhandledrejection (Node: the unhandledRejection event) — the place to log what slipped through.

window.addEventListener("unhandledrejection", event => {
  // event.promise, event.reason
});

cards.question: What do Promise.all, allSettled, race, and any do?
cards.answer:
The four static combinators take an iterable of promises and differ in what they wait for and how they fail:

methodresolves withon rejection
Promise.allarray of all results, input orderfirst rejection kills it
Promise.allSettledarray of {status, value/reason}never rejects
Promise.racefirst settled resultfirst settled error wins too
Promise.anyfirst fulfilled resultall fail → AggregateError
 
let [user, posts] = await Promise.all([fetchUser(), fetchPosts()]);
// parallel, both required — one failure rejects the whole thing

let results = await Promise.allSettled(urls.map(fetch));
// every outcome reported: {status:"fulfilled", value} / {status:"rejected", reason}

Promise.race([fetch(url), timeout(5000)]); // whichever settles first — timeouts
Promise.any(mirrors.map(fetch));           // first success — fallback sources

Details that matter: all keeps input order regardless of finish order, and non-promise values in the iterable pass straight through. any's combined failure carries all reasons in error.errors.

The small two: Promise.resolve(value) / Promise.reject(error) wrap a known value in a settled promise — mostly for keeping APIs promise-returning (cache hits, early exits).

cards.question: What is promisification?
cards.answer:
Promisification converts a callback-based function into one that returns a promise — so legacy APIs join modern chains and await.

The wrapper appends its own callback to the original arguments and settles the promise from it, assuming the Node error-first convention (err, result):

function promisify(f) {
  return function(...args) {
    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
      args.push((err, result) => {     // our callback, appended last
        if (err) reject(err);
        else resolve(result);
      });
      f.call(this, ...args);
    });
  };
}

let loadScriptPromise = promisify(loadScript);
await loadScriptPromise("path/script.js");

Variants exist for callbacks with multiple result arguments (resolve with an array); Node ships this as util.promisify, and modern Node exposes promise APIs directly (fs/promises).

The structural limit: a promise settles once. Promisification fits "call me back with the result" functions only — something that calls its callback repeatedly (an event listener, a watcher) loses every call after the first. Those need events or async iteration, not a promise.

cards.question: How would you implement Promise.all yourself?
cards.answer:
Count completions, store each result at its input index, resolve when the counter hits the total, reject on the first error. The index bookkeeping is the part interviewers watch for — finish order must not scramble result order.

function promiseAll(iterable) {
  return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    let promises = [...iterable];
    let results = new Array(promises.length);
    let remaining = promises.length;
    if (remaining === 0) return resolve(results); // edge case: empty input

    promises.forEach((p, i) => {
      Promise.resolve(p).then(value => {   // wrap — plain values allowed
        results[i] = value;                // input position, not finish order
        if (--remaining === 0) resolve(results);
      }, reject);                          // first rejection wins; later ones ignored
    });
  });
}

The patterns to narrate: Promise.resolve(p) normalizes non-promise values; results[i] pins order; the counter — not results.length — decides completion (sparse slots would lie); rejecting twice is harmless because settling is final.

The siblings are small edits of the same skeleton: allSettled — never reject, store {status, value/reason} from both handlers; race — no counter at all, wire every promise straight to resolve, reject; any — mirror of all: resolve on first success, count failures and reject with AggregateError when all fail.

cards.question: What does the async keyword do?
cards.answer:
async before a function guarantees it returns a promise — a returned plain value is wrapped in a resolved promise automatically — and it switches on the right to use await inside.

async function f() {
  return 1;
}

f().then(alert); // 1 — same as returning Promise.resolve(1)

The two faces are the same function: callers see an ordinary promise (chain it, pass it to Promise.all), the body reads synchronously. It works on every function form:

async function f() {}            // declaration
let g = async () => {};          // arrow
class Thumbnail {
  async load() {}                // class method — also returns a promise
}

A thrown error inside becomes a rejected promise — async functions never throw synchronously to the caller; success and failure both travel through the returned promise. That symmetry (return → resolve, throw → reject) is the whole contract.

cards.question: How does await work?
cards.answer:
await promise pauses the function until the promise settles, then returns its result. The pause costs nothing — the engine is free to run other code, handle events, and render while this function sleeps.

async function showAvatar() {
  let response = await fetch("/user.json");
  let user = await response.json();         // reads like sync code
  let img = await loadImage(user.avatar);
  img.show();
}

It's exactly promise.then in nicer clothes: everything after the await runs as a microtask once the promise settles. Like then, it accepts any thenable, not just real promises.

Where it's allowed: inside async functions, and at the top level of modules. In a regular function it's a syntax error — the function must be marked:

function f() {
  let x = await fetch(...); // SyntaxError
}

// in a module, top-level await is fine:
let response = await fetch("/config.json");

The subtle consequence: the first await is where the function actually yields control. Code before it runs synchronously with the caller — a detail that matters in ordering puzzles.

cards.question: How do you handle errors with async/await?
cards.answer:
A rejected promise makes await throw the error at that line — so ordinary try..catch handles async failures exactly like sync ones.

async function loadUser() {
  try {
    let response = await fetch("/no-such-user.json");
    let user = await response.json();
  } catch (err) {
    // network error from fetch OR parse error from json() — one place
    alert(err);
  }
}

Without a try..catch, the error rejects the async function's own promise — handle it where the function is called, with .catch at the outermost level:

loadUser().catch(alert); // the boundary catch — or unhandledrejection fires

The classic leak: forgetting that an async function you "fire and forget" still produces a promise. No await, no .catch — its failure becomes an unhandled rejection.

One trap deserves its own line — a returned promise isn't awaited for error purposes:

try {
  return fetchData();        // rejection happens AFTER we left the try block
} catch (e) { ... }          // never catches it

try {
  return await fetchData();  // await keeps the rejection inside the try
} catch (e) { ... }          // works

cards.question: How do you run async work in parallel — and avoid accidental serialization?
cards.answer:
await in a row means one after another. Independent operations should start first, await after — or go through Promise.all.

// sequential — 2 seconds total, for no reason:
let user = await fetchUser();    // 1s
let posts = await fetchPosts();  // 1s, starts only after user arrived

// parallel — 1 second:
let [user2, posts2] = await Promise.all([fetchUser(), fetchPosts()]);

The same mistake hides in loops. await inside for..of processes items one by one — sometimes that's wanted (rate-limited API, order matters); when it isn't, map to promises first:

for (let url of urls) {
  results.push(await fetch(url));  // serial — n × latency
}

let results2 = await Promise.all(urls.map(url => fetch(url))); // concurrent

(Note forEach(async ...) is a third, broken option — it neither waits nor collects; the promises just escape.)

Full parallelism isn't always safe — a thousand simultaneous fetches will hurt something. The middle ground is a worker pool: n loops sharing one iterator, each pulling the next item as it finishes — at most n in flight:

async function pool(items, n, worker) {
  let it = items.values();                      // shared iterator = the queue
  await Promise.all(Array.from({length: n}, async () => {
    for (let item of it) await worker(item);    // each loop pulls the next free item
  }));
}
await pool(urls, 5, url => fetch(url));         // max 5 concurrent

cards.question: What is a generator and how does yield work?
cards.answer:
A generator is a function that can pause and resume, producing values one at a time. Declared with function*; calling it runs no code — it returns a generator object that controls the execution.

function* generateSequence() {
  yield 1;
  yield 2;
  return 3;
}

let gen = generateSequence(); // nothing ran yet

gen.next(); // {value: 1, done: false} — ran until the first yield, paused
gen.next(); // {value: 2, done: false}
gen.next(); // {value: 3, done: true}  — return ends it
gen.next(); // {value: undefined, done: true} — done stays done

Each next() resumes from the paused yield and runs to the next one. The state — locals, position — lives in the generator object between calls.

Generators are iterable, so for..of and spread just work — with one gotcha: iteration stops at done: true, so the return value is ignored. Yield everything you want seen:

for (let v of generateSequence()) alert(v); // 1, 2 — not 3!
[...generateSequence()];                    // [1, 2]

That iterability makes generators the short way to write Symbol.iterator — compare with the hand-written range iterator:

let range = {
  from: 1, to: 5,
  *[Symbol.iterator]() {
    for (let i = this.from; i <= this.to; i++) yield i;
  }
};

yield* delegates to another generator inline — composition without collecting intermediate arrays.

cards.question: How do next(value), throw, and return communicate with a generator?
cards.answer:
yield is a two-way street: it passes a value out, and generator.next(arg) passes arg back in — it becomes the result of the paused yield expression.

function* gen() {
  let result = yield "2 + 2 = ?"; // out: the question; in: the answer
  alert(result);                  // 4
}

let g = gen();
let question = g.next().value; // "2 + 2 = ?" — paused at yield
