g.next(4);                     // resumes, yield evaluates to 4

Note the offset: the first next() just starts the generator (its argument would have no yield to land in); each later next(value) answers the previous yield.

The other two channels inject control instead of data:

g.throw(new Error("no answer")); // the paused yield THROWS this error —
                                  // catchable by try..catch inside the generator

g.return("done"); // finishes it: {value: "done", done: true}, body abandoned

So the outer code can resume, feed, abort, or error a paused computation. This protocol is the machinery async/await is built on — an async function is essentially a generator whose runner feeds promise results into next() and rejections into throw().

cards.question: What are async iterators and async generators?
cards.answer:
Async iteration is for data that arrives over time — each step is a promise. The protocol mirrors the sync one with three changes: the method is Symbol.asyncIterator, next() returns a promise of {value, done}, and you consume with for await..of.

for await (let chunk of stream) { ... } // awaits each next() under the hood

Writing one by hand is rare, because async generators do it in normal code — async function* may await between yields:

async function* fetchCommits(repo) {
  let url = `https://api.github.com/repos/${repo}/commits`;
  while (url) {
    let response = await fetch(url);
    let body = await response.json();
    url = nextPageFrom(response.headers.get("Link")); // follow pagination
    for (let commit of body) yield commit;            // hand out one at a time
  }
}

for await (let commit of fetchCommits("javascript-tutorial/en.javascript.info")) {
  // pagination invisible — just a stream of commits
}

That's the signature use case: paginated APIs, file/network streams, anything chunk-by-chunk — the generator hides the fetching, consumers see a flat sequence and can stop early (no wasted requests).

Limits to remember: for await..of needs an async context (async function or module top level), and spread doesn't work — [...gen] looks for Symbol.iterator, which an async iterable doesn't have. Use Array.fromAsync(gen) to collect one into an array.

cards.question: How does try..catch..finally work, and what can it not catch?
cards.answer:
try runs the code; if an error is thrown, the rest of try is skipped and catch (err) runs with the error object. finally runs on any exit — success, catch, even a return in mid-flight.

try {
  lalala;                  // error — stops here
  alert("never reached");
} catch (err) {
  err.name;    // "ReferenceError"
  err.message; // "lalala is not defined"
  err.stack;   // call trace — for logs
} finally {
  // cleanup: always runs — even if try did `return`
}

(If the error details aren't needed, modern syntax allows bare catch { ... }; cleanup without handling is try..finally with no catch.)

Two things are out of its reach:

— Parse-time errors. Broken syntax stops the engine before anything runs; only valid code can throw runtime errors.

— Asynchronous errors. The block is exited by the time a scheduled callback throws — the try..catch must live inside the callback:

try {
  setTimeout(() => { noSuchVariable; }, 1000); // escapes!
} catch (err) { /* never runs */ }

setTimeout(() => {
  try { noSuchVariable; } catch (err) { ... }  // right place
}, 1000);

The last resort for anything uncaught is the global handler — window.onerror in browsers — for logging, not recovery.

cards.question: How do throw and rethrowing work?
cards.answer:
throw raises any value as an error — by convention an Error object, so it carries name, message, and a stack. The discipline that keeps error handling sane: catch only what you know; rethrow the rest.

let json = '{"age": 30}';
try {
  let user = JSON.parse(json);
  if (!user.name) {
    throw new SyntaxError("Incomplete data: no name"); // our own throw
  }
  blabla(); // unexpected — a real bug
} catch (err) {
  if (err instanceof SyntaxError) {
    alert("JSON Error: " + err.message);  // expected — handle it
  } else {
    throw err;                            // not ours — pass it up
  }
}

Why rethrow matters: a catch that swallows everything also swallows programming mistakes — the blabla() bug above would vanish into the "JSON error" branch and become undebuggable. A catch block is a filter for anticipated failures, not a lid on the pot.

The rethrown error behaves like any throw — it travels to the next enclosing try..catch up the call stack (or to the global handler if nothing catches it).

cards.question: How do you create a custom error class?
cards.answer:
Extend Error, call super(message), and set this.name — that's the whole recipe. Custom classes give errors an identity that instanceof can test.

class ValidationError extends Error {
  constructor(message) {
    super(message);              // sets this.message
    this.name = "ValidationError"; // would otherwise stay "Error"
  }
}

function readUser(json) {
  let user = JSON.parse(json);
  if (!user.name) throw new ValidationError("No field: name");
  return user;
}

Handling code branches with instanceof — preferred over comparing err.name strings because it keeps working for subclasses:

try {
  readUser('{"age": 25}');
} catch (err) {
  if (err instanceof ValidationError) ...      // ours
  else if (err instanceof SyntaxError) ...     // bad JSON
  else throw err;                              // unknown — rethrow
}

Errors form hierarchies like any classes — PropertyRequiredError extends ValidationError with the property name baked in. A base class can even kill the name boilerplate for the whole family:

class MyError extends Error {
  constructor(message) {
    super(message);
    this.name = this.constructor.name; // each subclass names itself
  }
}

cards.question: What is the wrapping-exceptions technique?
cards.answer:
When a function can fail in many low-level ways, don't make every caller check each type — catch the low-level errors inside and throw one higher-level error that represents "this operation failed", keeping the original inside it.

The problem shape: readUser can hit a SyntaxError (bad JSON) or a ValidationError (wrong data) — and tomorrow a third kind. Callers shouldn't grow a new instanceof branch each time:

class ReadError extends Error {
  constructor(message, cause) {
    super(message);
    this.name = "ReadError";
    this.cause = cause;          // the original, for those who need details
  }
}

function readUser(json) {
  try {
    let user = JSON.parse(json);
    validateUser(user);
  } catch (err) {
    if (err instanceof SyntaxError)
      throw new ReadError("Syntax Error", err);
    if (err instanceof ValidationError)
      throw new ReadError("Validation Error", err);
    throw err;                   // unknown stays unwrapped — still rethrow!
  }
}

Now callers test one type and drill into err.cause only when they care:

try {
  readUser(json);
} catch (e) {
  if (e instanceof ReadError) console.log("Original:", e.cause);
  else throw e;
}

It's the error-side of abstraction layers: each layer speaks its own error vocabulary and translates the layer below.

cards.question: What built-in error types exist, and what's the operational vs programmer distinction?
cards.answer:
The built-ins tell you what kind of mistake happened: ReferenceError — an unknown identifier; TypeError — a value of the wrong type used (calling a non-function, reading a property of undefined); SyntaxError — unparseable code or JSON; RangeError — a value out of range (invalid array length, too-deep recursion).

undefinedVar;          // ReferenceError
null.foo;              // TypeError
JSON.parse("{bad");    // SyntaxError
new Array(-1);         // RangeError

All extend Error, so err instanceof Error catches the family; AggregateError (from Promise.any) bundles several.

The deeper split is by meaning, not type:

— Operational errors — expected failures of a correct program: network down, file missing, invalid user input. These you handle: catch, retry, show a message, fall back.

— Programmer errors — bugs: undefined where an object was promised, broken invariants. There's nothing sensible to do at the catch site — handling them hides them. Let them crash loudly (or reach the global logger) so they get fixed.

This is the rule behind "catch what you know, rethrow the rest": catch blocks exist for operational errors; programmer errors should pass through. Custom error classes mark your operational vocabulary — built-in types thrown unexpectedly usually mean a bug.

cards.question: What is the standard error cause option?
cards.answer:
Since ES2022, Error constructors take an options object with cause — the standard slot for "the error that led to this one". It's the wrapping-exceptions pattern, built into the language.

try {
  await fetch("/api/user");
} catch (err) {
  throw new Error("Failed to load user profile", {cause: err});
}

// upstream:
catch (e) {
  e.message; // "Failed to load user profile" — the layer's own story
  e.cause;   // the original TypeError: Failed to fetch
}

Why a standard slot beats an ad-hoc property: every layer agrees where to look, causes chain (e.cause.cause walks down through the layers), and tooling cooperates — Node and browser consoles print the cause chain with the stack, and structuredClone preserves it.

function findCause(err, Type) {        // walking the chain
  for (let e = err; e; e = e.cause)
    if (e instanceof Type) return e;
  return null;
}

It works on every built-in error type and your custom classes automatically — just remember to pass the options bag through super(message, options) when you extend. Rule of thumb: any catch that throws a new error should attach the old one as cause — rewrapping without it deletes the evidence.

cards.question: What is a Proxy and what can it intercept?
cards.answer:
A Proxy wraps an object and intercepts operations on it through traps — handler methods for property reads, writes, deletion, function calls, new, enumeration. A missing trap forwards the operation to the target untouched.

let proxy = new Proxy(target, {
  get(target, prop)        {...}, // reading proxy[prop]
  set(target, prop, value) {...}, // writing — must return true on success
  has(target, prop)        {...}, // the `in` operator
  deleteProperty(...)      {...}, // delete
  apply(...) / construct(...)     // calls / new — for function targets
  ownKeys(...)                    // for..in, Object.keys
});

The classic uses — defaults instead of undefined, and validation at the gate:

let dict = new Proxy({hello: "hola"}, {
  get: (t, phrase) => phrase in t ? t[phrase] : phrase // unknown → echo back
});

let numbers = new Proxy([], {
  set(t, prop, val) {
    if (typeof val != "number") return false; // → TypeError in strict mode
    t[prop] = val;
    return true;                              // set MUST report success
  }
});

The proxy replaces the target everywhere — you hand it out, not the original. Limits worth knowing: built-ins with internal slots (Map, Set, Date) and private #fields break through a naive proxy, because their methods reach for slots the proxy doesn't have (workaround: bind methods to the target); and === can't be trapped — a proxy never equals its target. Proxy.revocable makes a switch-off-able reference for access control.

cards.question: What is Reflect and why use it inside proxy traps?
cards.answer:
Reflect is a collection of methods that mirror the internal operations one-to-one — Reflect.get, Reflect.set, Reflect.has, Reflect.deleteProperty, Reflect.ownKeys… For every proxy trap there's a same-named Reflect method that performs the default behavior.

That symmetry makes traps trivial to write correctly — do your extra work, then delegate:

let user = new Proxy({name: "John"}, {
  get(target, prop, receiver) {
    console.log(`GET ${prop}`);
    return Reflect.get(target, prop, receiver); // default read
  }
});

Why not just target[prop]? The third argument, receiver. When the proxied object has a getter and something inherits from the proxy, target[prop] runs the getter with this = target — the inheriting object's own data is ignored. Reflect.get passes receiver as this, keeping inheritance correct:

let userProxy = new Proxy(user, {
  get(target, prop, receiver) {
    return Reflect.get(target, prop, receiver); // getter sees the real caller
  }
});
let admin = {__proto__: userProxy, _name: "Admin"};
admin.name; // "Admin" — with target[prop] it would say "Guest"

Outside proxies, Reflect offers function-call forms of operators — Reflect.has(obj, "key") for in, Reflect.deleteProperty for delete, Reflect.construct for new — handy when an operation needs to be passed around as a function. Rule: inside a trap, always finish with the matching Reflect call.

cards.question: What are the well-known symbols and which hooks do they expose?
cards.answer:
Well-known symbols are predefined keys (Symbol.iterator, Symbol.toPrimitive, Symbol.hasInstance…) the language itself looks up on your objects — each one a hook into a built-in behavior. Being symbols, they can never collide with normal property names.

The ones that earn their keep:

// Symbol.iterator — what for..of and spread call (see Iterables)
*[Symbol.iterator]() { ... }

// Symbol.toPrimitive — object-to-primitive conversion, one method for all hints
let money = {
  amount: 100,
  [Symbol.toPrimitive](hint) {        // "number" | "string" | "default"
    return hint == "string" ? "$100" : 100;
  }
};
+money;       // 100
`${money}`;   // "$100"

// Symbol.hasInstance — customizes instanceof
class Stack {
  static [Symbol.hasInstance](value) { return Array.isArray(value); }
}
[] instanceof Stack; // true

// Symbol.toStringTag — what Object.prototype.toString reports
class Db { get [Symbol.toStringTag]() { return "Db"; } }
{}.toString.call(new Db()); // "[object Db]"

Others fill narrower niches: Symbol.asyncIterator (for await..of), Symbol.species (which constructor map/slice use for derived arrays), Symbol.dispose (the new using declarations). The mental model: the spec's extension points, published as symbol keys — implement the symbol, and the language treats your object as a native citizen of that protocol.

cards.question: What does eval do, and why is it avoided?
cards.answer:
eval(code) executes a string as JavaScript and returns the value of its last statement. Its defining quirk: the code runs in the current lexical environment — it sees and can change your local variables.

let x = 5;
eval("x = 10; alert(x)"); // 10 — outer variable modified
eval("1 + 1");            // 2 — result of the last statement

That scope access is exactly why it's shunned:

— Minifiers can't touch it. Local variables get renamed to a, b during minification — but a string mentioning x can't be safely renamed, so tools skip optimizing around eval.

— Hidden dependencies. Code-in-a-string that reaches into surrounding scope is invisible to refactoring and to readers.

— Injection risk. Evaluating anything derived from user input is executing the user's code with your privileges. (CSP headers commonly forbid eval outright.)

If executing dynamic code is genuinely required, the contained alternative is new Function — it compiles the string in the global scope, so dependencies must be passed in explicitly as parameters:

let sum = new Function("a", "b", "return a + b");
sum(1, 2); // 3 — sees nothing local, by design

In modern code both are near-extinct: dynamic behavior comes from functions as values, JSON.parse for data, and dynamic import() for code.

cards.question: How does garbage collection work — what is reachability?
cards.answer:
Memory management is automatic and based on one criterion: reachability. A value stays in memory while it can be reached from a root; anything unreachable is collected. Roots are: the currently running function's locals and parameters, the locals of every function in the call chain, and global variables.

The key subtlety — referenced isn't reachable. Objects pointing at each other still die if nothing connects the cluster to a root:

function marry(man, woman) {
  man.wife = woman;
  woman.husband = man;
  return {father: man, mother: woman};
}
let family = marry({name: "John"}, {name: "Ann"});

family = null;
// John and Ann still reference each other — but the whole
// "island" is cut off from the roots, so all of it is collected

The algorithm is mark-and-sweep: start at the roots, mark them, follow every reference and mark what's found, repeat until nothing new — then delete everything unmarked. Picture paint poured on the roots, flowing along references; dry objects are garbage.

Engines keep it fast with three optimizations: generational (new objects, which usually die young, are scanned often; survivors graduate to a rarely-scanned old generation), incremental (the work is split into small pauses), and idle-time collection (runs when the CPU is free). None of it is controllable from code — you can't trigger or block GC; you can only manage reachability.

cards.question: What causes memory leaks in JavaScript, and how do you find one?
cards.answer:
A leak in a GC'd language means unwanted reachability — objects you're done with that something still points at. Four usual suspects:

Forgotten timers and listeners. A live setInterval references its callback, the callback's closure references everything it uses — forever, until cleared. Same for event listeners on long-lived targets (window, a global emitter):

setInterval(() => render(bigData), 1000);  // bigData immortal until clearInterval
window.addEventListener("resize", handler); // remove on teardown, or use
                                            // {signal: controller.signal} + abort()

Detached DOM nodes. An element removed from the page but still referenced from JS (a cached el, a Map of nodes) keeps itself — and its whole subtree — alive.

Ever-growing caches and collections. A module-level Map used as a cache only grows; keys nobody will ask for again are still reachable. Bound the size, evict, or key by object in a WeakMap so entries die with their keys.

Closures outliving their job. A long-lived callback that closed over a large structure pins it, even if it only needed one field — extract the field, let the structure go.

Finding one: DevTools → Memory. Take a heap snapshot, exercise the suspected flow, snapshot again, and diff — look for object counts that only ever grow, and check their retainer chains to see exactly what's holding them. The fix is always the same move: break the reference (null it, clear it, remove it) so reachability ends when usefulness does.

cards.question: What are WeakRef and FinalizationRegistry?
cards.answer:
A WeakRef holds an object without keeping it alive — the GC may collect the target anyway. deref() returns the object if it's still in memory, or undefined if it's gone.

let ref = new WeakRef(hugeImage);
// later:
let img = ref.deref();
if (img) {
  // still alive — use it
} else {
  // collected — recreate it
}

The use case is caching big objects by string key — a regular Map as values-cache pins every image in memory; a Map of WeakRefs lets memory pressure evict them while cache hits still work when they're alive. Also: referencing DOM nodes without preventing their cleanup after removal.

FinalizationRegistry is the companion — register a callback to run after an object is collected (e.g. to delete its stale WeakRef entry from the cache):

let registry = new FinalizationRegistry(key => cache.delete(key));
registry.register(obj, "user-42"); // the held value passed to the callback

The caveats are the headline: GC timing is unpredictable — collection may happen late or, for a given object, never; finalization callbacks aren't guaranteed to run at all (program exit, unreachable registry); and between "unreferenced" and "collected" the object can even come back via deref(). Hence the official advice: avoid if possible — correctness must never depend on these. For key-object association, WeakMap/WeakSet remain the right tool; WeakRef is for the narrow "nice to keep, fine to lose" cache.
